speeches.speech: As I remember, Adam, it was upon this fashion bequeathed me by will but poor a thousand crowns, and, as thou say'st, charged my brother, on his blessing, to breed me well; and there begins my sadness. My brother Jaques he keeps at school, and report speaks goldenly of his profit. For my part, he keeps me rustically at home, or, to speak more properly, stays me here at home unkept; for call you that keeping for a gentleman of my birth that differs not from the stalling of an ox? His horses are bred better; for, besides that they are fair with their feeding, they are taught their manage, and to that end riders dearly hir'd; but I, his brother, gain nothing under him but growth; for the which his animals on his dunghills are as much bound to him as I. Besides this nothing that he so plentifully gives me, the something that nature gave me his countenance seems to take from me. He lets me feed with his hinds, bars me the place of a brother, and as much as in him lies, mines my gentility with my education. This is it, Adam, that grieves me; and the spirit of my father, which I think is within me, begins to mutiny against this servitude. I will no longer endure it, though yet I know no wise remedy how to avoid it.
lines: As I remember, Adam, it was upon this fashion bequeathed

something that nature gave me his countenance seems to take from

speeches.speech: Yonder comes my master, your brother.
lines: Yonder comes my master, your brother.

speeches.speech: Go apart, Adam, and thou shalt hear how he will shake me up.
lines: Go apart, Adam, and thou shalt hear how he will shake me
up.

speeches.speech: Now, sir! what make you here?
lines: Now, sir! what make you here?

speeches.speech: Nothing; I am not taught to make any thing.
lines: Nothing; I am not taught to make any thing.

speeches.speech: What mar you then, sir?
lines: What mar you then, sir?

speeches.speech: Marry, sir, I am helping you to mar that which God made, a poor unworthy brother of yours, with idleness.
lines: Marry, sir, I am helping you to mar that which God made, a
poor unworthy brother of yours, with idleness.

speeches.speech: Marry, sir, be better employed, and be nought awhile.
lines: Marry, sir, be better employed, and be nought awhile.

speeches.speech: Shall I keep your hogs, and eat husks with them? What prodigal portion have I spent that I should come to such penury?
lines: Shall I keep your hogs, and eat husks with them? What
prodigal portion have I spent that I should come to such penury?

speeches.speech: Know you where you are, sir?
lines: Know you where you are, sir?

speeches.speech: O, sir, very well; here in your orchard.
lines: O, sir, very well; here in your orchard.

speeches.speech: Know you before whom, sir?
lines: Know you before whom, sir?

speeches.speech: Ay, better than him I am before knows me. I know you are my eldest brother; and in the gentle condition of blood, you should so know me. The courtesy of nations allows you my better in that you are the first-born; but the same tradition takes not away my blood, were there twenty brothers betwixt us. I have as much of my father in me as you, albeit I confess your coming before me is nearer to his reverence.
lines: Ay, better than him I am before knows me. I know you are
my eldest brother; and in the gentle condition of blood, you
should so know me. The courtesy of nations allows you my better
in that you are the first-born; but the same tradition takes not
away my blood, were there twenty brothers betwixt us. I have as
much of my father in me as you, albeit I confess your coming
before me is nearer to his reverence.

speeches.speech: What, boy!                               [Strikes him]
lines: What, boy!                               [Strikes him]

speeches.speech: Come, come, elder brother, you are too young in this.
lines: Come, come, elder brother, you are too young in this.

speeches.speech: Wilt thou lay hands on me, villain?
lines: Wilt thou lay hands on me, villain?

speeches.speech: I am no villain; I am the youngest son of Sir Rowland de Boys. He was my father; and he is thrice a villain that says such a father begot villains. Wert thou not my brother, I would not take this hand from thy throat till this other had pull'd out thy tongue for saying so. Thou has rail'd on thyself.
lines: I am no villain; I am the youngest son of Sir Rowland de
Boys. He was my father; and he is thrice a villain that says such
a father begot villains. Wert thou not my brother, I would not
take this hand from thy throat till this other had pull'd out thy
tongue for saying so. Thou has rail'd on thyself.

speeches.speech: [Coming forward] Sweet masters, be patient; for your father's remembrance, be at accord.
lines: [Coming forward] Sweet masters, be patient; for your father's
remembrance, be at accord.

speeches.speech: Let me go, I say.
lines: Let me go, I say.

speeches.speech: I will not, till I please; you shall hear me. My father charg'd you in his will to give me good education: you have train'd me like a peasant, obscuring and hiding from me all gentleman-like qualities. The spirit of my father grows strong in me, and I will no longer endure it; therefore allow me such exercises as may become a gentleman, or give me the poor allottery my father left me by testament; with that I will go buy my fortunes.
lines: I will not, till I please; you shall hear me. My father
charg'd you in his will to give me good education: you have
train'd me like a peasant, obscuring and hiding from me all
gentleman-like qualities. The spirit of my father grows strong in
me, and I will no longer endure it; therefore allow me such
exercises as may become a gentleman, or give me the poor
allottery my father left me by testament; with that I will go buy
my fortunes.

speeches.speech: And what wilt thou do? Beg, when that is spent? Well, sir, get you in. I will not long be troubled with you; you shall have some part of your will. I pray you leave me.
lines: And what wilt thou do? Beg, when that is spent? Well, sir,
get you in. I will not long be troubled with you; you shall have
some part of your will. I pray you leave me.

speeches.speech: I no further offend you than becomes me for my good.
lines: I no further offend you than becomes me for my good.

speeches.speech: Get you with him, you old dog.
lines: Get you with him, you old dog.

speeches.speech: Is 'old dog' my reward? Most true, I have lost my teeth in your service. God be with my old master! He would not have spoke such a word. Exeunt ORLANDO and ADAM
lines: Is 'old dog' my reward? Most true, I have lost my teeth in
your service. God be with my old master! He would not have spoke
such a word.
Exeunt ORLANDO and ADAM

speeches.speech: Is it even so? Begin you to grow upon me? I will physic your rankness, and yet give no thousand crowns neither. Holla, Dennis!
lines: Is it even so? Begin you to grow upon me? I will physic
your rankness, and yet give no thousand crowns neither. Holla,
Dennis!

speeches.speech: Calls your worship?
lines: Calls your worship?

speeches.speech: Was not Charles, the Duke's wrestler, here to speak with me?
lines: Was not Charles, the Duke's wrestler, here to speak with me?

speeches.speech: So please you, he is here at the door and importunes access to you.
lines: So please you, he is here at the door and importunes access
to you.

speeches.speech: Call him in. [Exit DENNIS] 'Twill be a good way; and to-morrow the wrestling is.
lines: Call him in. [Exit DENNIS] 'Twill be a good way; and
to-morrow the wrestling is.

speeches.speech: Good morrow to your worship.
lines: Good morrow to your worship.

speeches.speech: Good Monsieur Charles! What's the new news at the new court?
lines: Good Monsieur Charles! What's the new news at the new
court?

speeches.speech: There's no news at the court, sir, but the old news; that is, the old Duke is banished by his younger brother the new Duke; and three or four loving lords have put themselves into voluntary exile with him, whose lands and revenues enrich the new Duke; therefore he gives them good leave to wander.
lines: There's no news at the court, sir, but the old news; that
is, the old Duke is banished by his younger brother the new Duke;
and three or four loving lords have put themselves into voluntary
exile with him, whose lands and revenues enrich the new Duke;
therefore he gives them good leave to wander.

speeches.speech: Can you tell if Rosalind, the Duke's daughter, be banished with her father?
lines: Can you tell if Rosalind, the Duke's daughter, be banished
with her father?

speeches.speech: O, no; for the Duke's daughter, her cousin, so loves her, being ever from their cradles bred together, that she would have followed her exile, or have died to stay behind her. She is at the court, and no less beloved of her uncle than his own daughter; and never two ladies loved as they do.
lines: O, no; for the Duke's daughter, her cousin, so loves her,
being ever from their cradles bred together, that she would have
followed her exile, or have died to stay behind her. She is at
the court, and no less beloved of her uncle than his own
daughter; and never two ladies loved as they do.

speeches.speech: Where will the old Duke live?
lines: Where will the old Duke live?

speeches.speech: They say he is already in the Forest of Arden, and a many merry men with him; and there they live like the old Robin Hood of England. They say many young gentlemen flock to him every day, and fleet the time carelessly, as they did in the golden world.
lines: They say he is already in the Forest of Arden, and a many
merry men with him; and there they live like the old Robin Hood
of England. They say many young gentlemen flock to him every day,
and fleet the time carelessly, as they did in the golden world.

speeches.speech: What, you wrestle to-morrow before the new Duke?
lines: What, you wrestle to-morrow before the new Duke?

speeches.speech: Marry, do I, sir; and I came to acquaint you with a matter. I am given, sir, secretly to understand that your younger brother, Orlando, hath a disposition to come in disguis'd against me to try a fall. To-morrow, sir, I wrestle for my credit; and he that escapes me without some broken limb shall acquit him well. Your brother is but young and tender; and, for your love, I would be loath to foil him, as I must, for my own honour, if he come in; therefore, out of my love to you, I came hither to acquaint you withal, that either you might stay him from his intendment, or brook such disgrace well as he shall run into, in that it is thing of his own search and altogether against my will.
lines: Marry, do I, sir; and I came to acquaint you with a
matter. I am given, sir, secretly to understand that your younger
brother, Orlando, hath a disposition to come in disguis'd against
me to try a fall. To-morrow, sir, I wrestle for my credit; and he
that escapes me without some broken limb shall acquit him well.
Your brother is but young and tender; and, for your love, I would
be loath to foil him, as I must, for my own honour, if he come
in; therefore, out of my love to you, I came hither to acquaint
you withal, that either you might stay him from his intendment,
or brook such disgrace well as he shall run into, in that it is
thing of his own search and altogether against my will.

speeches.speech: Charles, I thank thee for thy love to me, which thou shalt find I will most kindly requite. I had myself notice of my brother's purpose herein, and have by underhand means laboured to dissuade him from it; but he is resolute. I'll tell thee, Charles, it is the stubbornest young fellow of France; full of ambition, an envious emulator of every man's good parts, a secret and villainous contriver against me his natural brother. Therefore use thy discretion: I had as lief thou didst break his neck as his finger. And thou wert best look to't; for if thou dost him any slight disgrace, or if he do not mightily grace himself on thee, he will practise against thee by poison, entrap thee by some treacherous device, and never leave thee till he hath ta'en thy life by some indirect means or other; for, I assure thee, and almost with tears I speak it, there is not one so young and so villainous this day living. I speak but brotherly of him; but should I anatomize him to thee as he is, I must blush and weep, and thou must look pale and wonder.
lines: Charles, I thank thee for thy love to me, which thou shalt
find I will most kindly requite. I had myself notice of my
brother's purpose herein, and have by underhand means laboured to
dissuade him from it; but he is resolute. I'll tell thee,
Charles, it is the stubbornest young fellow of France; full of
ambition, an envious emulator of every man's good parts, a secret
and villainous contriver against me his natural brother.
Therefore use thy discretion: I had as lief thou didst break his
neck as his finger. And thou wert best look to't; for if thou
dost him any slight disgrace, or if he do not mightily grace
himself on thee, he will practise against thee by poison, entrap
thee by some treacherous device, and never leave thee till he
hath ta'en thy life by some indirect means or other; for, I
assure thee, and almost with tears I speak it, there is not one
so young and so villainous this day living. I speak but brotherly
of him; but should I anatomize him to thee as he is, I must blush
and weep, and thou must look pale and wonder.

speeches.speech: I am heartily glad I came hither to you. If he come to-morrow I'll give him his payment. If ever he go alone again, I'll never wrestle for prize more. And so, God keep your worship!  Exit
lines: I am heartily glad I came hither to you. If he come
to-morrow I'll give him his payment. If ever he go alone again,
I'll never wrestle for prize more. And so, God keep your worship!  Exit

speeches.speech: Farewell, good Charles. Now will I stir this gamester. I hope I shall see an end of him; for my soul, yet I know not why, hates nothing more than he. Yet he's gentle; never school'd and yet learned; full of noble device; of all sorts enchantingly beloved; and, indeed, so much in the heart of the world, and especially of my own people, who best know him, that I am altogether misprised. But it shall not be so long; this wrestler shall clear all. Nothing remains but that I kindle the boy thither, which now I'll go about.                  Exit
lines: Farewell, good Charles. Now will I stir this gamester. I
hope I shall see an end of him; for my soul, yet I know not why,
hates nothing more than he. Yet he's gentle; never school'd and
yet learned; full of noble device; of all sorts enchantingly
beloved; and, indeed, so much in the heart of the world, and
especially of my own people, who best know him, that I am
altogether misprised. But it shall not be so long; this wrestler
shall clear all. Nothing remains but that I kindle the boy
thither, which now I'll go about.                  Exit

speeches.speech: I pray thee, Rosalind, sweet my coz, be merry.
lines: I pray thee, Rosalind, sweet my coz, be merry.

speeches.speech: Dear Celia, I show more mirth than I am mistress of; and would you yet I were merrier? Unless you could teach me to forget a banished father, you must not learn me how to remember any extraordinary pleasure.
lines: Dear Celia, I show more mirth than I am mistress of; and
would you yet I were merrier? Unless you could teach me to forget
a banished father, you must not learn me how to remember any
extraordinary pleasure.

speeches.speech: Herein I see thou lov'st me not with the full weight that I love thee. If my uncle, thy banished father, had banished thy uncle, the Duke my father, so thou hadst been still with me, I could have taught my love to take thy father for mine; so wouldst thou, if the truth of thy love to me were so righteously temper'd as mine is to thee.
lines: Herein I see thou lov'st me not with the full weight that I
love thee. If my uncle, thy banished father, had banished thy
uncle, the Duke my father, so thou hadst been still with me, I
could have taught my love to take thy father for mine; so wouldst
thou, if the truth of thy love to me were so righteously temper'd
as mine is to thee.

speeches.speech: Well, I will forget the condition of my estate, to rejoice in yours.
lines: Well, I will forget the condition of my estate, to
rejoice in yours.

speeches.speech: You know my father hath no child but I, nor none is like to have; and, truly, when he dies thou shalt be his heir; for what he hath taken away from thy father perforce, I will render thee again in affection. By mine honour, I will; and when I break that oath, let me turn monster; therefore, my sweet Rose, my dear Rose, be merry.
lines: You know my father hath no child but I, nor none is like to
have; and, truly, when he dies thou shalt be his heir; for what
he hath taken away from thy father perforce, I will render thee
again in affection. By mine honour, I will; and when I break that
oath, let me turn monster; therefore, my sweet Rose, my dear
Rose, be merry.

speeches.speech: From henceforth I will, coz, and devise sports. Let me see; what think you of falling in love?
lines: From henceforth I will, coz, and devise sports.
Let me see; what think you of falling in love?

speeches.speech: Marry, I prithee, do, to make sport withal; but love no man in good earnest, nor no further in sport neither than with safety of a pure blush thou mayst in honour come off again.
lines: Marry, I prithee, do, to make sport withal; but love no man
in good earnest, nor no further in sport neither than with safety
of a pure blush thou mayst in honour come off again.

speeches.speech: What shall be our sport, then?
lines: What shall be our sport, then?

speeches.speech: Let us sit and mock the good housewife Fortune from her wheel, that her gifts may henceforth be bestowed equally.
lines: Let us sit and mock the good housewife Fortune from her
wheel, that her gifts may henceforth be bestowed equally.

speeches.speech: I would we could do so; for her benefits are mightily misplaced; and the bountiful blind woman doth most mistake in her gifts to women.
lines: I would we could do so; for her benefits are mightily
misplaced; and the bountiful blind woman doth most mistake in her
gifts to women.

speeches.speech: 'Tis true; for those that she makes fair she scarce makes honest; and those that she makes honest she makes very ill-favouredly.
lines: 'Tis true; for those that she makes fair she scarce makes
honest; and those that she makes honest she makes very
ill-favouredly.

speeches.speech: Nay; now thou goest from Fortune's office to Nature's: Fortune reigns in gifts of the world, not in the lineaments of Nature.
lines: Nay; now thou goest from Fortune's office to Nature's:
Fortune reigns in gifts of the world, not in the lineaments of
Nature.

speeches.speech: No; when Nature hath made a fair creature, may she not by Fortune fall into the fire? Though Nature hath given us wit to flout at Fortune, hath not Fortune sent in this fool to cut off the argument?
lines: No; when Nature hath made a fair creature, may she not by
Fortune fall into the fire? Though Nature hath given us wit to
flout at Fortune, hath not Fortune sent in this fool to cut off
the argument?

speeches.speech: Indeed, there is Fortune too hard for Nature, when Fortune makes Nature's natural the cutter-off of Nature's wit.
lines: Indeed, there is Fortune too hard for Nature, when
Fortune makes Nature's natural the cutter-off of Nature's wit.

speeches.speech: Peradventure this is not Fortune's work neither, but Nature's, who perceiveth our natural wits too dull to reason of such goddesses, and hath sent this natural for our whetstone; for always the dullness of the fool is the whetstone of the wits. How now, wit! Whither wander you?
lines: Peradventure this is not Fortune's work neither, but
Nature's, who perceiveth our natural wits too dull to reason of
such goddesses, and hath sent this natural for our whetstone; for
always the dullness of the fool is the whetstone of the wits. How
now, wit! Whither wander you?

speeches.speech: Mistress, you must come away to your father.
lines: Mistress, you must come away to your father.

speeches.speech: Were you made the messenger?
lines: Were you made the messenger?

speeches.speech: No, by mine honour; but I was bid to come for you.
lines: No, by mine honour; but I was bid to come for you.

speeches.speech: Where learned you that oath, fool?
lines: Where learned you that oath, fool?

speeches.speech: Of a certain knight that swore by his honour they were good pancakes, and swore by his honour the mustard was naught. Now I'll stand to it, the pancakes were naught and the mustard was good, and yet was not the knight forsworn.
lines: Of a certain knight that swore by his honour they were
good pancakes, and swore by his honour the mustard was naught.
Now I'll stand to it, the pancakes were naught and the mustard
was good, and yet was not the knight forsworn.

speeches.speech: How prove you that, in the great heap of your knowledge?
lines: How prove you that, in the great heap of your knowledge?

speeches.speech: Ay, marry, now unmuzzle your wisdom.
lines: Ay, marry, now unmuzzle your wisdom.

speeches.speech: Stand you both forth now: stroke your chins, and swear by your beards that I am a knave.
lines: Stand you both forth now: stroke your chins, and swear
by your beards that I am a knave.

speeches.speech: By our beards, if we had them, thou art.
lines: By our beards, if we had them, thou art.

speeches.speech: By my knavery, if I had it, then I were. But if you swear by that that is not, you are not forsworn; no more was this knight, swearing by his honour, for he never had any; or if he had, he had sworn it away before ever he saw those pancackes or that mustard.
lines: By my knavery, if I had it, then I were. But if you
swear by that that is not, you are not forsworn; no more was this
knight, swearing by his honour, for he never had any; or if he
had, he had sworn it away before ever he saw those pancackes or
that mustard.

speeches.speech: Prithee, who is't that thou mean'st?
lines: Prithee, who is't that thou mean'st?

speeches.speech: One that old Frederick, your father, loves.
lines: One that old Frederick, your father, loves.

speeches.speech: My father's love is enough to honour him. Enough, speak no more of him; you'll be whipt for taxation one of these days.
lines: My father's love is enough to honour him. Enough, speak no
more of him; you'll be whipt for taxation one of these days.

speeches.speech: The more pity that fools may not speak wisely what wise men do foolishly.
lines: The more pity that fools may not speak wisely what wise
men do foolishly.

speeches.speech: By my troth, thou sayest true; for since the little wit that fools have was silenced, the little foolery that wise men have makes a great show. Here comes Monsieur Le Beau.
lines: By my troth, thou sayest true; for since the little wit that
fools have was silenced, the little foolery that wise men have
makes a great show. Here comes Monsieur Le Beau.

speeches.speech: With his mouth full of news.
lines: With his mouth full of news.

speeches.speech: Which he will put on us as pigeons feed their young.
lines: Which he will put on us as pigeons feed their young.

speeches.speech: Then shall we be news-cramm'd.
lines: Then shall we be news-cramm'd.

speeches.speech: All the better; we shall be the more marketable. Bon jour, Monsieur Le Beau. What's the news?
lines: All the better; we shall be the more marketable. Bon jour,
Monsieur Le Beau. What's the news?

speeches.speech: Fair Princess, you have lost much good sport.
lines: Fair Princess, you have lost much good sport.

speeches.speech: Sport! of what colour?
lines: Sport! of what colour?

speeches.speech: What colour, madam? How shall I answer you?
lines: What colour, madam? How shall I answer you?

speeches.speech: As wit and fortune will.
lines: As wit and fortune will.

speeches.speech: Or as the Destinies decrees.
lines: Or as the Destinies decrees.

speeches.speech: Well said; that was laid on with a trowel.
lines: Well said; that was laid on with a trowel.

speeches.speech: Nay, if I keep not my rank-
lines: Nay, if I keep not my rank-

speeches.speech: Thou losest thy old smell.
lines: Thou losest thy old smell.

speeches.speech: You amaze me, ladies. I would have told you of good wrestling, which you have lost the sight of.
lines: You amaze me, ladies. I would have told you of good
wrestling, which you have lost the sight of.

speeches.speech: Yet tell us the manner of the wrestling.
lines: Yet tell us the manner of the wrestling.

speeches.speech: I will tell you the beginning, and, if it please your ladyships, you may see the end; for the best is yet to do; and here, where you are, they are coming to perform it.
lines: I will tell you the beginning, and, if it please your
ladyships, you may see the end; for the best is yet to do; and
here, where you are, they are coming to perform it.

speeches.speech: Well, the beginning, that is dead and buried.
lines: Well, the beginning, that is dead and buried.

speeches.speech: There comes an old man and his three sons-
lines: There comes an old man and his three sons-

speeches.speech: I could match this beginning with an old tale.
lines: I could match this beginning with an old tale.

speeches.speech: Three proper young men, of excellent growth and presence.
lines: Three proper young men, of excellent growth and presence.

speeches.speech: With bills on their necks: 'Be it known unto all men by these presents'-
lines: With bills on their necks: 'Be it known unto all men by
these presents'-

speeches.speech: The eldest of the three wrestled with Charles, the Duke's wrestler; which Charles in a moment threw him, and broke three of his ribs, that there is little hope of life in him. So he serv'd the second, and so the third. Yonder they lie; the poor old man, their father, making such pitiful dole over them that all the beholders take his part with weeping.
lines: The eldest of the three wrestled with Charles, the Duke's
wrestler; which Charles in a moment threw him, and broke three of
his ribs, that there is little hope of life in him. So he serv'd
the second, and so the third. Yonder they lie; the poor old man,
their father, making such pitiful dole over them that all the
beholders take his part with weeping.

speeches.speech: Alas!
lines: Alas!

speeches.speech: But what is the sport, monsieur, that the ladies have lost?
lines: But what is the sport, monsieur, that the ladies have
lost?

speeches.speech: Why, this that I speak of.
lines: Why, this that I speak of.

speeches.speech: Thus men may grow wiser every day. It is the first time that ever I heard breaking of ribs was sport for ladies.
lines: Thus men may grow wiser every day. It is the first time
that ever I heard breaking of ribs was sport for ladies.

speeches.speech: Or I, I promise thee.
lines: Or I, I promise thee.

speeches.speech: But is there any else longs to see this broken music in his sides? Is there yet another dotes upon rib-breaking? Shall we see this wrestling, cousin?
lines: But is there any else longs to see this broken music in
his sides? Is there yet another dotes upon rib-breaking? Shall we
see this wrestling, cousin?

speeches.speech: You must, if you stay here; for here is the place appointed for the wrestling, and they are ready to perform it.
lines: You must, if you stay here; for here is the place
appointed for the wrestling, and they are ready to perform it.

speeches.speech: Yonder, sure, they are coming. Let us now stay and see it.
lines: Yonder, sure, they are coming. Let us now stay and see it.

speeches.speech: Come on; since the youth will not be entreated, his own peril on his forwardness.
lines: Come on; since the youth will not be entreated, his own
peril on his forwardness.

speeches.speech: Is yonder the man?
lines: Is yonder the man?

speeches.speech: Even he, madam.
lines: Even he, madam.

speeches.speech: Alas, he is too young; yet he looks successfully.
lines: Alas, he is too young; yet he looks successfully.

speeches.speech: How now, daughter and cousin! Are you crept hither to see the wrestling?
lines: How now, daughter and cousin! Are you crept hither to
see the wrestling?

speeches.speech: Ay, my liege; so please you give us leave.
lines: Ay, my liege; so please you give us leave.

speeches.speech: You will take little delight in it, I can tell you, there is such odds in the man. In pity of the challenger's youth I would fain dissuade him, but he will not be entreated. Speak to him, ladies; see if you can move him.
lines: You will take little delight in it, I can tell you,
there is such odds in the man. In pity of the challenger's youth
I would fain dissuade him, but he will not be entreated. Speak to
him, ladies; see if you can move him.

speeches.speech: Call him hither, good Monsieur Le Beau.
lines: Call him hither, good Monsieur Le Beau.

speeches.speech: Do so; I'll not be by. [DUKE FREDERICK goes apart]
lines: Do so; I'll not be by.
[DUKE FREDERICK goes apart]

speeches.speech: Monsieur the Challenger, the Princess calls for you.
lines: Monsieur the Challenger, the Princess calls for you.

speeches.speech: I attend them with all respect and duty.
lines: I attend them with all respect and duty.

speeches.speech: Young man, have you challeng'd Charles the wrestler?
lines: Young man, have you challeng'd Charles the wrestler?

speeches.speech: No, fair Princess; he is the general challenger. I come but in, as others do, to try with him the strength of my youth.
lines: No, fair Princess; he is the general challenger. I come
but in, as others do, to try with him the strength of my youth.

speeches.speech: Young gentleman, your spirits are too bold for your years. You have seen cruel proof of this man's strength; if you saw yourself with your eyes, or knew yourself with your judgment, the fear of your adventure would counsel you to a more equal enterprise. We pray you, for your own sake, to embrace your own safety and give over this attempt.
lines: Young gentleman, your spirits are too bold for your years.
You have seen cruel proof of this man's strength; if you saw
yourself with your eyes, or knew yourself with your judgment, the
fear of your adventure would counsel you to a more equal
enterprise. We pray you, for your own sake, to embrace your own
safety and give over this attempt.

speeches.speech: Do, young sir; your reputation shall not therefore be misprised: we will make it our suit to the Duke that the wrestling might not go forward.
lines: Do, young sir; your reputation shall not therefore be
misprised: we will make it our suit to the Duke that the
wrestling might not go forward.

speeches.speech: I beseech you, punish me not with your hard thoughts, wherein I confess me much guilty to deny so fair and excellent ladies any thing. But let your fair eyes and gentle wishes go with me to my trial; wherein if I be foil'd there is but one sham'd that was never gracious; if kill'd, but one dead that is willing to be so. I shall do my friends no wrong, for I have none to lament me; the world no injury, for in it I have nothing; only in the world I fill up a place, which may be better supplied when I have made it empty.
lines: I beseech you, punish me not with your hard thoughts,
wherein I confess me much guilty to deny so fair and excellent
ladies any thing. But let your fair eyes and gentle wishes go
with me to my trial; wherein if I be foil'd there is but one
sham'd that was never gracious; if kill'd, but one dead that is
willing to be so. I shall do my friends no wrong, for I have none
to lament me; the world no injury, for in it I have nothing; only
in the world I fill up a place, which may be better supplied when
I have made it empty.

speeches.speech: The little strength that I have, I would it were with you.
lines: The little strength that I have, I would it were with
you.

speeches.speech: And mine to eke out hers.
lines: And mine to eke out hers.

speeches.speech: Fare you well. Pray heaven I be deceiv'd in you!
lines: Fare you well. Pray heaven I be deceiv'd in you!

speeches.speech: Your heart's desires be with you!
lines: Your heart's desires be with you!

speeches.speech: Come, where is this young gallant that is so desirous to lie with his mother earth?
lines: Come, where is this young gallant that is so desirous to
lie with his mother earth?

speeches.speech: Ready, sir; but his will hath in it a more modest working.
lines: Ready, sir; but his will hath in it a more modest working.

speeches.speech: You shall try but one fall.
lines: You shall try but one fall.

speeches.speech: No, I warrant your Grace, you shall not entreat him to a second, that have so mightily persuaded him from a first.
lines: No, I warrant your Grace, you shall not entreat him to a
second, that have so mightily persuaded him from a first.

speeches.speech: You mean to mock me after; you should not have mock'd me before; but come your ways.
lines: You mean to mock me after; you should not have mock'd me
before; but come your ways.

speeches.speech: Now, Hercules be thy speed, young man!
lines: Now, Hercules be thy speed, young man!

speeches.speech: I would I were invisible, to catch the strong fellow by the leg.                                     [They wrestle]
lines: I would I were invisible, to catch the strong fellow by the
leg.                                     [They wrestle]

speeches.speech: O excellent young man!
lines: O excellent young man!

speeches.speech: If I had a thunderbolt in mine eye, I can tell who should down.
lines: If I had a thunderbolt in mine eye, I can tell who should
down.

speeches.speech: No more, no more.
lines: No more, no more.

speeches.speech: Yes, I beseech your Grace; I am not yet well breath'd.
lines: Yes, I beseech your Grace; I am not yet well breath'd.

speeches.speech: How dost thou, Charles?
lines: How dost thou, Charles?

speeches.speech: He cannot speak, my lord.
lines: He cannot speak, my lord.

speeches.speech: Bear him away. What is thy name, young man?
lines: Bear him away. What is thy name, young man?

speeches.speech: Orlando, my liege; the youngest son of Sir Rowland de Boys.
lines: Orlando, my liege; the youngest son of Sir Rowland de
Boys.

speeches.speech: I would thou hadst been son to some man else. The world esteem'd thy father honourable, But I did find him still mine enemy. Thou shouldst have better pleas'd me with this deed, Hadst thou descended from another house. But fare thee well; thou art a gallant youth; I would thou hadst told me of another father.
lines: I would thou hadst been son to some man else.
The world esteem'd thy father honourable,
But I did find him still mine enemy.
Thou shouldst have better pleas'd me with this deed,
Hadst thou descended from another house.
But fare thee well; thou art a gallant youth;
I would thou hadst told me of another father.

speeches.speech: Were I my father, coz, would I do this?
lines: Were I my father, coz, would I do this?

speeches.speech: I am more proud to be Sir Rowland's son, His youngest son- and would not change that calling To be adopted heir to Frederick.
lines: I am more proud to be Sir Rowland's son,
His youngest son- and would not change that calling
To be adopted heir to Frederick.

speeches.speech: My father lov'd Sir Rowland as his soul, And all the world was of my father's mind; Had I before known this young man his son, I should have given him tears unto entreaties Ere he should thus have ventur'd.
lines: My father lov'd Sir Rowland as his soul,
And all the world was of my father's mind;
Had I before known this young man his son,
I should have given him tears unto entreaties
Ere he should thus have ventur'd.

speeches.speech: Gentle cousin, Let us go thank him, and encourage him; My father's rough and envious disposition Sticks me at heart. Sir, you have well deserv'd; If you do keep your promises in love But justly as you have exceeded all promise, Your mistress shall be happy.
lines: Gentle cousin,
Let us go thank him, and encourage him;
My father's rough and envious disposition
Sticks me at heart. Sir, you have well deserv'd;
If you do keep your promises in love
But justly as you have exceeded all promise,
Your mistress shall be happy.

speeches.speech: Gentleman,        [Giving him a chain from her neck] Wear this for me; one out of suits with fortune, That could give more, but that her hand lacks means. Shall we go, coz?
lines: Gentleman,        [Giving him a chain from her neck]
Wear this for me; one out of suits with fortune,
That could give more, but that her hand lacks means.
Shall we go, coz?

speeches.speech: Ay. Fare you well, fair gentleman.
lines: Ay. Fare you well, fair gentleman.

speeches.speech: Can I not say 'I thank you'? My better parts Are all thrown down; and that which here stands up Is but a quintain, a mere lifeless block.
lines: Can I not say 'I thank you'? My better parts
Are all thrown down; and that which here stands up
Is but a quintain, a mere lifeless block.

speeches.speech: He calls us back. My pride fell with my fortunes; I'll ask him what he would. Did you call, sir? Sir, you have wrestled well, and overthrown More than your enemies.
lines: He calls us back. My pride fell with my fortunes;
I'll ask him what he would. Did you call, sir?
Sir, you have wrestled well, and overthrown
More than your enemies.

speeches.speech: Will you go, coz?
lines: Will you go, coz?

speeches.speech: Have with you. Fare you well.
lines: Have with you. Fare you well.

speeches.speech: What passion hangs these weights upon my tongue? I cannot speak to her, yet she urg'd conference. O poor Orlando, thou art overthrown! Or Charles or something weaker masters thee.
lines: What passion hangs these weights upon my tongue?
I cannot speak to her, yet she urg'd conference.
O poor Orlando, thou art overthrown!
Or Charles or something weaker masters thee.

speeches.speech: Good sir, I do in friendship counsel you To leave this place. Albeit you have deserv'd High commendation, true applause, and love, Yet such is now the Duke's condition That he misconstrues all that you have done. The Duke is humorous; what he is, indeed, More suits you to conceive than I to speak of.
lines: Good sir, I do in friendship counsel you
To leave this place. Albeit you have deserv'd
High commendation, true applause, and love,
Yet such is now the Duke's condition
That he misconstrues all that you have done.
The Duke is humorous; what he is, indeed,
More suits you to conceive than I to speak of.

speeches.speech: I thank you, sir; and pray you tell me this: Which of the two was daughter of the Duke That here was at the wrestling?
lines: I thank you, sir; and pray you tell me this:
Which of the two was daughter of the Duke
That here was at the wrestling?

speeches.speech: Neither his daughter, if we judge by manners; But yet, indeed, the smaller is his daughter; The other is daughter to the banish'd Duke, And here detain'd by her usurping uncle, To keep his daughter company; whose loves Are dearer than the natural bond of sisters. But I can tell you that of late this Duke Hath ta'en displeasure 'gainst his gentle niece, Grounded upon no other argument But that the people praise her for her virtues And pity her for her good father's sake; And, on my life, his malice 'gainst the lady Will suddenly break forth. Sir, fare you well. Hereafter, in a better world than this, I shall desire more love and knowledge of you.
lines: Neither his daughter, if we judge by manners;
But yet, indeed, the smaller is his daughter;
The other is daughter to the banish'd Duke,
And here detain'd by her usurping uncle,
To keep his daughter company; whose loves
Are dearer than the natural bond of sisters.
But I can tell you that of late this Duke
Hath ta'en displeasure 'gainst his gentle niece,
Grounded upon no other argument
But that the people praise her for her virtues
And pity her for her good father's sake;
And, on my life, his malice 'gainst the lady
Will suddenly break forth. Sir, fare you well.
Hereafter, in a better world than this,
I shall desire more love and knowledge of you.

speeches.speech: I rest much bounden to you; fare you well. [Exit LE BEAU] Thus must I from the smoke into the smother; From tyrant Duke unto a tyrant brother. But heavenly Rosalind!                                  Exit
lines: I rest much bounden to you; fare you well.
[Exit LE BEAU]
Thus must I from the smoke into the smother;
From tyrant Duke unto a tyrant brother.
But heavenly Rosalind!                                  Exit

speeches.speech: Why, cousin! why, Rosalind! Cupid have mercy! Not a word?
lines: Why, cousin! why, Rosalind! Cupid have mercy!
Not a word?

speeches.speech: Not one to throw at a dog.
lines: Not one to throw at a dog.

speeches.speech: No, thy words are too precious to be cast away upon curs; throw some of them at me; come, lame me with reasons.
lines: No, thy words are too precious to be cast away upon curs;
throw some of them at me; come, lame me with reasons.

speeches.speech: Then there were two cousins laid up, when the one should be lam'd with reasons and the other mad without any.
lines: Then there were two cousins laid up, when the one should
be lam'd with reasons and the other mad without any.

speeches.speech: But is all this for your father?
lines: But is all this for your father?

speeches.speech: No, some of it is for my child's father. O, how full of briers is this working-day world!
lines: No, some of it is for my child's father. O, how full of
briers is this working-day world!

speeches.speech: They are but burs, cousin, thrown upon thee in holiday foolery; if we walk not in the trodden paths, our very petticoats will catch them.
lines: They are but burs, cousin, thrown upon thee in holiday
foolery; if we walk not in the trodden paths, our very petticoats
will catch them.

speeches.speech: I could shake them off my coat: these burs are in my heart.
lines: I could shake them off my coat: these burs are in my
heart.

speeches.speech: Hem them away.
lines: Hem them away.

speeches.speech: I would try, if I could cry 'hem' and have him.
lines: I would try, if I could cry 'hem' and have him.

speeches.speech: Come, come, wrestle with thy affections.
lines: Come, come, wrestle with thy affections.

speeches.speech: O, they take the part of a better wrestler than myself.
lines: O, they take the part of a better wrestler than myself.

speeches.speech: O, a good wish upon you! You will try in time, in despite of a fall. But, turning these jests out of service, let us talk in good earnest. Is it possible, on such a sudden, you should fall into so strong a liking with old Sir Rowland's youngest son?
lines: O, a good wish upon you! You will try in time, in despite of
a fall. But, turning these jests out of service, let us talk in
good earnest. Is it possible, on such a sudden, you should fall
into so strong a liking with old Sir Rowland's youngest son?

speeches.speech: The Duke my father lov'd his father dearly.
lines: The Duke my father lov'd his father dearly.

speeches.speech: Doth it therefore ensue that you should love his son dearly? By this kind of chase I should hate him, for my father hated his father dearly; yet I hate not Orlando.
lines: Doth it therefore ensue that you should love his son dearly?
By this kind of chase I should hate him, for my father hated his
father dearly; yet I hate not Orlando.

speeches.speech: No, faith, hate him not, for my sake.
lines: No, faith, hate him not, for my sake.

speeches.speech: Why should I not? Doth he not deserve well?
lines: Why should I not? Doth he not deserve well?

speeches.speech: Let me love him for that; and do you love him because I do. Look, here comes the Duke.
lines: Let me love him for that; and do you love him because I
do. Look, here comes the Duke.

speeches.speech: With his eyes full of anger.
lines: With his eyes full of anger.

speeches.speech: Mistress, dispatch you with your safest haste, And get you from our court.
lines: Mistress, dispatch you with your safest haste,
And get you from our court.

speeches.speech: Me, uncle?
lines: Me, uncle?

speeches.speech: You, cousin. Within these ten days if that thou beest found So near our public court as twenty miles, Thou diest for it.
lines: You, cousin.
Within these ten days if that thou beest found
So near our public court as twenty miles,
Thou diest for it.

speeches.speech: I do beseech your Grace, Let me the knowledge of my fault bear with me. If with myself I hold intelligence, Or have acquaintance with mine own desires; If that I do not dream, or be not frantic- As I do trust I am not- then, dear uncle, Never so much as in a thought unborn Did I offend your Highness.
lines: I do beseech your Grace,
Let me the knowledge of my fault bear with me.
If with myself I hold intelligence,
Or have acquaintance with mine own desires;
If that I do not dream, or be not frantic-
As I do trust I am not- then, dear uncle,
Never so much as in a thought unborn
Did I offend your Highness.

speeches.speech: Thus do all traitors; If their purgation did consist in words, They are as innocent as grace itself. Let it suffice thee that I trust thee not.
lines: Thus do all traitors;
If their purgation did consist in words,
They are as innocent as grace itself.
Let it suffice thee that I trust thee not.

speeches.speech: Yet your mistrust cannot make me a traitor. Tell me whereon the likelihood depends.
lines: Yet your mistrust cannot make me a traitor.
Tell me whereon the likelihood depends.

speeches.speech: Thou art thy father's daughter; there's enough.
lines: Thou art thy father's daughter; there's enough.

speeches.speech: So was I when your Highness took his dukedom; So was I when your Highness banish'd him. Treason is not inherited, my lord; Or, if we did derive it from our friends, What's that to me? My father was no traitor. Then, good my liege, mistake me not so much To think my poverty is treacherous.
lines: So was I when your Highness took his dukedom;
So was I when your Highness banish'd him.
Treason is not inherited, my lord;
Or, if we did derive it from our friends,
What's that to me? My father was no traitor.
Then, good my liege, mistake me not so much
To think my poverty is treacherous.

speeches.speech: Dear sovereign, hear me speak.
lines: Dear sovereign, hear me speak.

speeches.speech: Ay, Celia; we stay'd her for your sake, Else had she with her father rang'd along.
lines: Ay, Celia; we stay'd her for your sake,
Else had she with her father rang'd along.

speeches.speech: I did not then entreat to have her stay; It was your pleasure, and your own remorse; I was too young that time to value her, But now I know her. If she be a traitor, Why so am I: we still have slept together, Rose at an instant, learn'd, play'd, eat together; And wheresoe'er we went, like Juno's swans, Still we went coupled and inseparable.
lines: I did not then entreat to have her stay;
It was your pleasure, and your own remorse;
I was too young that time to value her,
But now I know her. If she be a traitor,
Why so am I: we still have slept together,
Rose at an instant, learn'd, play'd, eat together;
And wheresoe'er we went, like Juno's swans,
Still we went coupled and inseparable.

speeches.speech: She is too subtle for thee; and her smoothness, Her very silence and her patience, Speak to the people, and they pity her. Thou art a fool. She robs thee of thy name; And thou wilt show more bright and seem more virtuous When she is gone. Then open not thy lips. Firm and irrevocable is my doom Which I have pass'd upon her; she is banish'd.
lines: She is too subtle for thee; and her smoothness,
Her very silence and her patience,
Speak to the people, and they pity her.
Thou art a fool. She robs thee of thy name;
And thou wilt show more bright and seem more virtuous
When she is gone. Then open not thy lips.
Firm and irrevocable is my doom
Which I have pass'd upon her; she is banish'd.

speeches.speech: Pronounce that sentence, then, on me, my liege; I cannot live out of her company.
lines: Pronounce that sentence, then, on me, my liege;
I cannot live out of her company.

speeches.speech: You are a fool. You, niece, provide yourself. If you outstay the time, upon mine honour, And in the greatness of my word, you die.
lines: You are a fool. You, niece, provide yourself.
If you outstay the time, upon mine honour,
And in the greatness of my word, you die.

speeches.speech: O my poor Rosalind! Whither wilt thou go? Wilt thou change fathers? I will give thee mine. I charge thee be not thou more griev'd than I am.
lines: O my poor Rosalind! Whither wilt thou go?
Wilt thou change fathers? I will give thee mine.
I charge thee be not thou more griev'd than I am.

speeches.speech: I have more cause.
lines: I have more cause.

speeches.speech: Thou hast not, cousin. Prithee be cheerful. Know'st thou not the Duke Hath banish'd me, his daughter?
lines: Thou hast not, cousin.
Prithee be cheerful. Know'st thou not the Duke
Hath banish'd me, his daughter?

speeches.speech: That he hath not.
lines: That he hath not.

speeches.speech: No, hath not? Rosalind lacks, then, the love Which teacheth thee that thou and I am one. Shall we be sund'red? Shall we part, sweet girl? No; let my father seek another heir. Therefore devise with me how we may fly, Whither to go, and what to bear with us; And do not seek to take your charge upon you, To bear your griefs yourself, and leave me out; For, by this heaven, now at our sorrows pale, Say what thou canst, I'll go along with thee.
lines: No, hath not? Rosalind lacks, then, the love
Which teacheth thee that thou and I am one.
Shall we be sund'red? Shall we part, sweet girl?
No; let my father seek another heir.
Therefore devise with me how we may fly,
Whither to go, and what to bear with us;
And do not seek to take your charge upon you,
To bear your griefs yourself, and leave me out;
For, by this heaven, now at our sorrows pale,
Say what thou canst, I'll go along with thee.

speeches.speech: Why, whither shall we go?
lines: Why, whither shall we go?

speeches.speech: To seek my uncle in the Forest of Arden.
lines: To seek my uncle in the Forest of Arden.

speeches.speech: Alas, what danger will it be to us, Maids as we are, to travel forth so far! Beauty provoketh thieves sooner than gold.
lines: Alas, what danger will it be to us,
Maids as we are, to travel forth so far!
Beauty provoketh thieves sooner than gold.

speeches.speech: I'll put myself in poor and mean attire, And with a kind of umber smirch my face; The like do you; so shall we pass along, And never stir assailants.
lines: I'll put myself in poor and mean attire,
And with a kind of umber smirch my face;
The like do you; so shall we pass along,
And never stir assailants.

speeches.speech: Were it not better, Because that I am more than common tall, That I did suit me all points like a man? A gallant curtle-axe upon my thigh, A boar spear in my hand; and- in my heart Lie there what hidden woman's fear there will- We'll have a swashing and a martial outside, As many other mannish cowards have That do outface it with their semblances.
lines: Were it not better,
Because that I am more than common tall,
That I did suit me all points like a man?
A gallant curtle-axe upon my thigh,
A boar spear in my hand; and- in my heart
Lie there what hidden woman's fear there will-
We'll have a swashing and a martial outside,
As many other mannish cowards have
That do outface it with their semblances.

speeches.speech: What shall I call thee when thou art a man?
lines: What shall I call thee when thou art a man?

speeches.speech: I'll have no worse a name than Jove's own page, And therefore look you call me Ganymede. But what will you be call'd?
lines: I'll have no worse a name than Jove's own page,
And therefore look you call me Ganymede.
But what will you be call'd?

speeches.speech: Something that hath a reference to my state: No longer Celia, but Aliena.
lines: Something that hath a reference to my state:
No longer Celia, but Aliena.

speeches.speech: But, cousin, what if we assay'd to steal The clownish fool out of your father's court? Would he not be a comfort to our travel?
lines: But, cousin, what if we assay'd to steal
The clownish fool out of your father's court?
Would he not be a comfort to our travel?

speeches.speech: He'll go along o'er the wide world with me; Leave me alone to woo him. Let's away, And get our jewels and our wealth together; Devise the fittest time and safest way To hide us from pursuit that will be made After my flight. Now go we in content To liberty, and not to banishment.               Exeunt
lines: He'll go along o'er the wide world with me;
Leave me alone to woo him. Let's away,
And get our jewels and our wealth together;
Devise the fittest time and safest way
To hide us from pursuit that will be made
After my flight. Now go we in content
To liberty, and not to banishment.               Exeunt

speeches.speech: Now, my co-mates and brothers in exile, Hath not old custom made this life more sweet Than that of painted pomp? Are not these woods More free from peril than the envious court? Here feel we not the penalty of Adam, The seasons' difference; as the icy fang And churlish chiding of the winter's wind, Which when it bites and blows upon my body, Even till I shrink with cold, I smile and say 'This is no flattery; these are counsellors That feelingly persuade me what I am.' Sweet are the uses of adversity, Which, like the toad, ugly and venomous, Wears yet a precious jewel in his head; And this our life, exempt from public haunt, Finds tongues in trees, books in the running brooks, Sermons in stones, and good in everything. I would not change it.
lines: Now, my co-mates and brothers in exile,
Hath not old custom made this life more sweet
Than that of painted pomp? Are not these woods
More free from peril than the envious court?
Here feel we not the penalty of Adam,
The seasons' difference; as the icy fang
And churlish chiding of the winter's wind,
Which when it bites and blows upon my body,
Even till I shrink with cold, I smile and say
'This is no flattery; these are counsellors
That feelingly persuade me what I am.'
Sweet are the uses of adversity,
Which, like the toad, ugly and venomous,
Wears yet a precious jewel in his head;
And this our life, exempt from public haunt,
Finds tongues in trees, books in the running brooks,
Sermons in stones, and good in everything.
I would not change it.

speeches.speech: Happy is your Grace, That can translate the stubbornness of fortune Into so quiet and so sweet a style.
lines: Happy is your Grace,
That can translate the stubbornness of fortune
Into so quiet and so sweet a style.

speeches.speech: Come, shall we go and kill us venison? And yet it irks me the poor dappled fools, Being native burghers of this desert city, Should, in their own confines, with forked heads Have their round haunches gor'd.
lines: Come, shall we go and kill us venison?
And yet it irks me the poor dappled fools,
Being native burghers of this desert city,
Should, in their own confines, with forked heads
Have their round haunches gor'd.

speeches.speech: Indeed, my lord, The melancholy Jaques grieves at that; And, in that kind, swears you do more usurp Than doth your brother that hath banish'd you. To-day my Lord of Amiens and myself Did steal behind him as he lay along Under an oak whose antique root peeps out Upon the brook that brawls along this wood! To the which place a poor sequest'red stag, That from the hunter's aim had ta'en a hurt, Did come to languish; and, indeed, my lord, The wretched animal heav'd forth such groans That their discharge did stretch his leathern coat Almost to bursting; and the big round tears Cours'd one another down his innocent nose In piteous chase; and thus the hairy fool, Much marked of the melancholy Jaques, Stood on th' extremest verge of the swift brook, Augmenting it with tears.
lines: Indeed, my lord,
The melancholy Jaques grieves at that;
And, in that kind, swears you do more usurp
Than doth your brother that hath banish'd you.
To-day my Lord of Amiens and myself
Did steal behind him as he lay along
Under an oak whose antique root peeps out
Upon the brook that brawls along this wood!
To the which place a poor sequest'red stag,
That from the hunter's aim had ta'en a hurt,
Did come to languish; and, indeed, my lord,
The wretched animal heav'd forth such groans
That their discharge did stretch his leathern coat
Almost to bursting; and the big round tears
Cours'd one another down his innocent nose
In piteous chase; and thus the hairy fool,
Much marked of the melancholy Jaques,
Stood on th' extremest verge of the swift brook,
Augmenting it with tears.

speeches.speech: But what said Jaques? Did he not moralize this spectacle?
lines: But what said Jaques?
Did he not moralize this spectacle?

speeches.speech: O, yes, into a thousand similes. First, for his weeping into the needless stream: 'Poor deer,' quoth he 'thou mak'st a testament As worldlings do, giving thy sum of more To that which had too much.' Then, being there alone, Left and abandoned of his velvet friends: ''Tis right'; quoth he 'thus misery doth part The flux of company.' Anon, a careless herd, Full of the pasture, jumps along by him And never stays to greet him. 'Ay,' quoth Jaques 'Sweep on, you fat and greasy citizens; 'Tis just the fashion. Wherefore do you look Upon that poor and broken bankrupt there?' Thus most invectively he pierceth through The body of the country, city, court, Yea, and of this our life; swearing that we Are mere usurpers, tyrants, and what's worse, To fright the animals, and to kill them up In their assign'd and native dwelling-place.
lines: O, yes, into a thousand similes.
First, for his weeping into the needless stream:
'Poor deer,' quoth he 'thou mak'st a testament
As worldlings do, giving thy sum of more
To that which had too much.' Then, being there alone,
Left and abandoned of his velvet friends:
''Tis right'; quoth he 'thus misery doth part
The flux of company.' Anon, a careless herd,
Full of the pasture, jumps along by him
And never stays to greet him. 'Ay,' quoth Jaques
'Sweep on, you fat and greasy citizens;
'Tis just the fashion. Wherefore do you look
Upon that poor and broken bankrupt there?'
Thus most invectively he pierceth through
The body of the country, city, court,
Yea, and of this our life; swearing that we
Are mere usurpers, tyrants, and what's worse,
To fright the animals, and to kill them up
In their assign'd and native dwelling-place.

speeches.speech: And did you leave him in this contemplation?
lines: And did you leave him in this contemplation?

speeches.speech: We did, my lord, weeping and commenting Upon the sobbing deer.
lines: We did, my lord, weeping and commenting
Upon the sobbing deer.

speeches.speech: Show me the place; I love to cope him in these sullen fits, For then he's full of matter.
lines: Show me the place;
I love to cope him in these sullen fits,
For then he's full of matter.

speeches.speech: I'll bring you to him straight.        Exeunt
lines: I'll bring you to him straight.        Exeunt

speeches.speech: Can it be possible that no man saw them? It cannot be; some villains of my court Are of consent and sufferance in this.
lines: Can it be possible that no man saw them?
It cannot be; some villains of my court
Are of consent and sufferance in this.

speeches.speech: I cannot hear of any that did see her. The ladies, her attendants of her chamber, Saw her abed, and in the morning early They found the bed untreasur'd of their mistress.
lines: I cannot hear of any that did see her.
The ladies, her attendants of her chamber,
Saw her abed, and in the morning early
They found the bed untreasur'd of their mistress.

speeches.speech: My lord, the roynish clown, at whom so oft Your Grace was wont to laugh, is also missing. Hisperia, the Princess' gentlewoman, Confesses that she secretly o'erheard Your daughter and her cousin much commend The parts and graces of the wrestler That did but lately foil the sinewy Charles; And she believes, wherever they are gone, That youth is surely in their company.
lines: My lord, the roynish clown, at whom so oft
Your Grace was wont to laugh, is also missing.
Hisperia, the Princess' gentlewoman,
Confesses that she secretly o'erheard
Your daughter and her cousin much commend
The parts and graces of the wrestler
That did but lately foil the sinewy Charles;
And she believes, wherever they are gone,
That youth is surely in their company.

speeches.speech: Send to his brother; fetch that gallant hither. If he be absent, bring his brother to me; I'll make him find him. Do this suddenly; And let not search and inquisition quail To bring again these foolish runaways.           Exeunt
lines: Send to his brother; fetch that gallant hither.
If he be absent, bring his brother to me;
I'll make him find him. Do this suddenly;
And let not search and inquisition quail
To bring again these foolish runaways.           Exeunt

speeches.speech: Who's there?
lines: Who's there?

speeches.speech: What, my young master? O my gentle master! O my sweet master! O you memory Of old Sir Rowland! Why, what make you here? Why are you virtuous? Why do people love you? And wherefore are you gentle, strong, and valiant? Why would you be so fond to overcome The bonny prizer of the humorous Duke? Your praise is come too swiftly home before you. Know you not, master, to some kind of men Their graces serve them but as enemies? No more do yours. Your virtues, gentle master, Are sanctified and holy traitors to you. O, what a world is this, when what is comely Envenoms him that bears it!
lines: What, my young master? O my gentle master!
O my sweet master! O you memory
Of old Sir Rowland! Why, what make you here?
Why are you virtuous? Why do people love you?
And wherefore are you gentle, strong, and valiant?
Why would you be so fond to overcome
The bonny prizer of the humorous Duke?
Your praise is come too swiftly home before you.
Know you not, master, to some kind of men
Their graces serve them but as enemies?
No more do yours. Your virtues, gentle master,
Are sanctified and holy traitors to you.
O, what a world is this, when what is comely
Envenoms him that bears it!

speeches.speech: Why, what's the matter?
lines: Why, what's the matter?

speeches.speech: O unhappy youth! Come not within these doors; within this roof The enemy of all your graces lives. Your brother- no, no brother; yet the son- Yet not the son; I will not call him son Of him I was about to call his father- Hath heard your praises; and this night he means To burn the lodging where you use to lie, And you within it. If he fail of that, He will have other means to cut you off; I overheard him and his practices. This is no place; this house is but a butchery; Abhor it, fear it, do not enter it.
lines: O unhappy youth!
Come not within these doors; within this roof
The enemy of all your graces lives.
Your brother- no, no brother; yet the son-
Yet not the son; I will not call him son
Of him I was about to call his father-
Hath heard your praises; and this night he means
To burn the lodging where you use to lie,
And you within it. If he fail of that,
He will have other means to cut you off;
I overheard him and his practices.
This is no place; this house is but a butchery;
Abhor it, fear it, do not enter it.

speeches.speech: Why, whither, Adam, wouldst thou have me go?
lines: Why, whither, Adam, wouldst thou have me go?

speeches.speech: No matter whither, so you come not here.
lines: No matter whither, so you come not here.

speeches.speech: What, wouldst thou have me go and beg my food, Or with a base and boist'rous sword enforce A thievish living on the common road? This I must do, or know not what to do; Yet this I will not do, do how I can. I rather will subject me to the malice Of a diverted blood and bloody brother.
lines: What, wouldst thou have me go and beg my food,
Or with a base and boist'rous sword enforce
A thievish living on the common road?
This I must do, or know not what to do;
Yet this I will not do, do how I can.
I rather will subject me to the malice
Of a diverted blood and bloody brother.

speeches.speech: But do not so. I have five hundred crowns, The thrifty hire I sav'd under your father, Which I did store to be my foster-nurse, When service should in my old limbs lie lame, And unregarded age in corners thrown. Take that, and He that doth the ravens feed, Yea, providently caters for the sparrow, Be comfort to my age! Here is the gold; All this I give you. Let me be your servant; Though I look old, yet I am strong and lusty; For in my youth I never did apply Hot and rebellious liquors in my blood, Nor did not with unbashful forehead woo The means of weakness and debility; Therefore my age is as a lusty winter, Frosty, but kindly. Let me go with you; I'll do the service of a younger man In all your business and necessities.
lines: But do not so. I have five hundred crowns,
The thrifty hire I sav'd under your father,
Which I did store to be my foster-nurse,
When service should in my old limbs lie lame,
And unregarded age in corners thrown.
Take that, and He that doth the ravens feed,
Yea, providently caters for the sparrow,
Be comfort to my age! Here is the gold;
All this I give you. Let me be your servant;
Though I look old, yet I am strong and lusty;
For in my youth I never did apply
Hot and rebellious liquors in my blood,
Nor did not with unbashful forehead woo
The means of weakness and debility;
Therefore my age is as a lusty winter,
Frosty, but kindly. Let me go with you;
I'll do the service of a younger man
In all your business and necessities.

speeches.speech: O good old man, how well in thee appears The constant service of the antique world, When service sweat for duty, not for meed! Thou art not for the fashion of these times, Where none will sweat but for promotion, And having that do choke their service up Even with the having; it is not so with thee. But, poor old man, thou prun'st a rotten tree That cannot so much as a blossom yield In lieu of all thy pains and husbandry. But come thy ways, we'll go along together, And ere we have thy youthful wages spent We'll light upon some settled low content.
lines: O good old man, how well in thee appears
The constant service of the antique world,
When service sweat for duty, not for meed!
Thou art not for the fashion of these times,
Where none will sweat but for promotion,
And having that do choke their service up
Even with the having; it is not so with thee.
But, poor old man, thou prun'st a rotten tree
That cannot so much as a blossom yield
In lieu of all thy pains and husbandry.
But come thy ways, we'll go along together,
And ere we have thy youthful wages spent
We'll light upon some settled low content.

speeches.speech: Master, go on; and I will follow thee To the last gasp, with truth and loyalty. From seventeen years till now almost four-score Here lived I, but now live here no more. At seventeen years many their fortunes seek, But at fourscore it is too late a week; Yet fortune cannot recompense me better Than to die well and not my master's debtor.     Exeunt
lines: Master, go on; and I will follow thee
To the last gasp, with truth and loyalty.
From seventeen years till now almost four-score
Here lived I, but now live here no more.
At seventeen years many their fortunes seek,
But at fourscore it is too late a week;
Yet fortune cannot recompense me better
Than to die well and not my master's debtor.     Exeunt

speeches.speech: O Jupiter, how weary are my spirits!
lines: O Jupiter, how weary are my spirits!

speeches.speech: I care not for my spirits, if my legs were not weary.
lines: I care not for my spirits, if my legs were not weary.

speeches.speech: I could find in my heart to disgrace my man's apparel, and to cry like a woman; but I must comfort the weaker vessel, as doublet and hose ought to show itself courageous to petticoat; therefore, courage, good Aliena.
lines: I could find in my heart to disgrace my man's apparel,
and to cry like a woman; but I must comfort the weaker vessel, as
doublet and hose ought to show itself courageous to petticoat;
therefore, courage, good Aliena.

speeches.speech: I pray you bear with me; I cannot go no further.
lines: I pray you bear with me; I cannot go no further.

speeches.speech: For my part, I had rather bear with you than bear you; yet I should bear no cross if I did bear you; for I think you have no money in your purse.
lines: For my part, I had rather bear with you than bear you;
yet I should bear no cross if I did bear you; for I think you
have no money in your purse.

speeches.speech: Well, this is the Forest of Arden.
lines: Well, this is the Forest of Arden.

speeches.speech: Ay, now am I in Arden; the more fool I; when I was at home I was in a better place; but travellers must be content.
lines: Ay, now am I in Arden; the more fool I; when I was at
home I was in a better place; but travellers must be content.

speeches.speech: Ay, be so, good Touchstone. Look you, who comes here, a young man and an old in solemn talk.
lines: Ay, be so, good Touchstone. Look you, who comes here, a
young man and an old in solemn talk.

speeches.speech: That is the way to make her scorn you still.
lines: That is the way to make her scorn you still.

speeches.speech: O Corin, that thou knew'st how I do love her!
lines: O Corin, that thou knew'st how I do love her!

speeches.speech: I partly guess; for I have lov'd ere now.
lines: I partly guess; for I have lov'd ere now.

speeches.speech: No, Corin, being old, thou canst not guess, Though in thy youth thou wast as true a lover As ever sigh'd upon a midnight pillow. But if thy love were ever like to mine, As sure I think did never man love so, How many actions most ridiculous Hast thou been drawn to by thy fantasy?
lines: No, Corin, being old, thou canst not guess,
Though in thy youth thou wast as true a lover
As ever sigh'd upon a midnight pillow.
But if thy love were ever like to mine,
As sure I think did never man love so,
How many actions most ridiculous
Hast thou been drawn to by thy fantasy?

speeches.speech: Into a thousand that I have forgotten.
lines: Into a thousand that I have forgotten.

speeches.speech: O, thou didst then never love so heartily! If thou rememb'rest not the slightest folly That ever love did make thee run into, Thou hast not lov'd; Or if thou hast not sat as I do now, Wearing thy hearer in thy mistress' praise, Thou hast not lov'd; Or if thou hast not broke from company Abruptly, as my passion now makes me, Thou hast not lov'd. O Phebe, Phebe, Phebe!                          Exit Silvius
lines: O, thou didst then never love so heartily!
If thou rememb'rest not the slightest folly
That ever love did make thee run into,
Thou hast not lov'd;
Or if thou hast not sat as I do now,
Wearing thy hearer in thy mistress' praise,
Thou hast not lov'd;
Or if thou hast not broke from company
Abruptly, as my passion now makes me,
Thou hast not lov'd.
O Phebe, Phebe, Phebe!                          Exit Silvius

speeches.speech: Alas, poor shepherd! searching of thy wound, I have by hard adventure found mine own.
lines: Alas, poor shepherd! searching of thy wound,
I have by hard adventure found mine own.

speeches.speech: And I mine. I remember, when I was in love, I broke my sword upon a stone, and bid him take that for coming a-night to Jane Smile; and I remember the kissing of her batler, and the cow's dugs that her pretty chapt hands had milk'd; and I remember the wooing of peascod instead of her; from whom I took two cods, and giving her them again, said with weeping tears 'Wear these for my sake.' We that are true lovers run into strange capers; but as all is mortal in nature, so is all nature in love mortal in folly.
lines: And I mine. I remember, when I was in love, I broke my
sword upon a stone, and bid him take that for coming a-night to
Jane Smile; and I remember the kissing of her batler, and the
cow's dugs that her pretty chapt hands had milk'd; and I remember
the wooing of peascod instead of her; from whom I took two cods,
and giving her them again, said with weeping tears 'Wear these
for my sake.' We that are true lovers run into strange capers;
but as all is mortal in nature, so is all nature in love mortal
in folly.

speeches.speech: Thou speak'st wiser than thou art ware of.
lines: Thou speak'st wiser than thou art ware of.

speeches.speech: Nay, I shall ne'er be ware of mine own wit till I break my shins against it.
lines: Nay, I shall ne'er be ware of mine own wit till I break
my shins against it.

speeches.speech: Jove, Jove! this shepherd's passion Is much upon my fashion.
lines: Jove, Jove! this shepherd's passion
Is much upon my fashion.

speeches.speech: And mine; but it grows something stale with me.
lines: And mine; but it grows something stale with me.

speeches.speech: I pray you, one of you question yond man If he for gold will give us any food; I faint almost to death.
lines: I pray you, one of you question yond man
If he for gold will give us any food;
I faint almost to death.

speeches.speech: Holla, you clown!
lines: Holla, you clown!

speeches.speech: Peace, fool; he's not thy kinsman.
lines: Peace, fool; he's not thy kinsman.

speeches.speech: Who calls?
lines: Who calls?

speeches.speech: Your betters, sir.
lines: Your betters, sir.

speeches.speech: Else are they very wretched.
lines: Else are they very wretched.

speeches.speech: Peace, I say. Good even to you, friend.
lines: Peace, I say. Good even to you, friend.

speeches.speech: And to you, gentle sir, and to you all.
lines: And to you, gentle sir, and to you all.

speeches.speech: I prithee, shepherd, if that love or gold Can in this desert place buy entertainment, Bring us where we may rest ourselves and feed. Here's a young maid with travel much oppress'd, And faints for succour.
lines: I prithee, shepherd, if that love or gold
Can in this desert place buy entertainment,
Bring us where we may rest ourselves and feed.
Here's a young maid with travel much oppress'd,
And faints for succour.

speeches.speech: Fair sir, I pity her, And wish, for her sake more than for mine own, My fortunes were more able to relieve her; But I am shepherd to another man, And do not shear the fleeces that I graze. My master is of churlish disposition, And little recks to find the way to heaven By doing deeds of hospitality. Besides, his cote, his flocks, and bounds of feed, Are now on sale; and at our sheepcote now, By reason of his absence, there is nothing That you will feed on; but what is, come see, And in my voice most welcome shall you be.
lines: Fair sir, I pity her,
And wish, for her sake more than for mine own,
My fortunes were more able to relieve her;
But I am shepherd to another man,
And do not shear the fleeces that I graze.
My master is of churlish disposition,
And little recks to find the way to heaven
By doing deeds of hospitality.
Besides, his cote, his flocks, and bounds of feed,
Are now on sale; and at our sheepcote now,
By reason of his absence, there is nothing
That you will feed on; but what is, come see,
And in my voice most welcome shall you be.

speeches.speech: What is he that shall buy his flock and pasture?
lines: What is he that shall buy his flock and pasture?

speeches.speech: That young swain that you saw here but erewhile, That little cares for buying any thing.
lines: That young swain that you saw here but erewhile,
That little cares for buying any thing.

speeches.speech: I pray thee, if it stand with honesty, Buy thou the cottage, pasture, and the flock, And thou shalt have to pay for it of us.
lines: I pray thee, if it stand with honesty,
Buy thou the cottage, pasture, and the flock,
And thou shalt have to pay for it of us.

speeches.speech: And we will mend thy wages. I like this place, And willingly could waste my time in it.
lines: And we will mend thy wages. I like this place,
And willingly could waste my time in it.

speeches.speech: Assuredly the thing is to be sold. Go with me; if you like upon report The soil, the profit, and this kind of life, I will your very faithful feeder be, And buy it with your gold right suddenly.        Exeunt
lines: Assuredly the thing is to be sold.
Go with me; if you like upon report
The soil, the profit, and this kind of life,
I will your very faithful feeder be,
And buy it with your gold right suddenly.        Exeunt

speeches.speech: Under the greenwood tree Who loves to lie with me, And turn his merry note Unto the sweet bird's throat, Come hither, come hither, come hither. Here shall he see No enemy But winter and rough weather.
lines: Under the greenwood tree
Who loves to lie with me,
And turn his merry note
Unto the sweet bird's throat,
Come hither, come hither, come hither.
Here shall he see
No enemy
But winter and rough weather.

speeches.speech: More, more, I prithee, more.
lines: More, more, I prithee, more.

speeches.speech: It will make you melancholy, Monsieur Jaques.
lines: It will make you melancholy, Monsieur Jaques.

speeches.speech: I thank it. More, I prithee, more. I can suck melancholy out of a song, as a weasel sucks eggs. More, I prithee, more.
lines: I thank it. More, I prithee, more. I can suck melancholy
out of a song, as a weasel sucks eggs. More, I prithee, more.

speeches.speech: My voice is ragged; I know I cannot please you.
lines: My voice is ragged; I know I cannot please you.

speeches.speech: I do not desire you to please me; I do desire you to sing. Come, more; another stanzo. Call you 'em stanzos?
lines: I do not desire you to please me; I do desire you to sing.
Come, more; another stanzo. Call you 'em stanzos?

speeches.speech: What you will, Monsieur Jaques.
lines: What you will, Monsieur Jaques.

speeches.speech: Nay, I care not for their names; they owe me nothing. Will you sing?
lines: Nay, I care not for their names; they owe me nothing. Will
you sing?

speeches.speech: More at your request than to please myself.
lines: More at your request than to please myself.

speeches.speech: Well then, if ever I thank any man, I'll thank you; but that they call compliment is like th' encounter of two dog-apes; and when a man thanks me heartily, methinks have given him a penny, and he renders me the beggarly thanks. Come, sing; and you that will not, hold your tongues.
lines: Well then, if ever I thank any man, I'll thank you; but
that they call compliment is like th' encounter of two dog-apes;
and when a man thanks me heartily, methinks have given him a
penny, and he renders me the beggarly thanks. Come, sing; and you
that will not, hold your tongues.

speeches.speech: Well, I'll end the song. Sirs, cover the while; the Duke will drink under this tree. He hath been all this day to look you.
lines: Well, I'll end the song. Sirs, cover the while; the Duke
will drink under this tree. He hath been all this day to look
you.

speeches.speech: And I have been all this day to avoid him. He is too disputable for my company. I think of as many matters as he; but I give heaven thanks, and make no boast of them. Come, warble, come. SONG [All together here] Who doth ambition shun, And loves to live i' th' sun, Seeking the food he eats, And pleas'd with what he gets, Come hither, come hither, come hither. Here shall he see No enemy But winter and rough weather.
lines: And I have been all this day to avoid him. He is too
disputable for my company. I think of as many matters as he; but
I give heaven thanks, and make no boast of them. Come, warble, come.
SONG
[All together here]
Who doth ambition shun,
And loves to live i' th' sun,
Seeking the food he eats,
And pleas'd with what he gets,
Come hither, come hither, come hither.
Here shall he see
No enemy
