But winter and rough weather.

speeches.speech: I'll give you a verse to this note that I made yesterday in despite of my invention.
lines: I'll give you a verse to this note that I made yesterday in
despite of my invention.

speeches.speech: And I'll sing it.
lines: And I'll sing it.

speeches.speech: Thus it goes: If it do come to pass That any man turn ass, Leaving his wealth and ease A stubborn will to please, Ducdame, ducdame, ducdame; Here shall he see Gross fools as he, An if he will come to me.
lines: Thus it goes:
If it do come to pass
That any man turn ass,
Leaving his wealth and ease
A stubborn will to please,
Ducdame, ducdame, ducdame;
Here shall he see
Gross fools as he,
An if he will come to me.

speeches.speech: What's that 'ducdame'?
lines: What's that 'ducdame'?

speeches.speech: 'Tis a Greek invocation, to call fools into a circle. I'll go sleep, if I can; if I cannot, I'll rail against all the first-born of Egypt.
lines: 'Tis a Greek invocation, to call fools into a circle. I'll
go sleep, if I can; if I cannot, I'll rail against all the
first-born of Egypt.

speeches.speech: And I'll go seek the Duke; his banquet is prepar'd.
lines: And I'll go seek the Duke; his banquet is prepar'd.

speeches.speech: Dear master, I can go no further. O, I die for food! Here lie I down, and measure out my grave. Farewell, kind master.
lines: Dear master, I can go no further. O, I die for food! Here lie
I down, and measure out my grave. Farewell, kind master.

speeches.speech: Why, how now, Adam! No greater heart in thee? Live a little; comfort a little; cheer thyself a little. If this uncouth forest yield anything savage, I will either be food for it or bring it for food to thee. Thy conceit is nearer death than thy powers. For my sake be comfortable; hold death awhile at the arm's end. I will here be with thee presently; and if I bring thee not something to eat, I will give thee leave to die; but if thou diest before I come, thou art a mocker of my labour. Well said! thou look'st cheerly; and I'll be with thee quickly. Yet thou liest in the bleak air. Come, I will bear thee to some shelter; and thou shalt not die for lack of a dinner, if there live anything in this desert. Cheerly, good Adam!          Exeunt
lines: Why, how now, Adam! No greater heart in thee? Live a
little; comfort a little; cheer thyself a little. If this uncouth
forest yield anything savage, I will either be food for it or
bring it for food to thee. Thy conceit is nearer death than thy
powers. For my sake be comfortable; hold death awhile at the
arm's end. I will here be with thee presently; and if I bring thee
not something to eat, I will give thee leave to die; but if thou
diest before I come, thou art a mocker of my labour. Well said!
thou look'st cheerly; and I'll be with thee quickly. Yet thou
liest in the bleak air. Come, I will bear thee to some shelter;
and thou shalt not die for lack of a dinner, if there live
anything in this desert. Cheerly, good Adam!          Exeunt

speeches.speech: I think he be transform'd into a beast; For I can nowhere find him like a man.
lines: I think he be transform'd into a beast;
For I can nowhere find him like a man.

speeches.speech: My lord, he is but even now gone hence; Here was he merry, hearing of a song.
lines: My lord, he is but even now gone hence;
Here was he merry, hearing of a song.

speeches.speech: If he, compact of jars, grow musical, We shall have shortly discord in the spheres. Go seek him; tell him I would speak with him.
lines: If he, compact of jars, grow musical,
We shall have shortly discord in the spheres.
Go seek him; tell him I would speak with him.

speeches.speech: He saves my labour by his own approach.
lines: He saves my labour by his own approach.

speeches.speech: Why, how now, monsieur! what a life is this, That your poor friends must woo your company? What, you look merrily!
lines: Why, how now, monsieur! what a life is this,
That your poor friends must woo your company?
What, you look merrily!

speeches.speech: A fool, a fool! I met a fool i' th' forest, A motley fool. A miserable world! As I do live by food, I met a fool, Who laid him down and bask'd him in the sun, And rail'd on Lady Fortune in good terms, In good set terms- and yet a motley fool. 'Good morrow, fool,' quoth I; 'No, sir,' quoth he, 'Call me not fool till heaven hath sent me fortune.' And then he drew a dial from his poke, And, looking on it with lack-lustre eye, Says very wisely, 'It is ten o'clock; Thus we may see,' quoth he, 'how the world wags; 'Tis but an hour ago since it was nine; And after one hour more 'twill be eleven; And so, from hour to hour, we ripe and ripe, And then, from hour to hour, we rot and rot; And thereby hangs a tale.' When I did hear The motley fool thus moral on the time, My lungs began to crow like chanticleer That fools should be so deep contemplative; And I did laugh sans intermission An hour by his dial. O noble fool! A worthy fool! Motley's the only wear.
lines: A fool, a fool! I met a fool i' th' forest,
A motley fool. A miserable world!
As I do live by food, I met a fool,
Who laid him down and bask'd him in the sun,
And rail'd on Lady Fortune in good terms,
In good set terms- and yet a motley fool.
'Good morrow, fool,' quoth I; 'No, sir,' quoth he,
'Call me not fool till heaven hath sent me fortune.'
And then he drew a dial from his poke,
And, looking on it with lack-lustre eye,
Says very wisely, 'It is ten o'clock;
Thus we may see,' quoth he, 'how the world wags;
'Tis but an hour ago since it was nine;
And after one hour more 'twill be eleven;
And so, from hour to hour, we ripe and ripe,
And then, from hour to hour, we rot and rot;
And thereby hangs a tale.' When I did hear
The motley fool thus moral on the time,
My lungs began to crow like chanticleer
That fools should be so deep contemplative;
And I did laugh sans intermission
An hour by his dial. O noble fool!
A worthy fool! Motley's the only wear.

speeches.speech: What fool is this?
lines: What fool is this?

speeches.speech: O worthy fool! One that hath been a courtier, And says, if ladies be but young and fair, They have the gift to know it; and in his brain, Which is as dry as the remainder biscuit After a voyage, he hath strange places cramm'd With observation, the which he vents In mangled forms. O that I were a fool! I am ambitious for a motley coat.
lines: O worthy fool! One that hath been a courtier,
And says, if ladies be but young and fair,
They have the gift to know it; and in his brain,
Which is as dry as the remainder biscuit
After a voyage, he hath strange places cramm'd
With observation, the which he vents
In mangled forms. O that I were a fool!
I am ambitious for a motley coat.

speeches.speech: Thou shalt have one.
lines: Thou shalt have one.

speeches.speech: It is my only suit, Provided that you weed your better judgments Of all opinion that grows rank in them That I am wise. I must have liberty Withal, as large a charter as the wind, To blow on whom I please, for so fools have; And they that are most galled with my folly, They most must laugh. And why, sir, must they so? The why is plain as way to parish church: He that a fool doth very wisely hit Doth very foolishly, although he smart, Not to seem senseless of the bob; if not, The wise man's folly is anatomiz'd Even by the squand'ring glances of the fool. Invest me in my motley; give me leave To speak my mind, and I will through and through Cleanse the foul body of th' infected world, If they will patiently receive my medicine.
lines: It is my only suit,
Provided that you weed your better judgments
Of all opinion that grows rank in them
That I am wise. I must have liberty
Withal, as large a charter as the wind,
To blow on whom I please, for so fools have;
And they that are most galled with my folly,
They most must laugh. And why, sir, must they so?
The why is plain as way to parish church:
He that a fool doth very wisely hit
Doth very foolishly, although he smart,
Not to seem senseless of the bob; if not,
The wise man's folly is anatomiz'd
Even by the squand'ring glances of the fool.
Invest me in my motley; give me leave
To speak my mind, and I will through and through
Cleanse the foul body of th' infected world,
If they will patiently receive my medicine.

speeches.speech: Fie on thee! I can tell what thou wouldst do.
lines: Fie on thee! I can tell what thou wouldst do.

speeches.speech: What, for a counter, would I do but good?
lines: What, for a counter, would I do but good?

speeches.speech: Most mischievous foul sin, in chiding sin; For thou thyself hast been a libertine, As sensual as the brutish sting itself; And all th' embossed sores and headed evils That thou with license of free foot hast caught Wouldst thou disgorge into the general world.
lines: Most mischievous foul sin, in chiding sin;
For thou thyself hast been a libertine,
As sensual as the brutish sting itself;
And all th' embossed sores and headed evils
That thou with license of free foot hast caught
Wouldst thou disgorge into the general world.

speeches.speech: Why, who cries out on pride That can therein tax any private party? Doth it not flow as hugely as the sea, Till that the wearer's very means do ebb? What woman in the city do I name When that I say the city-woman bears The cost of princes on unworthy shoulders? Who can come in and say that I mean her, When such a one as she such is her neighbour? Or what is he of basest function That says his bravery is not on my cost, Thinking that I mean him, but therein suits His folly to the mettle of my speech? There then! how then? what then? Let me see wherein My tongue hath wrong'd him: if it do him right, Then he hath wrong'd himself; if he be free, Why then my taxing like a wild-goose flies, Unclaim'd of any man. But who comes here?
lines: Why, who cries out on pride
That can therein tax any private party?
Doth it not flow as hugely as the sea,
Till that the wearer's very means do ebb?
What woman in the city do I name
When that I say the city-woman bears
The cost of princes on unworthy shoulders?
Who can come in and say that I mean her,
When such a one as she such is her neighbour?
Or what is he of basest function
That says his bravery is not on my cost,
Thinking that I mean him, but therein suits
His folly to the mettle of my speech?
There then! how then? what then? Let me see wherein
My tongue hath wrong'd him: if it do him right,
Then he hath wrong'd himself; if he be free,
Why then my taxing like a wild-goose flies,
Unclaim'd of any man. But who comes here?

speeches.speech: Forbear, and eat no more.
lines: Forbear, and eat no more.

speeches.speech: Why, I have eat none yet.
lines: Why, I have eat none yet.

speeches.speech: Nor shalt not, till necessity be serv'd.
lines: Nor shalt not, till necessity be serv'd.

speeches.speech: Of what kind should this cock come of?
lines: Of what kind should this cock come of?

speeches.speech: Art thou thus bolden'd, man, by thy distress? Or else a rude despiser of good manners, That in civility thou seem'st so empty?
lines: Art thou thus bolden'd, man, by thy distress?
Or else a rude despiser of good manners,
That in civility thou seem'st so empty?

speeches.speech: You touch'd my vein at first: the thorny point Of bare distress hath ta'en from me the show Of smooth civility; yet am I inland bred, And know some nurture. But forbear, I say; He dies that touches any of this fruit Till I and my affairs are answered.
lines: You touch'd my vein at first: the thorny point
Of bare distress hath ta'en from me the show
Of smooth civility; yet am I inland bred,
And know some nurture. But forbear, I say;
He dies that touches any of this fruit
Till I and my affairs are answered.

speeches.speech: An you will not be answer'd with reason, I must die.
lines: An you will not be answer'd with reason, I must die.

speeches.speech: What would you have? Your gentleness shall force More than your force move us to gentleness.
lines: What would you have? Your gentleness shall force
More than your force move us to gentleness.

speeches.speech: I almost die for food, and let me have it.
lines: I almost die for food, and let me have it.

speeches.speech: Sit down and feed, and welcome to our table.
lines: Sit down and feed, and welcome to our table.

speeches.speech: Speak you so gently? Pardon me, I pray you; I thought that all things had been savage here, And therefore put I on the countenance Of stern commandment. But whate'er you are That in this desert inaccessible, Under the shade of melancholy boughs, Lose and neglect the creeping hours of time; If ever you have look'd on better days, If ever been where bells have knoll'd to church, If ever sat at any good man's feast, If ever from your eyelids wip'd a tear, And know what 'tis to pity and be pitied, Let gentleness my strong enforcement be; In the which hope I blush, and hide my sword.
lines: Speak you so gently? Pardon me, I pray you;
I thought that all things had been savage here,
And therefore put I on the countenance
Of stern commandment. But whate'er you are
That in this desert inaccessible,
Under the shade of melancholy boughs,
Lose and neglect the creeping hours of time;
If ever you have look'd on better days,
If ever been where bells have knoll'd to church,
If ever sat at any good man's feast,
If ever from your eyelids wip'd a tear,
And know what 'tis to pity and be pitied,
Let gentleness my strong enforcement be;
In the which hope I blush, and hide my sword.

speeches.speech: True is it that we have seen better days, And have with holy bell been knoll'd to church, And sat at good men's feasts, and wip'd our eyes Of drops that sacred pity hath engend'red; And therefore sit you down in gentleness, And take upon command what help we have That to your wanting may be minist'red.
lines: True is it that we have seen better days,
And have with holy bell been knoll'd to church,
And sat at good men's feasts, and wip'd our eyes
Of drops that sacred pity hath engend'red;
And therefore sit you down in gentleness,
And take upon command what help we have
That to your wanting may be minist'red.

speeches.speech: Then but forbear your food a little while, Whiles, like a doe, I go to find my fawn, And give it food. There is an old poor man Who after me hath many a weary step Limp'd in pure love; till he be first suffic'd, Oppress'd with two weak evils, age and hunger, I will not touch a bit.
lines: Then but forbear your food a little while,
Whiles, like a doe, I go to find my fawn,
And give it food. There is an old poor man
Who after me hath many a weary step
Limp'd in pure love; till he be first suffic'd,
Oppress'd with two weak evils, age and hunger,
I will not touch a bit.

speeches.speech: Go find him out. And we will nothing waste till you return.
lines: Go find him out.
And we will nothing waste till you return.

speeches.speech: I thank ye; and be blest for your good comfort!  Exit
lines: I thank ye; and be blest for your good comfort!  Exit

speeches.speech: Thou seest we are not all alone unhappy: This wide and universal theatre Presents more woeful pageants than the scene Wherein we play in.
lines: Thou seest we are not all alone unhappy:
This wide and universal theatre
Presents more woeful pageants than the scene
Wherein we play in.

speeches.speech: All the world's a stage, And all the men and women merely players; They have their exits and their entrances; And one man in his time plays many parts, His acts being seven ages. At first the infant, Mewling and puking in the nurse's arms; Then the whining school-boy, with his satchel And shining morning face, creeping like snail Unwillingly to school. And then the lover, Sighing like furnace, with a woeful ballad Made to his mistress' eyebrow. Then a soldier, Full of strange oaths, and bearded like the pard, Jealous in honour, sudden and quick in quarrel, Seeking the bubble reputation Even in the cannon's mouth. And then the justice, In fair round belly with good capon lin'd, With eyes severe and beard of formal cut, Full of wise saws and modern instances; And so he plays his part. The sixth age shifts Into the lean and slipper'd pantaloon, With spectacles on nose and pouch on side, His youthful hose, well sav'd, a world too wide For his shrunk shank; and his big manly voice, Turning again toward childish treble, pipes And whistles in his sound. Last scene of all, That ends this strange eventful history, Is second childishness and mere oblivion; Sans teeth, sans eyes, sans taste, sans every thing.
lines: All the world's a stage,
And all the men and women merely players;
They have their exits and their entrances;
And one man in his time plays many parts,
His acts being seven ages. At first the infant,
Mewling and puking in the nurse's arms;
Then the whining school-boy, with his satchel
And shining morning face, creeping like snail
Unwillingly to school. And then the lover,
Sighing like furnace, with a woeful ballad
Made to his mistress' eyebrow. Then a soldier,
Full of strange oaths, and bearded like the pard,
Jealous in honour, sudden and quick in quarrel,
Seeking the bubble reputation
Even in the cannon's mouth. And then the justice,
In fair round belly with good capon lin'd,
With eyes severe and beard of formal cut,
Full of wise saws and modern instances;
And so he plays his part. The sixth age shifts
Into the lean and slipper'd pantaloon,
With spectacles on nose and pouch on side,
His youthful hose, well sav'd, a world too wide
For his shrunk shank; and his big manly voice,
Turning again toward childish treble, pipes
And whistles in his sound. Last scene of all,
That ends this strange eventful history,
Is second childishness and mere oblivion;
Sans teeth, sans eyes, sans taste, sans every thing.

speeches.speech: Welcome. Set down your venerable burden, And let him feed.
lines: Welcome. Set down your venerable burden,
And let him feed.

speeches.speech: I thank you most for him.
lines: I thank you most for him.

speeches.speech: So had you need; I scarce can speak to thank you for myself.
lines: So had you need;
I scarce can speak to thank you for myself.

speeches.speech: Welcome; fall to. I will not trouble you As yet to question you about your fortunes. Give us some music; and, good cousin, sing. SONG Blow, blow, thou winter wind, Thou art not so unkind As man's ingratitude; Thy tooth is not so keen, Because thou art not seen, Although thy breath be rude. Heigh-ho! sing heigh-ho! unto the green holly. Most friendship is feigning, most loving mere folly. Then, heigh-ho, the holly! This life is most jolly. Freeze, freeze, thou bitter sky, That dost not bite so nigh As benefits forgot; Though thou the waters warp, Thy sting is not so sharp As friend rememb'red not. Heigh-ho! sing, &c.
lines: Welcome; fall to. I will not trouble you
As yet to question you about your fortunes.
Give us some music; and, good cousin, sing.
SONG
Blow, blow, thou winter wind,
Thou art not so unkind
As man's ingratitude;
Thy tooth is not so keen,
Because thou art not seen,
Although thy breath be rude.
Heigh-ho! sing heigh-ho! unto the green holly.
Most friendship is feigning, most loving mere folly.
Then, heigh-ho, the holly!
This life is most jolly.
Freeze, freeze, thou bitter sky,
That dost not bite so nigh
As benefits forgot;
Though thou the waters warp,
Thy sting is not so sharp
As friend rememb'red not.
Heigh-ho! sing, &c.

speeches.speech: If that you were the good Sir Rowland's son, As you have whisper'd faithfully you were, And as mine eye doth his effigies witness Most truly limn'd and living in your face, Be truly welcome hither. I am the Duke That lov'd your father. The residue of your fortune, Go to my cave and tell me. Good old man, Thou art right welcome as thy master is. Support him by the arm. Give me your hand, And let me all your fortunes understand.         Exeunt
lines: If that you were the good Sir Rowland's son,
As you have whisper'd faithfully you were,
And as mine eye doth his effigies witness
Most truly limn'd and living in your face,
Be truly welcome hither. I am the Duke
That lov'd your father. The residue of your fortune,
Go to my cave and tell me. Good old man,
Thou art right welcome as thy master is.
Support him by the arm. Give me your hand,
And let me all your fortunes understand.         Exeunt

speeches.speech: Not see him since! Sir, sir, that cannot be. But were I not the better part made mercy, I should not seek an absent argument Of my revenge, thou present. But look to it: Find out thy brother wheresoe'er he is; Seek him with candle; bring him dead or living Within this twelvemonth, or turn thou no more To seek a living in our territory. Thy lands and all things that thou dost call thine Worth seizure do we seize into our hands, Till thou canst quit thee by thy brother's mouth Of what we think against thee.
lines: Not see him since! Sir, sir, that cannot be.
But were I not the better part made mercy,
I should not seek an absent argument
Of my revenge, thou present. But look to it:
Find out thy brother wheresoe'er he is;
Seek him with candle; bring him dead or living
Within this twelvemonth, or turn thou no more
To seek a living in our territory.
Thy lands and all things that thou dost call thine
Worth seizure do we seize into our hands,
Till thou canst quit thee by thy brother's mouth
Of what we think against thee.

speeches.speech: O that your Highness knew my heart in this! I never lov'd my brother in my life.
lines: O that your Highness knew my heart in this!
I never lov'd my brother in my life.

speeches.speech: More villain thou. Well, push him out of doors; And let my officers of such a nature Make an extent upon his house and lands. Do this expediently, and turn him going.         Exeunt
lines: More villain thou. Well, push him out of doors;
And let my officers of such a nature
Make an extent upon his house and lands.
Do this expediently, and turn him going.         Exeunt

speeches.speech: Hang there, my verse, in witness of my love; And thou, thrice-crowned Queen of Night, survey With thy chaste eye, from thy pale sphere above, Thy huntress' name that my full life doth sway. O Rosalind! these trees shall be my books, And in their barks my thoughts I'll character, That every eye which in this forest looks Shall see thy virtue witness'd every where. Run, run, Orlando; carve on every tree, The fair, the chaste, and unexpressive she.        Exit
lines: Hang there, my verse, in witness of my love;
And thou, thrice-crowned Queen of Night, survey
With thy chaste eye, from thy pale sphere above,
Thy huntress' name that my full life doth sway.
O Rosalind! these trees shall be my books,
And in their barks my thoughts I'll character,
That every eye which in this forest looks
Shall see thy virtue witness'd every where.
Run, run, Orlando; carve on every tree,
The fair, the chaste, and unexpressive she.        Exit

speeches.speech: And how like you this shepherd's life, Master Touchstone?
lines: And how like you this shepherd's life, Master Touchstone?

speeches.speech: Truly, shepherd, in respect of itself, it is a good life; but in respect that it is a shepherd's life, it is nought. In respect that it is solitary, I like it very well; but in respect that it is private, it is a very vile life. Now in respect it is in the fields, it pleaseth me well; but in respect it is not in the court, it is tedious. As it is a spare life, look you, it fits my humour well; but as there is no more plenty in it, it goes much against my stomach. Hast any philosophy in thee, shepherd?
lines: Truly, shepherd, in respect of itself, it is a good
life; but in respect that it is a shepherd's life, it is nought.
In respect that it is solitary, I like it very well; but in
respect that it is private, it is a very vile life. Now in
respect it is in the fields, it pleaseth me well; but in respect
it is not in the court, it is tedious. As it is a spare life,
look you, it fits my humour well; but as there is no more plenty
in it, it goes much against my stomach. Hast any philosophy in
thee, shepherd?

speeches.speech: No more but that I know the more one sickens the worse at ease he is; and that he that wants money, means, and content, is without three good friends; that the property of rain is to wet, and fire to burn; that good pasture makes fat sheep; and that a great cause of the night is lack of the sun; that he that hath learned no wit by nature nor art may complain of good breeding, or comes of a very dull kindred.
lines: No more but that I know the more one sickens the worse at
ease he is; and that he that wants money, means, and content, is
without three good friends; that the property of rain is to wet,
and fire to burn; that good pasture makes fat sheep; and that a
great cause of the night is lack of the sun; that he that hath
learned no wit by nature nor art may complain of good breeding,
or comes of a very dull kindred.

speeches.speech: Such a one is a natural philosopher. Wast ever in court, shepherd?
lines: Such a one is a natural philosopher. Wast ever in
court, shepherd?

speeches.speech: No, truly.
lines: No, truly.

speeches.speech: Then thou art damn'd.
lines: Then thou art damn'd.

speeches.speech: Nay, I hope.
lines: Nay, I hope.

speeches.speech: Truly, thou art damn'd, like an ill-roasted egg, all on one side.
lines: Truly, thou art damn'd, like an ill-roasted egg, all on
one side.

speeches.speech: For not being at court? Your reason.
lines: For not being at court? Your reason.

speeches.speech: Why, if thou never wast at court thou never saw'st good manners; if thou never saw'st good manners, then thy manners must be wicked; and wickedness is sin, and sin is damnation. Thou art in a parlous state, shepherd.
lines: Why, if thou never wast at court thou never saw'st good
manners; if thou never saw'st good manners, then thy manners must
be wicked; and wickedness is sin, and sin is damnation. Thou art
in a parlous state, shepherd.

speeches.speech: Not a whit, Touchstone. Those that are good manners at the court are as ridiculous in the country as the behaviour of the country is most mockable at the court. You told me you salute not at the court, but you kiss your hands; that courtesy would be uncleanly if courtiers were shepherds.
lines: Not a whit, Touchstone. Those that are good manners at the
court are as ridiculous in the country as the behaviour of the
country is most mockable at the court. You told me you salute not
at the court, but you kiss your hands; that courtesy would be
uncleanly if courtiers were shepherds.

speeches.speech: Instance, briefly; come, instance.
lines: Instance, briefly; come, instance.

speeches.speech: Why, we are still handling our ewes; and their fells, you know, are greasy.
lines: Why, we are still handling our ewes; and their fells, you
know, are greasy.

speeches.speech: Why, do not your courtier's hands sweat? And is not the grease of a mutton as wholesome as the sweat of a man? Shallow, shallow. A better instance, I say; come.
lines: Why, do not your courtier's hands sweat? And is not the
grease of a mutton as wholesome as the sweat of a man? Shallow,
shallow. A better instance, I say; come.

speeches.speech: Besides, our hands are hard.
lines: Besides, our hands are hard.

speeches.speech: Your lips will feel them the sooner. Shallow again. A more sounder instance; come.
lines: Your lips will feel them the sooner. Shallow again. A
more sounder instance; come.

speeches.speech: And they are often tarr'd over with the surgery of our sheep; and would you have us kiss tar? The courtier's hands are perfum'd with civet.
lines: And they are often tarr'd over with the surgery of our
sheep; and would you have us kiss tar? The courtier's hands are
perfum'd with civet.

speeches.speech: Most shallow man! thou worm's meat in respect of a good piece of flesh indeed! Learn of the wise, and perpend: civet is of a baser birth than tar- the very uncleanly flux of a cat. Mend the instance, shepherd.
lines: Most shallow man! thou worm's meat in respect of a good
piece of flesh indeed! Learn of the wise, and perpend: civet is
of a baser birth than tar- the very uncleanly flux of a cat. Mend
the instance, shepherd.

speeches.speech: You have too courtly a wit for me; I'll rest.
lines: You have too courtly a wit for me; I'll rest.

speeches.speech: Wilt thou rest damn'd? God help thee, shallow man! God make incision in thee! thou art raw.
lines: Wilt thou rest damn'd? God help thee, shallow man! God
make incision in thee! thou art raw.

speeches.speech: Sir, I am a true labourer: I earn that I eat, get that I wear; owe no man hate, envy no man's happiness; glad of other men's good, content with my harm; and the greatest of my pride is to see my ewes graze and my lambs suck.
lines: Sir, I am a true labourer: I earn that I eat, get that I
wear; owe no man hate, envy no man's happiness; glad of other
men's good, content with my harm; and the greatest of my pride is
to see my ewes graze and my lambs suck.

speeches.speech: That is another simple sin in you: to bring the ewes and the rams together, and to offer to get your living by the copulation of cattle; to be bawd to a bell-wether, and to betray a she-lamb of a twelvemonth to crooked-pated, old, cuckoldly ram, out of all reasonable match. If thou beest not damn'd for this, the devil himself will have no shepherds; I cannot see else how thou shouldst scape.
lines: That is another simple sin in you: to bring the ewes
and the rams together, and to offer to get your living by the
copulation of cattle; to be bawd to a bell-wether, and to betray
a she-lamb of a twelvemonth to crooked-pated, old, cuckoldly ram,
out of all reasonable match. If thou beest not damn'd for this,
the devil himself will have no shepherds; I cannot see else how
thou shouldst scape.

speeches.speech: Here comes young Master Ganymede, my new mistress's brother.
lines: Here comes young Master Ganymede, my new mistress's brother.

speeches.speech: 'From the east to western Inde, No jewel is like Rosalinde. Her worth, being mounted on the wind, Through all the world bears Rosalinde. All the pictures fairest lin'd Are but black to Rosalinde. Let no face be kept in mind But the fair of Rosalinde.'
lines: 'From the east to western Inde,
No jewel is like Rosalinde.
Her worth, being mounted on the wind,
Through all the world bears Rosalinde.
All the pictures fairest lin'd
Are but black to Rosalinde.
Let no face be kept in mind
But the fair of Rosalinde.'

speeches.speech: I'll rhyme you so eight years together, dinners, and suppers, and sleeping hours, excepted. It is the right butter-women's rank to market.
lines: I'll rhyme you so eight years together, dinners, and
suppers, and sleeping hours, excepted. It is the right
butter-women's rank to market.

speeches.speech: Out, fool!
lines: Out, fool!

speeches.speech: For a taste: If a hart do lack a hind, Let him seek out Rosalinde. If the cat will after kind, So be sure will Rosalinde. Winter garments must be lin'd, So must slender Rosalinde. They that reap must sheaf and bind, Then to cart with Rosalinde. Sweetest nut hath sourest rind, Such a nut is Rosalinde. He that sweetest rose will find Must find love's prick and Rosalinde. This is the very false gallop of verses; why do you infect yourself with them?
lines: For a taste:
If a hart do lack a hind,
Let him seek out Rosalinde.
If the cat will after kind,
So be sure will Rosalinde.
Winter garments must be lin'd,
So must slender Rosalinde.
They that reap must sheaf and bind,
Then to cart with Rosalinde.
Sweetest nut hath sourest rind,
Such a nut is Rosalinde.
He that sweetest rose will find
Must find love's prick and Rosalinde.
This is the very false gallop of verses; why do you infect
yourself with them?

speeches.speech: Peace, you dull fool! I found them on a tree.
lines: Peace, you dull fool! I found them on a tree.

speeches.speech: Truly, the tree yields bad fruit.
lines: Truly, the tree yields bad fruit.

speeches.speech: I'll graff it with you, and then I shall graff it with a medlar. Then it will be the earliest fruit i' th' country; for you'll be rotten ere you be half ripe, and that's the right virtue of the medlar.
lines: I'll graff it with you, and then I shall graff it with a
medlar. Then it will be the earliest fruit i' th' country; for
you'll be rotten ere you be half ripe, and that's the right
virtue of the medlar.

speeches.speech: You have said; but whether wisely or no, let the forest judge. Enter CELIA, with a writing
lines: You have said; but whether wisely or no, let the forest
judge.
Enter CELIA, with a writing

speeches.speech: Peace! Here comes my sister, reading; stand aside.
lines: Peace!
Here comes my sister, reading; stand aside.

speeches.speech: 'Why should this a desert be? For it is unpeopled? No; Tongues I'll hang on every tree That shall civil sayings show. Some, how brief the life of man Runs his erring pilgrimage, That the streching of a span Buckles in his sum of age; Some, of violated vows 'Twixt the souls of friend and friend; But upon the fairest boughs, Or at every sentence end, Will I Rosalinda write, Teaching all that read to know The quintessence of every sprite Heaven would in little show. Therefore heaven Nature charg'd That one body should be fill'd With all graces wide-enlarg'd. Nature presently distill'd Helen's cheek, but not her heart, Cleopatra's majesty, Atalanta's better part, Sad Lucretia's modesty. Thus Rosalinde of many parts By heavenly synod was devis'd, Of many faces, eyes, and hearts, To have the touches dearest priz'd. Heaven would that she these gifts should have, And I to live and die her slave.'
lines: 'Why should this a desert be?
For it is unpeopled? No;
Tongues I'll hang on every tree
That shall civil sayings show.
Some, how brief the life of man
Runs his erring pilgrimage,
That the streching of a span
Buckles in his sum of age;
Some, of violated vows
'Twixt the souls of friend and friend;
But upon the fairest boughs,
Or at every sentence end,
Will I Rosalinda write,
Teaching all that read to know
The quintessence of every sprite
Heaven would in little show.
Therefore heaven Nature charg'd
That one body should be fill'd
With all graces wide-enlarg'd.
Nature presently distill'd
Helen's cheek, but not her heart,
Cleopatra's majesty,
Atalanta's better part,
Sad Lucretia's modesty.
Thus Rosalinde of many parts
By heavenly synod was devis'd,
Of many faces, eyes, and hearts,
To have the touches dearest priz'd.
Heaven would that she these gifts should have,
And I to live and die her slave.'

speeches.speech: O most gentle Jupiter! What tedious homily of love have you wearied your parishioners withal, and never cried 'Have patience, good people.'
lines: O most gentle Jupiter! What tedious homily of love have
you wearied your parishioners withal, and never cried 'Have
patience, good people.'

speeches.speech: How now! Back, friends; shepherd, go off a little; go with him, sirrah.
lines: How now! Back, friends; shepherd, go off a little; go with
him, sirrah.

speeches.speech: Come, shepherd, let us make an honourable retreat; though not with bag and baggage, yet with scrip and scrippage.
lines: Come, shepherd, let us make an honourable retreat;
though not with bag and baggage, yet with scrip and scrippage.

speeches.speech: Didst thou hear these verses?
lines: Didst thou hear these verses?

speeches.speech: O, yes, I heard them all, and more too; for some of them had in them more feet than the verses would bear.
lines: O, yes, I heard them all, and more too; for some of them
had in them more feet than the verses would bear.

speeches.speech: That's no matter; the feet might bear the verses.
lines: That's no matter; the feet might bear the verses.

speeches.speech: Ay, but the feet were lame, and could not bear themselves without the verse, and therefore stood lamely in the verse.
lines: Ay, but the feet were lame, and could not bear themselves
without the verse, and therefore stood lamely in the verse.

speeches.speech: But didst thou hear without wondering how thy name should be hang'd and carved upon these trees?
lines: But didst thou hear without wondering how thy name should be
hang'd and carved upon these trees?

speeches.speech: I was seven of the nine days out of the wonder before you came; for look here what I found on a palm-tree. I was never so berhym'd since Pythagoras' time that I was an Irish rat, which I can hardly remember.
lines: I was seven of the nine days out of the wonder before you
came; for look here what I found on a palm-tree. I was never so
berhym'd since Pythagoras' time that I was an Irish rat, which I
can hardly remember.

speeches.speech: Trow you who hath done this?
lines: Trow you who hath done this?

speeches.speech: Is it a man?
lines: Is it a man?

speeches.speech: And a chain, that you once wore, about his neck. Change you colour?
lines: And a chain, that you once wore, about his neck.
Change you colour?

speeches.speech: I prithee, who?
lines: I prithee, who?

speeches.speech: O Lord, Lord! it is a hard matter for friends to meet; but mountains may be remov'd with earthquakes, and so encounter.
lines: O Lord, Lord! it is a hard matter for friends to meet; but
mountains may be remov'd with earthquakes, and so encounter.

speeches.speech: Nay, but who is it?
lines: Nay, but who is it?

speeches.speech: Is it possible?
lines: Is it possible?

speeches.speech: Nay, I prithee now, with most petitionary vehemence, tell me who it is.
lines: Nay, I prithee now, with most petitionary vehemence, tell
me who it is.

speeches.speech: O wonderful, wonderful, most wonderful wonderful, and yet again wonderful, and after that, out of all whooping!
lines: O wonderful, wonderful, most wonderful wonderful, and yet
again wonderful, and after that, out of all whooping!

speeches.speech: Good my complexion! dost thou think, though I am caparison'd like a man, I have a doublet and hose in my disposition? One inch of delay more is a South Sea of discovery. I prithee tell me who is it quickly, and speak apace. I would thou could'st stammer, that thou mightst pour this conceal'd man out of thy mouth, as wine comes out of narrow-mouth'd bottle- either too much at once or none at all. I prithee take the cork out of thy mouth that I may drink thy tidings.
lines: Good my complexion! dost thou think, though I am
caparison'd like a man, I have a doublet and hose in my
disposition? One inch of delay more is a South Sea of discovery.
I prithee tell me who is it quickly, and speak apace. I would
thou could'st stammer, that thou mightst pour this conceal'd man
out of thy mouth, as wine comes out of narrow-mouth'd bottle-
either too much at once or none at all. I prithee take the cork
out of thy mouth that I may drink thy tidings.

speeches.speech: So you may put a man in your belly.
lines: So you may put a man in your belly.

speeches.speech: Is he of God's making? What manner of man? Is his head worth a hat or his chin worth a beard?
lines: Is he of God's making? What manner of man?
Is his head worth a hat or his chin worth a beard?

speeches.speech: Nay, he hath but a little beard.
lines: Nay, he hath but a little beard.

speeches.speech: Why, God will send more if the man will be thankful. Let me stay the growth of his beard, if thou delay me not the knowledge of his chin.
lines: Why, God will send more if the man will be thankful. Let
me stay the growth of his beard, if thou delay me not the
knowledge of his chin.

speeches.speech: It is young Orlando, that tripp'd up the wrestler's heels and your heart both in an instant.
lines: It is young Orlando, that tripp'd up the wrestler's heels
and your heart both in an instant.

speeches.speech: Nay, but the devil take mocking! Speak sad brow and true maid.
lines: Nay, but the devil take mocking! Speak sad brow and true
maid.

speeches.speech: I' faith, coz, 'tis he.
lines: I' faith, coz, 'tis he.

speeches.speech: Orlando?
lines: Orlando?

speeches.speech: Orlando.
lines: Orlando.

speeches.speech: Alas the day! what shall I do with my doublet and hose? What did he when thou saw'st him? What said he? How look'd he? Wherein went he? What makes he here? Did he ask for me? Where remains he? How parted he with thee? And when shalt thou see him again? Answer me in one word.
lines: Alas the day! what shall I do with my doublet and hose?
What did he when thou saw'st him? What said he? How look'd he?
Wherein went he? What makes he here? Did he ask for me? Where
remains he? How parted he with thee? And when shalt thou see him
again? Answer me in one word.

speeches.speech: You must borrow me Gargantua's mouth first; 'tis a word too great for any mouth of this age's size. To say ay and no to these particulars is more than to answer in a catechism.
lines: You must borrow me Gargantua's mouth first; 'tis a word too
great for any mouth of this age's size. To say ay and no to these
particulars is more than to answer in a catechism.

speeches.speech: But doth he know that I am in this forest, and in man's apparel? Looks he as freshly as he did the day he wrestled?
lines: But doth he know that I am in this forest, and in man's
apparel? Looks he as freshly as he did the day he wrestled?

speeches.speech: It is as easy to count atomies as to resolve the propositions of a lover; but take a taste of my finding him, and relish it with good observance. I found him under a tree, like a dropp'd acorn.
lines: It is as easy to count atomies as to resolve the
propositions of a lover; but take a taste of my finding him, and
relish it with good observance. I found him under a tree, like a
dropp'd acorn.

speeches.speech: It may well be call'd Jove's tree, when it drops forth such fruit.
lines: It may well be call'd Jove's tree, when it drops forth
such fruit.

speeches.speech: Give me audience, good madam.
lines: Give me audience, good madam.

speeches.speech: Proceed.
lines: Proceed.

speeches.speech: There lay he, stretch'd along like a wounded knight.
lines: There lay he, stretch'd along like a wounded knight.

speeches.speech: Though it be pity to see such a sight, it well becomes the ground.
lines: Though it be pity to see such a sight, it well becomes
the ground.

speeches.speech: Cry 'Holla' to thy tongue, I prithee; it curvets unseasonably. He was furnish'd like a hunter.
lines: Cry 'Holla' to thy tongue, I prithee; it curvets
unseasonably. He was furnish'd like a hunter.

speeches.speech: O, ominous! he comes to kill my heart.
lines: O, ominous! he comes to kill my heart.

speeches.speech: I would sing my song without a burden; thou bring'st me out of tune.
lines: I would sing my song without a burden; thou bring'st me out
of tune.

speeches.speech: Do you not know I am a woman? When I think, I must speak. Sweet, say on.
lines: Do you not know I am a woman? When I think, I must speak.
Sweet, say on.

speeches.speech: You bring me out. Soft! comes he not here?
lines: You bring me out. Soft! comes he not here?

speeches.speech: 'Tis he; slink by, and note him.
lines: 'Tis he; slink by, and note him.

speeches.speech: I thank you for your company; but, good faith, I had as lief have been myself alone.
lines: I thank you for your company; but, good faith, I had as
lief have been myself alone.

speeches.speech: And so had I; but yet, for fashion sake, I thank you too for your society.
lines: And so had I; but yet, for fashion sake, I thank you too
for your society.

speeches.speech: God buy you; let's meet as little as we can.
lines: God buy you; let's meet as little as we can.

speeches.speech: I do desire we may be better strangers.
lines: I do desire we may be better strangers.

speeches.speech: I pray you mar no more trees with writing love songs in their barks.
lines: I pray you mar no more trees with writing love songs in
their barks.

speeches.speech: I pray you mar no more of my verses with reading them ill-favouredly.
lines: I pray you mar no more of my verses with reading them
ill-favouredly.

speeches.speech: Rosalind is your love's name?
lines: Rosalind is your love's name?

speeches.speech: Yes, just.
lines: Yes, just.

speeches.speech: I do not like her name.
lines: I do not like her name.

speeches.speech: There was no thought of pleasing you when she was christen'd.
lines: There was no thought of pleasing you when she was
christen'd.

speeches.speech: What stature is she of?
lines: What stature is she of?

speeches.speech: Just as high as my heart.
lines: Just as high as my heart.

speeches.speech: You are full of pretty answers. Have you not been acquainted with goldsmiths' wives, and conn'd them out of rings?
lines: You are full of pretty answers. Have you not been
acquainted with goldsmiths' wives, and conn'd them out of rings?

speeches.speech: Not so; but I answer you right painted cloth, from whence you have studied your questions.
lines: Not so; but I answer you right painted cloth, from whence
you have studied your questions.

speeches.speech: You have a nimble wit; I think 'twas made of Atalanta's heels. Will you sit down with me? and we two will rail against our mistress the world, and all our misery.
lines: You have a nimble wit; I think 'twas made of Atalanta's
heels. Will you sit down with me? and we two will rail against
our mistress the world, and all our misery.

speeches.speech: I will chide no breather in the world but myself, against whom I know most faults.
lines: I will chide no breather in the world but myself, against
whom I know most faults.

speeches.speech: The worst fault you have is to be in love.
lines: The worst fault you have is to be in love.

speeches.speech: 'Tis a fault I will not change for your best virtue. I am weary of you.
lines: 'Tis a fault I will not change for your best virtue. I am
weary of you.

speeches.speech: By my troth, I was seeking for a fool when I found you.
lines: By my troth, I was seeking for a fool when I found you.

speeches.speech: He is drown'd in the brook; look but in, and you shall see him.
lines: He is drown'd in the brook; look but in, and you shall see
him.

speeches.speech: There I shall see mine own figure.
lines: There I shall see mine own figure.

speeches.speech: Which I take to be either a fool or a cipher.
lines: Which I take to be either a fool or a cipher.

speeches.speech: I'll tarry no longer with you; farewell, good Signior Love.
lines: I'll tarry no longer with you; farewell, good Signior Love.

speeches.speech: I am glad of your departure; adieu, good Monsieur Melancholy.
lines: I am glad of your departure; adieu, good Monsieur
Melancholy.

speeches.speech: [Aside to CELIA] I will speak to him like a saucy lackey, and under that habit play the knave with him.- Do you hear, forester?
lines: [Aside to CELIA] I will speak to him like a saucy lackey,
and under that habit play the knave with him.- Do you hear,
forester?

speeches.speech: Very well; what would you?
lines: Very well; what would you?

speeches.speech: I pray you, what is't o'clock?
lines: I pray you, what is't o'clock?

speeches.speech: You should ask me what time o' day; there's no clock in the forest.
lines: You should ask me what time o' day; there's no clock in
the forest.

speeches.speech: Then there is no true lover in the forest, else sighing every minute and groaning every hour would detect the lazy foot of Time as well as a clock.
lines: Then there is no true lover in the forest, else sighing
every minute and groaning every hour would detect the lazy foot
of Time as well as a clock.

speeches.speech: And why not the swift foot of Time? Had not that been as proper?
lines: And why not the swift foot of Time? Had not that been as
proper?

speeches.speech: By no means, sir. Time travels in divers paces with divers persons. I'll tell you who Time ambles withal, who Time trots withal, who Time gallops withal, and who he stands still withal.
lines: By no means, sir. Time travels in divers paces with
divers persons. I'll tell you who Time ambles withal, who Time
trots withal, who Time gallops withal, and who he stands still
withal.

speeches.speech: I prithee, who doth he trot withal?
lines: I prithee, who doth he trot withal?

speeches.speech: Marry, he trots hard with a young maid between the contract of her marriage and the day it is solemniz'd; if the interim be but a se'nnight, Time's pace is so hard that it seems the length of seven year.
lines: Marry, he trots hard with a young maid between the
contract of her marriage and the day it is solemniz'd; if the
interim be but a se'nnight, Time's pace is so hard that it seems
the length of seven year.

speeches.speech: Who ambles Time withal?
lines: Who ambles Time withal?

speeches.speech: With a priest that lacks Latin and a rich man that hath not the gout; for the one sleeps easily because he cannot study, and the other lives merrily because he feels no pain; the one lacking the burden of lean and wasteful learning, the other knowing no burden of heavy tedious penury. These Time ambles withal.
lines: With a priest that lacks Latin and a rich man that hath
not the gout; for the one sleeps easily because he cannot study,
and the other lives merrily because he feels no pain; the one
lacking the burden of lean and wasteful learning, the other
knowing no burden of heavy tedious penury. These Time ambles
withal.

speeches.speech: Who doth he gallop withal?
lines: Who doth he gallop withal?

speeches.speech: With a thief to the gallows; for though he go as softly as foot can fall, he thinks himself too soon there.
lines: With a thief to the gallows; for though he go as softly
as foot can fall, he thinks himself too soon there.

speeches.speech: Who stays it still withal?
lines: Who stays it still withal?

speeches.speech: With lawyers in the vacation; for they sleep between term and term, and then they perceive not how Time moves.
lines: With lawyers in the vacation; for they sleep between term
and term, and then they perceive not how Time moves.

speeches.speech: Where dwell you, pretty youth?
lines: Where dwell you, pretty youth?

speeches.speech: With this shepherdess, my sister; here in the skirts of the forest, like fringe upon a petticoat.
lines: With this shepherdess, my sister; here in the skirts of
the forest, like fringe upon a petticoat.

speeches.speech: Are you native of this place?
lines: Are you native of this place?

speeches.speech: As the coney that you see dwell where she is kindled.
lines: As the coney that you see dwell where she is kindled.

speeches.speech: Your accent is something finer than you could purchase in so removed a dwelling.
lines: Your accent is something finer than you could purchase in
so removed a dwelling.

speeches.speech: I have been told so of many; but indeed an old religious uncle of mine taught me to speak, who was in his youth an inland man; one that knew courtship too well, for there he fell in love. I have heard him read many lectures against it; and I thank God I am not a woman, to be touch'd with so many giddy offences as he hath generally tax'd their whole sex withal.
lines: I have been told so of many; but indeed an old religious
uncle of mine taught me to speak, who was in his youth an inland
man; one that knew courtship too well, for there he fell in love.
I have heard him read many lectures against it; and I thank God I
am not a woman, to be touch'd with so many giddy offences as he
hath generally tax'd their whole sex withal.

speeches.speech: Can you remember any of the principal evils that he laid to the charge of women?
lines: Can you remember any of the principal evils that he laid
to the charge of women?

speeches.speech: There were none principal; they were all like one another as halfpence are; every one fault seeming monstrous till his fellow-fault came to match it.
lines: There were none principal; they were all like one another
as halfpence are; every one fault seeming monstrous till his
fellow-fault came to match it.

speeches.speech: I prithee recount some of them.
lines: I prithee recount some of them.

speeches.speech: No; I will not cast away my physic but on those that are sick. There is a man haunts the forest that abuses our young plants with carving 'Rosalind' on their barks; hangs odes upon hawthorns and elegies on brambles; all, forsooth, deifying the name of Rosalind. If I could meet that fancy-monger, I would give him some good counsel, for he seems to have the quotidian of love upon him.
lines: No; I will not cast away my physic but on those that are
sick. There is a man haunts the forest that abuses our young
plants with carving 'Rosalind' on their barks; hangs odes upon
hawthorns and elegies on brambles; all, forsooth, deifying the
name of Rosalind. If I could meet that fancy-monger, I would give
him some good counsel, for he seems to have the quotidian of love
upon him.

speeches.speech: I am he that is so love-shak'd; I pray you tell me your remedy.
lines: I am he that is so love-shak'd; I pray you tell me your
remedy.

speeches.speech: There is none of my uncle's marks upon you; he taught me how to know a man in love; in which cage of rushes I am sure you are not prisoner.
lines: There is none of my uncle's marks upon you; he taught me
how to know a man in love; in which cage of rushes I am sure you
are not prisoner.

speeches.speech: What were his marks?
lines: What were his marks?

speeches.speech: A lean cheek, which you have not; a blue eye and sunken, which you have not; an unquestionable spirit, which you have not; a beard neglected, which you have not; but I pardon you for that, for simply your having in beard is a younger brother's revenue. Then your hose should be ungarter'd, your bonnet unbanded, your sleeve unbutton'd, your shoe untied, and every thing about you demonstrating a careless desolation. But you are no such man; you are rather point-device in your accoutrements, as loving yourself than seeming the lover of any other.
lines: A lean cheek, which you have not; a blue eye and sunken,
which you have not; an unquestionable spirit, which you have not;
a beard neglected, which you have not; but I pardon you for that,
for simply your having in beard is a younger brother's revenue.
Then your hose should be ungarter'd, your bonnet unbanded, your
sleeve unbutton'd, your shoe untied, and every thing about you
demonstrating a careless desolation. But you are no such man; you
are rather point-device in your accoutrements, as loving yourself
than seeming the lover of any other.

speeches.speech: Fair youth, I would I could make thee believe I love.
lines: Fair youth, I would I could make thee believe I love.

speeches.speech: Me believe it! You may as soon make her that you love believe it; which, I warrant, she is apter to do than to confess she does. That is one of the points in the which women still give the lie to their consciences. But, in good sooth, are you he that hangs the verses on the trees wherein Rosalind is so admired?
lines: Me believe it! You may as soon make her that you love
believe it; which, I warrant, she is apter to do than to confess
she does. That is one of the points in the which women still give
the lie to their consciences. But, in good sooth, are you he that
hangs the verses on the trees wherein Rosalind is so admired?

speeches.speech: I swear to thee, youth, by the white hand of Rosalind, I am that he, that unfortunate he.
lines: I swear to thee, youth, by the white hand of Rosalind, I
am that he, that unfortunate he.

speeches.speech: But are you so much in love as your rhymes speak?
lines: But are you so much in love as your rhymes speak?

speeches.speech: Neither rhyme nor reason can express how much.
lines: Neither rhyme nor reason can express how much.

speeches.speech: Love is merely a madness; and, I tell you, deserves as well a dark house and a whip as madmen do; and the reason why they are not so punish'd and cured is that the lunacy is so ordinary that the whippers are in love too. Yet I profess curing it by counsel.
lines: Love is merely a madness; and, I tell you, deserves as
well a dark house and a whip as madmen do; and the reason why
they are not so punish'd and cured is that the lunacy is so
ordinary that the whippers are in love too. Yet I profess curing
it by counsel.

speeches.speech: Did you ever cure any so?
lines: Did you ever cure any so?

speeches.speech: Yes, one; and in this manner. He was to imagine me his love, his mistress; and I set him every day to woo me; at which time would I, being but a moonish youth, grieve, be effeminate, changeable, longing and liking, proud, fantastical, apish, shallow, inconstant, full of tears, full of smiles; for every passion something and for no passion truly anything, as boys and women are for the most part cattle of this colour; would now like him, now loathe him; then entertain him, then forswear him; now weep for him, then spit at him; that I drave my suitor from his mad humour of love to a living humour of madness; which was, to forswear the full stream of the world and to live in a nook merely monastic. And thus I cur'd him; and this way will I take upon me to wash your liver as clean as a sound sheep's heart, that there shall not be one spot of love in 't.
lines: Yes, one; and in this manner. He was to imagine me his
love, his mistress; and I set him every day to woo me; at which
time would I, being but a moonish youth, grieve, be effeminate,
changeable, longing and liking, proud, fantastical, apish,
shallow, inconstant, full of tears, full of smiles; for every
passion something and for no passion truly anything, as boys and
women are for the most part cattle of this colour; would now like
him, now loathe him; then entertain him, then forswear him; now
weep for him, then spit at him; that I drave my suitor from his
mad humour of love to a living humour of madness; which was, to
forswear the full stream of the world and to live in a nook
merely monastic. And thus I cur'd him; and this way will I take
upon me to wash your liver as clean as a sound sheep's heart,
that there shall not be one spot of love in 't.

speeches.speech: I would not be cured, youth.
lines: I would not be cured, youth.

speeches.speech: I would cure you, if you would but call me Rosalind, and come every day to my cote and woo me.
lines: I would cure you, if you would but call me Rosalind, and
come every day to my cote and woo me.

speeches.speech: Now, by the faith of my love, I will. Tell me where it is.
lines: Now, by the faith of my love, I will. Tell me where it is.

speeches.speech: Go with me to it, and I'll show it you; and, by the way, you shall tell me where in the forest you live. Will you go?
lines: Go with me to it, and I'll show it you; and, by the way,
you shall tell me where in the forest you live. Will you go?

speeches.speech: With all my heart, good youth.
lines: With all my heart, good youth.

speeches.speech: Nay, you must call me Rosalind. Come, sister, will you go?                                                   Exeunt
lines: Nay, you must call me Rosalind. Come, sister, will you
go?                                                   Exeunt

speeches.speech: Come apace, good Audrey; I will fetch up your goats, Audrey. And how, Audrey, am I the man yet? Doth my simple feature content you?
lines: Come apace, good Audrey; I will fetch up your goats,
Audrey. And how, Audrey, am I the man yet? Doth my simple feature
content you?

speeches.speech: Your features! Lord warrant us! What features?
lines: Your features! Lord warrant us! What features?

speeches.speech: I am here with thee and thy goats, as the most capricious poet, honest Ovid, was among the Goths.
lines: I am here with thee and thy goats, as the most
capricious poet, honest Ovid, was among the Goths.

speeches.speech: [Aside] O knowledge ill-inhabited, worse than Jove in a thatch'd house!
lines: [Aside] O knowledge ill-inhabited, worse than Jove in a
thatch'd house!

speeches.speech: When a man's verses cannot be understood, nor a man's good wit seconded with the forward child understanding, it strikes a man more dead than a great reckoning in a little room. Truly, I would the gods had made thee poetical.
lines: When a man's verses cannot be understood, nor a man's
good wit seconded with the forward child understanding, it
strikes a man more dead than a great reckoning in a little room.
Truly, I would the gods had made thee poetical.

speeches.speech: I do not know what 'poetical' is. Is it honest in deed and word? Is it a true thing?
lines: I do not know what 'poetical' is. Is it honest in deed and
word? Is it a true thing?

speeches.speech: No, truly; for the truest poetry is the most feigning, and lovers are given to poetry; and what they swear in poetry may be said as lovers they do feign.
lines: No, truly; for the truest poetry is the most feigning,
and lovers are given to poetry; and what they swear in poetry may
be said as lovers they do feign.

speeches.speech: Do you wish, then, that the gods had made me poetical?
lines: Do you wish, then, that the gods had made me poetical?

speeches.speech: I do, truly, for thou swear'st to me thou art honest; now, if thou wert a poet, I might have some hope thou didst feign.
lines: I do, truly, for thou swear'st to me thou art honest;
now, if thou wert a poet, I might have some hope thou didst
feign.

speeches.speech: Would you not have me honest?
lines: Would you not have me honest?

speeches.speech: No, truly, unless thou wert hard-favour'd; for honesty coupled to beauty is to have honey a sauce to sugar.
lines: No, truly, unless thou wert hard-favour'd; for honesty
coupled to beauty is to have honey a sauce to sugar.

speeches.speech: [Aside] A material fool!
lines: [Aside] A material fool!

speeches.speech: Well, I am not fair; and therefore I pray the gods make me honest.
lines: Well, I am not fair; and therefore I pray the gods make me
honest.

speeches.speech: Truly, and to cast away honesty upon a foul slut were to put good meat into an unclean dish.
lines: Truly, and to cast away honesty upon a foul slut were
to put good meat into an unclean dish.

speeches.speech: I am not a slut, though I thank the gods I am foul.
lines: I am not a slut, though I thank the gods I am foul.

speeches.speech: Well, praised be the gods for thy foulness; sluttishness may come hereafter. But be it as it may be, I will marry thee; and to that end I have been with Sir Oliver Martext, the vicar of the next village, who hath promis'd to meet me in this place of the forest, and to couple us.
lines: Well, praised be the gods for thy foulness;
sluttishness may come hereafter. But be it as it may be, I will
marry thee; and to that end I have been with Sir Oliver Martext,
the vicar of the next village, who hath promis'd to meet me in
this place of the forest, and to couple us.

speeches.speech: [Aside] I would fain see this meeting.
lines: [Aside] I would fain see this meeting.

speeches.speech: Well, the gods give us joy!
lines: Well, the gods give us joy!

speeches.speech: Amen. A man may, if he were of a fearful heart, stagger in this attempt; for here we have no temple but the wood, no assembly but horn-beasts. But what though? Courage! As horns are odious, they are necessary. It is said: 'Many a man knows no end of his goods.' Right! Many a man has good horns and knows no end of them. Well, that is the dowry of his wife; 'tis none of his own getting. Horns? Even so. Poor men alone? No, no; the noblest deer hath them as huge as the rascal. Is the single man therefore blessed? No; as a wall'd town is more worthier than a village, so is the forehead of a married man more honourable than the bare brow of a bachelor; and by how much defence is better than no skill, by so much is horn more precious than to want. Here comes Sir Oliver. [Enter SIR OLIVER MARTEXT] Sir Oliver Martext, you are well met. Will you dispatch us here under this tree, or shall we go with you to your chapel?
lines: Amen. A man may, if he were of a fearful heart, stagger
in this attempt; for here we have no temple but the wood, no
assembly but horn-beasts. But what though? Courage! As horns are
odious, they are necessary. It is said: 'Many a man knows no end
of his goods.' Right! Many a man has good horns and knows no end
of them. Well, that is the dowry of his wife; 'tis none of his
own getting. Horns? Even so. Poor men alone? No, no; the noblest
deer hath them as huge as the rascal. Is the single man therefore
blessed? No; as a wall'd town is more worthier than a village, so
is the forehead of a married man more honourable than the bare
brow of a bachelor; and by how much defence is better than no
skill, by so much is horn more precious than to want. Here comes
Sir Oliver.
[Enter SIR OLIVER MARTEXT]
Sir Oliver Martext, you are well met. Will you dispatch us here
under this tree, or shall we go with you to your chapel?

speeches.speech: Is there none here to give the woman?
lines: Is there none here to give the woman?

speeches.speech: I will not take her on gift of any man.
lines: I will not take her on gift of any man.

speeches.speech: Truly, she must be given, or the marriage is not lawful.
lines: Truly, she must be given, or the marriage is not lawful.

speeches.speech: [Discovering himself] Proceed, proceed; I'll give her.
lines: [Discovering himself] Proceed, proceed; I'll give her.

speeches.speech: Good even, good Master What-ye-call't; how do you, sir? You are very well met. Goddild you for your last company. I am very glad to see you. Even a toy in hand here, sir. Nay; pray be cover'd.
lines: Good even, good Master What-ye-call't; how do you, sir?
You are very well met. Goddild you for your last company. I am
very glad to see you. Even a toy in hand here, sir. Nay; pray be
cover'd.

speeches.speech: Will you be married, motley?
lines: Will you be married, motley?

speeches.speech: As the ox hath his bow, sir, the horse his curb, and the falcon her bells, so man hath his desires; and as pigeons bill, so wedlock would be nibbling.
lines: As the ox hath his bow, sir, the horse his curb, and
the falcon her bells, so man hath his desires; and as pigeons
bill, so wedlock would be nibbling.

speeches.speech: And will you, being a man of your breeding, be married under a bush, like a beggar? Get you to church and have a good priest that can tell you what marriage is; this fellow will but join you together as they join wainscot; then one of you will prove a shrunk panel, and like green timber warp, warp.
lines: And will you, being a man of your breeding, be married
under a bush, like a beggar? Get you to church and have a good
priest that can tell you what marriage is; this fellow will but
join you together as they join wainscot; then one of you will
prove a shrunk panel, and like green timber warp, warp.

speeches.speech: [Aside] I am not in the mind but I were better to be married of him than of another; for he is not like to marry me well; and not being well married, it will be a good excuse for me hereafter to leave my wife.
lines: [Aside] I am not in the mind but I were better to be
married of him than of another; for he is not like to marry me
well; and not being well married, it will be a good excuse for me
hereafter to leave my wife.

speeches.speech: Go thou with me, and let me counsel thee.
lines: Go thou with me, and let me counsel thee.

speeches.speech: Come, sweet Audrey; We must be married or we must live in bawdry. Farewell, good Master Oliver. Not- O sweet Oliver, O brave Oliver, Leave me not behind thee. But- Wind away, Begone, I say, I will not to wedding with thee. Exeunt JAQUES, TOUCHSTONE, and AUDREY
lines: Come, sweet Audrey;
We must be married or we must live in bawdry.
Farewell, good Master Oliver. Not-
O sweet Oliver,
O brave Oliver,
Leave me not behind thee.
But-
Wind away,
Begone, I say,
I will not to wedding with thee.
Exeunt JAQUES, TOUCHSTONE, and AUDREY

speeches.speech: 'Tis no matter; ne'er a fantastical knave of them all shall flout me out of my calling.                  Exit
lines: 'Tis no matter; ne'er a fantastical knave of them all
shall flout me out of my calling.                  Exit

speeches.speech: Never talk to me; I will weep.
lines: Never talk to me; I will weep.

speeches.speech: Do, I prithee; but yet have the grace to consider that tears do not become a man.
lines: Do, I prithee; but yet have the grace to consider that tears
do not become a man.

speeches.speech: But have I not cause to weep?
lines: But have I not cause to weep?

speeches.speech: As good cause as one would desire; therefore weep.
lines: As good cause as one would desire; therefore weep.

speeches.speech: His very hair is of the dissembling colour.
lines: His very hair is of the dissembling colour.

speeches.speech: Something browner than Judas's. Marry, his kisses are Judas's own children.
lines: Something browner than Judas's.
Marry, his kisses are Judas's own children.

speeches.speech: I' faith, his hair is of a good colour.
lines: I' faith, his hair is of a good colour.

speeches.speech: An excellent colour: your chestnut was ever the only colour.
lines: An excellent colour: your chestnut was ever the only colour.

speeches.speech: And his kissing is as full of sanctity as the touch of holy bread.
lines: And his kissing is as full of sanctity as the touch of
holy bread.

speeches.speech: He hath bought a pair of cast lips of Diana. A nun of winter's sisterhood kisses not more religiously; the very ice of chastity is in them.
lines: He hath bought a pair of cast lips of Diana. A nun of
winter's sisterhood kisses not more religiously; the very ice of
chastity is in them.

speeches.speech: But why did he swear he would come this morning, and comes not?
lines: But why did he swear he would come this morning, and
comes not?

speeches.speech: Nay, certainly, there is no truth in him.
lines: Nay, certainly, there is no truth in him.

speeches.speech: Do you think so?
lines: Do you think so?

speeches.speech: Yes; I think he is not a pick-purse nor a horse-stealer; but for his verity in love, I do think him as concave as covered goblet or a worm-eaten nut.
lines: Yes; I think he is not a pick-purse nor a horse-stealer; but
for his verity in love, I do think him as concave as covered
goblet or a worm-eaten nut.

speeches.speech: Not true in love?
lines: Not true in love?

speeches.speech: Yes, when he is in; but I think he is not in.
lines: Yes, when he is in; but I think he is not in.

speeches.speech: You have heard him swear downright he was.
lines: You have heard him swear downright he was.

speeches.speech: 'Was' is not 'is'; besides, the oath of a lover is no stronger than the word of a tapster; they are both the confirmer of false reckonings. He attends here in the forest on the Duke, your father.
lines: 'Was' is not 'is'; besides, the oath of a lover is no
stronger than the word of a tapster; they are both the confirmer
of false reckonings. He attends here in the forest on the Duke,
your father.

speeches.speech: I met the Duke yesterday, and had much question with him. He asked me of what parentage I was; I told him, of as good as he; so he laugh'd and let me go. But what talk we of fathers when there is such a man as Orlando?
lines: I met the Duke yesterday, and had much question with him.
He asked me of what parentage I was; I told him, of as good as
he; so he laugh'd and let me go. But what talk we of fathers when
there is such a man as Orlando?

speeches.speech: O, that's a brave man! He writes brave verses, speaks brave words, swears brave oaths, and breaks them bravely, quite traverse, athwart the heart of his lover; as a puny tilter, that spurs his horse but on one side, breaks his staff like a noble goose. But all's brave that youth mounts and folly guides. Who comes here?
lines: O, that's a brave man! He writes brave verses, speaks brave
words, swears brave oaths, and breaks them bravely, quite
traverse, athwart the heart of his lover; as a puny tilter, that
spurs his horse but on one side, breaks his staff like a noble
goose. But all's brave that youth mounts and folly guides. Who
comes here?

speeches.speech: Mistress and master, you have oft enquired After the shepherd that complain'd of love, Who you saw sitting by me on the turf, Praising the proud disdainful shepherdess That was his mistress.
lines: Mistress and master, you have oft enquired
After the shepherd that complain'd of love,
Who you saw sitting by me on the turf,
Praising the proud disdainful shepherdess
That was his mistress.

speeches.speech: Well, and what of him?
lines: Well, and what of him?

speeches.speech: If you will see a pageant truly play'd Between the pale complexion of true love And the red glow of scorn and proud disdain, Go hence a little, and I shall conduct you, If you will mark it.
lines: If you will see a pageant truly play'd
Between the pale complexion of true love
And the red glow of scorn and proud disdain,
Go hence a little, and I shall conduct you,
If you will mark it.

speeches.speech: O, come, let us remove! The sight of lovers feedeth those in love. Bring us to this sight, and you shall say I'll prove a busy actor in their play.           Exeunt
lines: O, come, let us remove!
The sight of lovers feedeth those in love.
Bring us to this sight, and you shall say
I'll prove a busy actor in their play.           Exeunt

speeches.speech: Sweet Phebe, do not scorn me; do not, Phebe. Say that you love me not; but say not so In bitterness. The common executioner, Whose heart th' accustom'd sight of death makes hard, Falls not the axe upon the humbled neck But first begs pardon. Will you sterner be Than he that dies and lives by bloody drops?
lines: Sweet Phebe, do not scorn me; do not, Phebe.
Say that you love me not; but say not so
In bitterness. The common executioner,
Whose heart th' accustom'd sight of death makes hard,
Falls not the axe upon the humbled neck
But first begs pardon. Will you sterner be
Than he that dies and lives by bloody drops?

speeches.speech: I would not be thy executioner; I fly thee, for I would not injure thee. Thou tell'st me there is murder in mine eye. 'Tis pretty, sure, and very probable, That eyes, that are the frail'st and softest things, Who shut their coward gates on atomies, Should be call'd tyrants, butchers, murderers! Now I do frown on thee with all my heart; And if mine eyes can wound, now let them kill thee. Now counterfeit to swoon; why, now fall down; Or, if thou canst not, O, for shame, for shame, Lie not, to say mine eyes are murderers. Now show the wound mine eye hath made in thee. Scratch thee but with a pin, and there remains Some scar of it; lean upon a rush, The cicatrice and capable impressure Thy palm some moment keeps; but now mine eyes, Which I have darted at thee, hurt thee not; Nor, I am sure, there is not force in eyes That can do hurt.
lines: I would not be thy executioner;
I fly thee, for I would not injure thee.
Thou tell'st me there is murder in mine eye.
'Tis pretty, sure, and very probable,
That eyes, that are the frail'st and softest things,
Who shut their coward gates on atomies,
Should be call'd tyrants, butchers, murderers!
Now I do frown on thee with all my heart;
And if mine eyes can wound, now let them kill thee.
Now counterfeit to swoon; why, now fall down;
Or, if thou canst not, O, for shame, for shame,
Lie not, to say mine eyes are murderers.
Now show the wound mine eye hath made in thee.
Scratch thee but with a pin, and there remains
Some scar of it; lean upon a rush,
The cicatrice and capable impressure
Thy palm some moment keeps; but now mine eyes,
Which I have darted at thee, hurt thee not;
Nor, I am sure, there is not force in eyes
That can do hurt.

speeches.speech: O dear Phebe, If ever- as that ever may be near- You meet in some fresh cheek the power of fancy, Then shall you know the wounds invisible That love's keen arrows make.
lines: O dear Phebe,
If ever- as that ever may be near-
You meet in some fresh cheek the power of fancy,
Then shall you know the wounds invisible
That love's keen arrows make.

speeches.speech: But till that time Come not thou near me; and when that time comes, Afflict me with thy mocks, pity me not; As till that time I shall not pity thee.
lines: But till that time
Come not thou near me; and when that time comes,
Afflict me with thy mocks, pity me not;
As till that time I shall not pity thee.

speeches.speech: [Advancing] And why, I pray you? Who might be your mother, That you insult, exult, and all at once, Over the wretched? What though you have no beauty- As, by my faith, I see no more in you Than without candle may go dark to bed- Must you be therefore proud and pitiless? Why, what means this? Why do you look on me? I see no more in you than in the ordinary Of nature's sale-work. 'Od's my little life, I think she means to tangle my eyes too! No faith, proud mistress, hope not after it; 'Tis not your inky brows, your black silk hair, Your bugle eyeballs, nor your cheek of cream, That can entame my spirits to your worship. You foolish shepherd, wherefore do you follow her, Like foggy south, puffing with wind and rain? You are a thousand times a properer man Than she a woman. 'Tis such fools as you That makes the world full of ill-favour'd children. 'Tis not her glass, but you, that flatters her; And out of you she sees herself more proper Than any of her lineaments can show her. But, mistress, know yourself. Down on your knees, And thank heaven, fasting, for a good man's love; For I must tell you friendly in your ear: Sell when you can; you are not for all markets. Cry the man mercy, love him, take his offer; Foul is most foul, being foul to be a scoffer. So take her to thee, shepherd. Fare you well.
lines: [Advancing] And why, I pray you? Who might be your
mother,
That you insult, exult, and all at once,
Over the wretched? What though you have no beauty-
As, by my faith, I see no more in you
Than without candle may go dark to bed-
Must you be therefore proud and pitiless?
Why, what means this? Why do you look on me?
I see no more in you than in the ordinary
Of nature's sale-work. 'Od's my little life,
I think she means to tangle my eyes too!
No faith, proud mistress, hope not after it;
'Tis not your inky brows, your black silk hair,
Your bugle eyeballs, nor your cheek of cream,
That can entame my spirits to your worship.
You foolish shepherd, wherefore do you follow her,
Like foggy south, puffing with wind and rain?
You are a thousand times a properer man
Than she a woman. 'Tis such fools as you
That makes the world full of ill-favour'd children.
'Tis not her glass, but you, that flatters her;
And out of you she sees herself more proper
Than any of her lineaments can show her.
But, mistress, know yourself. Down on your knees,
And thank heaven, fasting, for a good man's love;
For I must tell you friendly in your ear:
Sell when you can; you are not for all markets.
Cry the man mercy, love him, take his offer;
Foul is most foul, being foul to be a scoffer.
So take her to thee, shepherd. Fare you well.

speeches.speech: Sweet youth, I pray you chide a year together; I had rather hear you chide than this man woo.
lines: Sweet youth, I pray you chide a year together;
I had rather hear you chide than this man woo.

speeches.speech: He's fall'n in love with your foulness, and she'll fall in love with my anger. If it be so, as fast as she answers thee with frowning looks, I'll sauce her with bitter words. Why look you so upon me?
lines: He's fall'n in love with your foulness, and she'll fall
in love with my anger. If it be so, as fast as she answers thee
with frowning looks, I'll sauce her with bitter words. Why look
you so upon me?

speeches.speech: For no ill will I bear you.
lines: For no ill will I bear you.

speeches.speech: I pray you do not fall in love with me, For I am falser than vows made in wine; Besides, I like you not. If you will know my house, 'Tis at the tuft of olives here hard by.
