Will you go, sister? Shepherd, ply her hard. Come, sister. Shepherdess, look on him better, And be not proud; though all the world could see, None could be so abus'd in sight as he. Come, to our flock.   Exeunt ROSALIND, CELIA, and CORIN
lines: I pray you do not fall in love with me,
For I am falser than vows made in wine;
Besides, I like you not. If you will know my house,
'Tis at the tuft of olives here hard by.
Will you go, sister? Shepherd, ply her hard.
Come, sister. Shepherdess, look on him better,
And be not proud; though all the world could see,
None could be so abus'd in sight as he.
Come, to our flock.   Exeunt ROSALIND, CELIA, and CORIN

speeches.speech: Dead shepherd, now I find thy saw of might: 'Who ever lov'd that lov'd not at first sight?'
lines: Dead shepherd, now I find thy saw of might:
'Who ever lov'd that lov'd not at first sight?'

speeches.speech: Sweet Phebe.
lines: Sweet Phebe.

speeches.speech: Ha! what say'st thou, Silvius?
lines: Ha! what say'st thou, Silvius?

speeches.speech: Sweet Phebe, pity me.
lines: Sweet Phebe, pity me.

speeches.speech: Why, I am sorry for thee, gentle Silvius.
lines: Why, I am sorry for thee, gentle Silvius.

speeches.speech: Wherever sorrow is, relief would be. If you do sorrow at my grief in love, By giving love, your sorrow and my grief Were both extermin'd.
lines: Wherever sorrow is, relief would be.
If you do sorrow at my grief in love,
By giving love, your sorrow and my grief
Were both extermin'd.

speeches.speech: Thou hast my love; is not that neighbourly?
lines: Thou hast my love; is not that neighbourly?

speeches.speech: I would have you.
lines: I would have you.

speeches.speech: Why, that were covetousness. Silvius, the time was that I hated thee; And yet it is not that I bear thee love; But since that thou canst talk of love so well, Thy company, which erst was irksome to me, I will endure; and I'll employ thee too. But do not look for further recompense Than thine own gladness that thou art employ'd.
lines: Why, that were covetousness.
Silvius, the time was that I hated thee;
And yet it is not that I bear thee love;
But since that thou canst talk of love so well,
Thy company, which erst was irksome to me,
I will endure; and I'll employ thee too.
But do not look for further recompense
Than thine own gladness that thou art employ'd.

speeches.speech: So holy and so perfect is my love, And I in such a poverty of grace, That I shall think it a most plenteous crop To glean the broken ears after the man That the main harvest reaps; loose now and then A scatt'red smile, and that I'll live upon.
lines: So holy and so perfect is my love,
And I in such a poverty of grace,
That I shall think it a most plenteous crop
To glean the broken ears after the man
That the main harvest reaps; loose now and then
A scatt'red smile, and that I'll live upon.

speeches.speech: Know'st thou the youth that spoke to me erewhile?
lines: Know'st thou the youth that spoke to me erewhile?

speeches.speech: Not very well; but I have met him oft; And he hath bought the cottage and the bounds That the old carlot once was master of.
lines: Not very well; but I have met him oft;
And he hath bought the cottage and the bounds
That the old carlot once was master of.

speeches.speech: Think not I love him, though I ask for him; 'Tis but a peevish boy; yet he talks well. But what care I for words? Yet words do well When he that speaks them pleases those that hear. It is a pretty youth- not very pretty; But, sure, he's proud; and yet his pride becomes him. He'll make a proper man. The best thing in him Is his complexion; and faster than his tongue Did make offence, his eye did heal it up. He is not very tall; yet for his years he's tall; His leg is but so-so; and yet 'tis well. There was a pretty redness in his lip, A little riper and more lusty red Than that mix'd in his cheek; 'twas just the difference Betwixt the constant red and mingled damask. There be some women, Silvius, had they mark'd him In parcels as I did, would have gone near To fall in love with him; but, for my part, I love him not, nor hate him not; and yet I have more cause to hate him than to love him; For what had he to do to chide at me? He said mine eyes were black, and my hair black, And, now I am rememb'red, scorn'd at me. I marvel why I answer'd not again; But that's all one: omittance is no quittance. I'll write to him a very taunting letter, And thou shalt bear it; wilt thou, Silvius?
lines: Think not I love him, though I ask for him;
'Tis but a peevish boy; yet he talks well.
But what care I for words? Yet words do well
When he that speaks them pleases those that hear.
It is a pretty youth- not very pretty;
But, sure, he's proud; and yet his pride becomes him.
He'll make a proper man. The best thing in him
Is his complexion; and faster than his tongue
Did make offence, his eye did heal it up.
He is not very tall; yet for his years he's tall;
His leg is but so-so; and yet 'tis well.
There was a pretty redness in his lip,
A little riper and more lusty red
Than that mix'd in his cheek; 'twas just the difference
Betwixt the constant red and mingled damask.
There be some women, Silvius, had they mark'd him
In parcels as I did, would have gone near
To fall in love with him; but, for my part,
I love him not, nor hate him not; and yet
I have more cause to hate him than to love him;
For what had he to do to chide at me?
He said mine eyes were black, and my hair black,
And, now I am rememb'red, scorn'd at me.
I marvel why I answer'd not again;
But that's all one: omittance is no quittance.
I'll write to him a very taunting letter,
And thou shalt bear it; wilt thou, Silvius?

speeches.speech: Phebe, with all my heart.
lines: Phebe, with all my heart.

speeches.speech: I'll write it straight; The matter's in my head and in my heart; I will be bitter with him and passing short. Go with me, Silvius.                             Exeunt
lines: I'll write it straight;
The matter's in my head and in my heart;
I will be bitter with him and passing short.
Go with me, Silvius.                             Exeunt

speeches.speech: I prithee, pretty youth, let me be better acquainted with thee.
lines: I prithee, pretty youth, let me be better acquainted with
thee.

speeches.speech: They say you are a melancholy fellow.
lines: They say you are a melancholy fellow.

speeches.speech: I am so; I do love it better than laughing.
lines: I am so; I do love it better than laughing.

speeches.speech: Those that are in extremity of either are abominable fellows, and betray themselves to every modern censure worse than drunkards.
lines: Those that are in extremity of either are abominable
fellows, and betray themselves to every modern censure worse than
drunkards.

speeches.speech: Why, 'tis good to be sad and say nothing.
lines: Why, 'tis good to be sad and say nothing.

speeches.speech: Why then, 'tis good to be a post.
lines: Why then, 'tis good to be a post.

speeches.speech: I have neither the scholar's melancholy, which is emulation; nor the musician's, which is fantastical; nor the courtier's, which is proud; nor the soldier's, which is ambitious; nor the lawyer's, which is politic; nor the lady's, which is nice; nor the lover's, which is all these; but it is a melancholy of mine own, compounded of many simples, extracted from many objects, and, indeed, the sundry contemplation of my travels; in which my often rumination wraps me in a most humorous sadness.
lines: I have neither the scholar's melancholy, which is
emulation; nor the musician's, which is fantastical; nor the
courtier's, which is proud; nor the soldier's, which is
ambitious; nor the lawyer's, which is politic; nor the lady's,
which is nice; nor the lover's, which is all these; but it is a
melancholy of mine own, compounded of many simples, extracted
from many objects, and, indeed, the sundry contemplation of my
travels; in which my often rumination wraps me in a most humorous
sadness.

speeches.speech: A traveller! By my faith, you have great reason to be sad. I fear you have sold your own lands to see other men's; then to have seen much and to have nothing is to have rich eyes and poor hands.
lines: A traveller! By my faith, you have great reason to be
sad. I fear you have sold your own lands to see other men's; then
to have seen much and to have nothing is to have rich eyes and
poor hands.

speeches.speech: Yes, I have gain'd my experience.
lines: Yes, I have gain'd my experience.

speeches.speech: And your experience makes you sad. I had rather have a fool to make me merry than experience to make me sad- and to travel for it too.
lines: And your experience makes you sad. I had rather have a
fool to make me merry than experience to make me sad- and to
travel for it too.

speeches.speech: Good day, and happiness, dear Rosalind!
lines: Good day, and happiness, dear Rosalind!

speeches.speech: Nay, then, God buy you, an you talk in blank verse.
lines: Nay, then, God buy you, an you talk in blank verse.

speeches.speech: Farewell, Monsieur Traveller; look you lisp and wear strange suits, disable all the benefits of your own country, be out of love with your nativity, and almost chide God for making you that countenance you are; or I will scarce think you have swam in a gondola. [Exit JAQUES] Why, how now, Orlando! where have you been all this while? You a lover! An you serve me such another trick, never come in my sight more.
lines: Farewell, Monsieur Traveller; look you lisp and wear
strange suits, disable all the benefits of your own country, be
out of love with your nativity, and almost chide God for making
you that countenance you are; or I will scarce think you have
swam in a gondola. [Exit JAQUES] Why, how now, Orlando! where
have you been all this while? You a lover! An you serve me such
another trick, never come in my sight more.

speeches.speech: My fair Rosalind, I come within an hour of my promise.
lines: My fair Rosalind, I come within an hour of my promise.

speeches.speech: Break an hour's promise in love! He that will divide a minute into a thousand parts, and break but a part of the thousand part of a minute in the affairs of love, it may be said of him that Cupid hath clapp'd him o' th' shoulder, but I'll warrant him heart-whole.
lines: Break an hour's promise in love! He that will divide a
minute into a thousand parts, and break but a part of the
thousand part of a minute in the affairs of love, it may be said
of him that Cupid hath clapp'd him o' th' shoulder, but I'll
warrant him heart-whole.

speeches.speech: Pardon me, dear Rosalind.
lines: Pardon me, dear Rosalind.

speeches.speech: Nay, an you be so tardy, come no more in my sight. I had as lief be woo'd of a snail.
lines: Nay, an you be so tardy, come no more in my sight. I had
as lief be woo'd of a snail.

speeches.speech: Of a snail!
lines: Of a snail!

speeches.speech: Ay, of a snail; for though he comes slowly, he carries his house on his head- a better jointure, I think, than you make a woman; besides, he brings his destiny with him.
lines: Ay, of a snail; for though he comes slowly, he carries
his house on his head- a better jointure, I think, than you make
a woman; besides, he brings his destiny with him.

speeches.speech: What's that?
lines: What's that?

speeches.speech: Why, horns; which such as you are fain to be beholding to your wives for; but he comes armed in his fortune, and prevents the slander of his wife.
lines: Why, horns; which such as you are fain to be beholding to
your wives for; but he comes armed in his fortune, and prevents
the slander of his wife.

speeches.speech: Virtue is no horn-maker; and my Rosalind is virtuous.
lines: Virtue is no horn-maker; and my Rosalind is virtuous.

speeches.speech: And I am your Rosalind.
lines: And I am your Rosalind.

speeches.speech: It pleases him to call you so; but he hath a Rosalind of a better leer than you.
lines: It pleases him to call you so; but he hath a Rosalind of a
better leer than you.

speeches.speech: Come, woo me, woo me; for now I am in a holiday humour, and like enough to consent. What would you say to me now, an I were your very very Rosalind?
lines: Come, woo me, woo me; for now I am in a holiday humour,
and like enough to consent. What would you say to me now, an I
were your very very Rosalind?

speeches.speech: I would kiss before I spoke.
lines: I would kiss before I spoke.

speeches.speech: Nay, you were better speak first; and when you were gravell'd for lack of matter, you might take occasion to kiss. Very good orators, when they are out, they will spit; and for lovers lacking- God warn us!- matter, the cleanliest shift is to kiss.
lines: Nay, you were better speak first; and when you were
gravell'd for lack of matter, you might take occasion to kiss.
Very good orators, when they are out, they will spit; and for
lovers lacking- God warn us!- matter, the cleanliest shift is to
kiss.

speeches.speech: How if the kiss be denied?
lines: How if the kiss be denied?

speeches.speech: Then she puts you to entreaty, and there begins new matter.
lines: Then she puts you to entreaty, and there begins new
matter.

speeches.speech: Who could be out, being before his beloved mistress?
lines: Who could be out, being before his beloved mistress?

speeches.speech: Marry, that should you, if I were your mistress; or I should think my honesty ranker than my wit.
lines: Marry, that should you, if I were your mistress; or I
should think my honesty ranker than my wit.

speeches.speech: What, of my suit?
lines: What, of my suit?

speeches.speech: Not out of your apparel, and yet out of your suit. Am not I your Rosalind?
lines: Not out of your apparel, and yet out of your suit.
Am not I your Rosalind?

speeches.speech: I take some joy to say you are, because I would be talking of her.
lines: I take some joy to say you are, because I would be talking
of her.

speeches.speech: Well, in her person, I say I will not have you.
lines: Well, in her person, I say I will not have you.

speeches.speech: Then, in mine own person, I die.
lines: Then, in mine own person, I die.

speeches.speech: No, faith, die by attorney. The poor world is almost six thousand years old, and in all this time there was not any man died in his own person, videlicet, in a love-cause. Troilus had his brains dash'd out with a Grecian club; yet he did what he could to die before, and he is one of the patterns of love. Leander, he would have liv'd many a fair year, though Hero had turn'd nun, if it had not been for a hot midsummer night; for, good youth, he went but forth to wash him in the Hellespont, and, being taken with the cramp, was drown'd; and the foolish chroniclers of that age found it was- Hero of Sestos. But these are all lies: men have died from time to time, and worms have eaten them, but not for love.
lines: No, faith, die by attorney. The poor world is almost six
thousand years old, and in all this time there was not any man
died in his own person, videlicet, in a love-cause. Troilus had
his brains dash'd out with a Grecian club; yet he did what he
could to die before, and he is one of the patterns of love.
Leander, he would have liv'd many a fair year, though Hero had
turn'd nun, if it had not been for a hot midsummer night; for,
good youth, he went but forth to wash him in the Hellespont, and,
being taken with the cramp, was drown'd; and the foolish
chroniclers of that age found it was- Hero of Sestos. But these
are all lies: men have died from time to time, and worms have
eaten them, but not for love.

speeches.speech: I would not have my right Rosalind of this mind; for, I protest, her frown might kill me.
lines: I would not have my right Rosalind of this mind; for, I
protest, her frown might kill me.

speeches.speech: By this hand, it will not kill a fly. But come, now I will be your Rosalind in a more coming-on disposition; and ask me what you will, I will grant it.
lines: By this hand, it will not kill a fly. But come, now I
will be your Rosalind in a more coming-on disposition; and ask me
what you will, I will grant it.

speeches.speech: Then love me, Rosalind.
lines: Then love me, Rosalind.

speeches.speech: Yes, faith, will I, Fridays and Saturdays, and all.
lines: Yes, faith, will I, Fridays and Saturdays, and all.

speeches.speech: And wilt thou have me?
lines: And wilt thou have me?

speeches.speech: Ay, and twenty such.
lines: Ay, and twenty such.

speeches.speech: What sayest thou?
lines: What sayest thou?

speeches.speech: Are you not good?
lines: Are you not good?

speeches.speech: I hope so.
lines: I hope so.

speeches.speech: Why then, can one desire too much of a good thing? Come, sister, you shall be the priest, and marry us. Give me your hand, Orlando. What do you say, sister?
lines: Why then, can one desire too much of a good thing? Come,
sister, you shall be the priest, and marry us. Give me your hand,
Orlando. What do you say, sister?

speeches.speech: Pray thee, marry us.
lines: Pray thee, marry us.

speeches.speech: I cannot say the words.
lines: I cannot say the words.

speeches.speech: You must begin 'Will you, Orlando'-
lines: You must begin 'Will you, Orlando'-

speeches.speech: Go to. Will you, Orlando, have to wife this Rosalind?
lines: Go to. Will you, Orlando, have to wife this Rosalind?

speeches.speech: I will.
lines: I will.

speeches.speech: Ay, but when?
lines: Ay, but when?

speeches.speech: Why, now; as fast as she can marry us.
lines: Why, now; as fast as she can marry us.

speeches.speech: Then you must say 'I take thee, Rosalind, for wife.'
lines: Then you must say 'I take thee, Rosalind, for wife.'

speeches.speech: I take thee, Rosalind, for wife.
lines: I take thee, Rosalind, for wife.

speeches.speech: I might ask you for your commission; but- I do take thee, Orlando, for my husband. There's a girl goes before the priest; and, certainly, a woman's thought runs before her actions.
lines: I might ask you for your commission; but- I do take thee,
Orlando, for my husband. There's a girl goes before the priest;
and, certainly, a woman's thought runs before her actions.

speeches.speech: So do all thoughts; they are wing'd.
lines: So do all thoughts; they are wing'd.

speeches.speech: Now tell me how long you would have her, after you have possess'd her.
lines: Now tell me how long you would have her, after you have
possess'd her.

speeches.speech: For ever and a day.
lines: For ever and a day.

speeches.speech: Say 'a day' without the 'ever.' No, no, Orlando; men are April when they woo, December when they wed: maids are May when they are maids, but the sky changes when they are wives. I will be more jealous of thee than a Barbary cock-pigeon over his hen, more clamorous than a parrot against rain, more new-fangled than an ape, more giddy in my desires than a monkey. I will weep for nothing, like Diana in the fountain, and I will do that when you are dispos'd to be merry; I will laugh like a hyen, and that when thou are inclin'd to sleep.
lines: Say 'a day' without the 'ever.' No, no, Orlando; men are
April when they woo, December when they wed: maids are May when
they are maids, but the sky changes when they are wives. I will
be more jealous of thee than a Barbary cock-pigeon over his hen,
more clamorous than a parrot against rain, more new-fangled than
an ape, more giddy in my desires than a monkey. I will weep for
nothing, like Diana in the fountain, and I will do that when you
are dispos'd to be merry; I will laugh like a hyen, and that when
thou are inclin'd to sleep.

speeches.speech: But will my Rosalind do so?
lines: But will my Rosalind do so?

speeches.speech: By my life, she will do as I do.
lines: By my life, she will do as I do.

speeches.speech: O, but she is wise.
lines: O, but she is wise.

speeches.speech: Or else she could not have the wit to do this. The wiser, the waywarder. Make the doors upon a woman's wit, and it will out at the casement; shut that, and 'twill out at the key-hole; stop that, 'twill fly with the smoke out at the chimney.
lines: Or else she could not have the wit to do this. The wiser,
the waywarder. Make the doors upon a woman's wit, and it will out
at the casement; shut that, and 'twill out at the key-hole; stop
that, 'twill fly with the smoke out at the chimney.

speeches.speech: A man that had a wife with such a wit, he might say 'Wit, whither wilt?'
lines: A man that had a wife with such a wit, he might say 'Wit,
whither wilt?'

speeches.speech: Nay, you might keep that check for it, till you met your wife's wit going to your neighbour's bed.
lines: Nay, you might keep that check for it, till you met your
wife's wit going to your neighbour's bed.

speeches.speech: And what wit could wit have to excuse that?
lines: And what wit could wit have to excuse that?

speeches.speech: Marry, to say she came to seek you there. You shall never take her without her answer, unless you take her without her tongue. O, that woman that cannot make her fault her husband's occasion, let her never nurse her child herself, for she will breed it like a fool!
lines: Marry, to say she came to seek you there. You shall never
take her without her answer, unless you take her without her
tongue. O, that woman that cannot make her fault her husband's
occasion, let her never nurse her child herself, for she will
breed it like a fool!

speeches.speech: For these two hours, Rosalind, I will leave thee.
lines: For these two hours, Rosalind, I will leave thee.

speeches.speech: Alas, dear love, I cannot lack thee two hours!
lines: Alas, dear love, I cannot lack thee two hours!

speeches.speech: I must attend the Duke at dinner; by two o'clock I will be with thee again.
lines: I must attend the Duke at dinner; by two o'clock I will be
with thee again.

speeches.speech: Ay, go your ways, go your ways. I knew what you would prove; my friends told me as much, and I thought no less. That flattering tongue of yours won me. 'Tis but one cast away, and so, come death! Two o'clock is your hour?
lines: Ay, go your ways, go your ways. I knew what you would
prove; my friends told me as much, and I thought no less. That
flattering tongue of yours won me. 'Tis but one cast away, and
so, come death! Two o'clock is your hour?

speeches.speech: Ay, sweet Rosalind.
lines: Ay, sweet Rosalind.

speeches.speech: By my troth, and in good earnest, and so God mend me, and by all pretty oaths that are not dangerous, if you break one jot of your promise, or come one minute behind your hour, I will think you the most pathetical break-promise, and the most hollow lover, and the most unworthy of her you call Rosalind, that may be chosen out of the gross band of the unfaithful. Therefore beware my censure, and keep your promise.
lines: By my troth, and in good earnest, and so God mend me, and
by all pretty oaths that are not dangerous, if you break one jot
of your promise, or come one minute behind your hour, I will
think you the most pathetical break-promise, and the most hollow
lover, and the most unworthy of her you call Rosalind, that may
be chosen out of the gross band of the unfaithful. Therefore
beware my censure, and keep your promise.

speeches.speech: With no less religion than if thou wert indeed my Rosalind; so, adieu.
lines: With no less religion than if thou wert indeed my
Rosalind; so, adieu.

speeches.speech: Well, Time is the old justice that examines all such offenders, and let Time try. Adieu.        Exit ORLANDO
lines: Well, Time is the old justice that examines all such
offenders, and let Time try. Adieu.        Exit ORLANDO

speeches.speech: You have simply misus'd our sex in your love-prate. We must have your doublet and hose pluck'd over your head, and show the world what the bird hath done to her own nest.
lines: You have simply misus'd our sex in your love-prate. We must
have your doublet and hose pluck'd over your head, and show the
world what the bird hath done to her own nest.

speeches.speech: O coz, coz, coz, my pretty little coz, that thou didst know how many fathom deep I am in love! But it cannot be sounded; my affection hath an unknown bottom, like the Bay of Portugal.
lines: O coz, coz, coz, my pretty little coz, that thou didst
know how many fathom deep I am in love! But it cannot be sounded;
my affection hath an unknown bottom, like the Bay of Portugal.

speeches.speech: Or rather, bottomless; that as fast as you pour affection in, it runs out.
lines: Or rather, bottomless; that as fast as you pour affection
in, it runs out.

speeches.speech: No; that same wicked bastard of Venus, that was begot of thought, conceiv'd of spleen, and born of madness; that blind rascally boy, that abuses every one's eyes, because his own are out- let him be judge how deep I am in love. I'll tell thee, Aliena, I cannot be out of the sight of Orlando. I'll go find a shadow, and sigh till he come.
lines: No; that same wicked bastard of Venus, that was begot of
thought, conceiv'd of spleen, and born of madness; that blind
rascally boy, that abuses every one's eyes, because his own are
out- let him be judge how deep I am in love. I'll tell thee,
Aliena, I cannot be out of the sight of Orlando. I'll go find a
shadow, and sigh till he come.

speeches.speech: And I'll sleep.                             Exeunt
lines: And I'll sleep.                             Exeunt

speeches.speech: Which is he that killed the deer?
lines: Which is he that killed the deer?

speeches.speech: Sir, it was I.
lines: Sir, it was I.

speeches.speech: Let's present him to the Duke, like a Roman conqueror; and it would do well to set the deer's horns upon his head for a branch of victory. Have you no song, forester, for this purpose?
lines: Let's present him to the Duke, like a Roman conqueror; and
it would do well to set the deer's horns upon his head for a
branch of victory. Have you no song, forester, for this purpose?

speeches.speech: Yes, sir.
lines: Yes, sir.

speeches.speech: Sing it; 'tis no matter how it be in tune, so it make noise enough. SONG. What shall he have that kill'd the deer? His leather skin and horns to wear. [The rest shall hear this burden:] Then sing him home. Take thou no scorn to wear the horn; It was a crest ere thou wast born. Thy father's father wore it; And thy father bore it. The horn, the horn, the lusty horn, Is not a thing to laugh to scorn.              Exeunt
lines: Sing it; 'tis no matter how it be in tune, so it make noise
enough.
SONG.
What shall he have that kill'd the deer?
His leather skin and horns to wear.
[The rest shall hear this burden:]
Then sing him home.
Take thou no scorn to wear the horn;
It was a crest ere thou wast born.
Thy father's father wore it;
And thy father bore it.
The horn, the horn, the lusty horn,
Is not a thing to laugh to scorn.              Exeunt

speeches.speech: How say you now? Is it not past two o'clock? And here much Orlando!
lines: How say you now? Is it not past two o'clock?
And here much Orlando!

speeches.speech: I warrant you, with pure love and troubled brain, he hath ta'en his bow and arrows, and is gone forth- to sleep. Look, who comes here.
lines: I warrant you, with pure love and troubled brain, he hath
ta'en his bow and arrows, and is gone forth- to sleep. Look, who
comes here.

speeches.speech: My errand is to you, fair youth; My gentle Phebe did bid me give you this. I know not the contents; but, as I guess By the stern brow and waspish action Which she did use as she was writing of it, It bears an angry tenour. Pardon me, I am but as a guiltless messenger.
lines: My errand is to you, fair youth;
My gentle Phebe did bid me give you this.
I know not the contents; but, as I guess
By the stern brow and waspish action
Which she did use as she was writing of it,
It bears an angry tenour. Pardon me,
I am but as a guiltless messenger.

speeches.speech: Patience herself would startle at this letter, And play the swaggerer. Bear this, bear all. She says I am not fair, that I lack manners; She calls me proud, and that she could not love me, Were man as rare as Phoenix. 'Od's my will! Her love is not the hare that I do hunt; Why writes she so to me? Well, shepherd, well, This is a letter of your own device.
lines: Patience herself would startle at this letter,
And play the swaggerer. Bear this, bear all.
She says I am not fair, that I lack manners;
She calls me proud, and that she could not love me,
Were man as rare as Phoenix. 'Od's my will!
Her love is not the hare that I do hunt;
Why writes she so to me? Well, shepherd, well,
This is a letter of your own device.

speeches.speech: No, I protest, I know not the contents; Phebe did write it.
lines: No, I protest, I know not the contents;
Phebe did write it.

speeches.speech: Come, come, you are a fool, And turn'd into the extremity of love. I saw her hand; she has a leathern hand, A freestone-colour'd hand; I verily did think That her old gloves were on, but 'twas her hands; She has a huswife's hand- but that's no matter. I say she never did invent this letter: This is a man's invention, and his hand.
lines: Come, come, you are a fool,
And turn'd into the extremity of love.
I saw her hand; she has a leathern hand,
A freestone-colour'd hand; I verily did think
That her old gloves were on, but 'twas her hands;
She has a huswife's hand- but that's no matter.
I say she never did invent this letter:
This is a man's invention, and his hand.

speeches.speech: Sure, it is hers.
lines: Sure, it is hers.

speeches.speech: Why, 'tis a boisterous and a cruel style; A style for challengers. Why, she defies me, Like Turk to Christian. Women's gentle brain Could not drop forth such giant-rude invention, Such Ethiope words, blacker in their effect Than in their countenance. Will you hear the letter?
lines: Why, 'tis a boisterous and a cruel style;
A style for challengers. Why, she defies me,
Like Turk to Christian. Women's gentle brain
Could not drop forth such giant-rude invention,
Such Ethiope words, blacker in their effect
Than in their countenance. Will you hear the letter?

speeches.speech: So please you, for I never heard it yet; Yet heard too much of Phebe's cruelty.
lines: So please you, for I never heard it yet;
Yet heard too much of Phebe's cruelty.

speeches.speech: She Phebes me: mark how the tyrant writes.                                                 [Reads] 'Art thou god to shepherd turn'd, That a maiden's heart hath burn'd?' Can a woman rail thus?
lines: She Phebes me: mark how the tyrant writes.                                                 [Reads]
'Art thou god to shepherd turn'd,
That a maiden's heart hath burn'd?'
Can a woman rail thus?

speeches.speech: Call you this railing?
lines: Call you this railing?

speeches.speech: 'Why, thy godhead laid apart, Warr'st thou with a woman's heart?' Did you ever hear such railing? 'Whiles the eye of man did woo me, That could do no vengeance to me.' Meaning me a beast. 'If the scorn of your bright eyne Have power to raise such love in mine, Alack, in me what strange effect Would they work in mild aspect! Whiles you chid me, I did love; How then might your prayers move! He that brings this love to the Little knows this love in me; And by him seal up thy mind, Whether that thy youth and kind Will the faithful offer take Of me and all that I can make; Or else by him my love deny, And then I'll study how to die.'
lines: 'Why, thy godhead laid apart,
Warr'st thou with a woman's heart?'
Did you ever hear such railing?
'Whiles the eye of man did woo me,
That could do no vengeance to me.'
Meaning me a beast.
'If the scorn of your bright eyne
Have power to raise such love in mine,
Alack, in me what strange effect
Would they work in mild aspect!
Whiles you chid me, I did love;
How then might your prayers move!
He that brings this love to the
Little knows this love in me;
And by him seal up thy mind,
Whether that thy youth and kind
Will the faithful offer take
Of me and all that I can make;
Or else by him my love deny,
And then I'll study how to die.'

speeches.speech: Call you this chiding?
lines: Call you this chiding?

speeches.speech: Alas, poor shepherd!
lines: Alas, poor shepherd!

speeches.speech: Do you pity him? No, he deserves no pity. Wilt thou love such a woman? What, to make thee an instrument, and play false strains upon thee! Not to be endur'd! Well, go your way to her, for I see love hath made thee tame snake, and say this to her- that if she love me, I charge her to love thee; if she will not, I will never have her unless thou entreat for her. If you be a true lover, hence, and not a word; for here comes more company.
lines: Do you pity him? No, he deserves no pity. Wilt thou love
such a woman? What, to make thee an instrument, and play false
strains upon thee! Not to be endur'd! Well, go your way to her,
for I see love hath made thee tame snake, and say this to her-
that if she love me, I charge her to love thee; if she will not,
I will never have her unless thou entreat for her. If you be a
true lover, hence, and not a word; for here comes more company.

speeches.speech: Good morrow, fair ones; pray you, if you know, Where in the purlieus of this forest stands A sheep-cote fenc'd about with olive trees?
lines: Good morrow, fair ones; pray you, if you know,
Where in the purlieus of this forest stands
A sheep-cote fenc'd about with olive trees?

speeches.speech: West of this place, down in the neighbour bottom. The rank of osiers by the murmuring stream Left on your right hand brings you to the place. But at this hour the house doth keep itself; There's none within.
lines: West of this place, down in the neighbour bottom.
The rank of osiers by the murmuring stream
Left on your right hand brings you to the place.
But at this hour the house doth keep itself;
There's none within.

speeches.speech: If that an eye may profit by a tongue, Then should I know you by description- Such garments, and such years: 'The boy is fair, Of female favour, and bestows himself Like a ripe sister; the woman low, And browner than her brother.' Are not you The owner of the house I did inquire for?
lines: If that an eye may profit by a tongue,
Then should I know you by description-
Such garments, and such years: 'The boy is fair,
Of female favour, and bestows himself
Like a ripe sister; the woman low,
And browner than her brother.' Are not you
The owner of the house I did inquire for?

speeches.speech: It is no boast, being ask'd, to say we are.
lines: It is no boast, being ask'd, to say we are.

speeches.speech: Orlando doth commend him to you both; And to that youth he calls his Rosalind He sends this bloody napkin. Are you he?
lines: Orlando doth commend him to you both;
And to that youth he calls his Rosalind
He sends this bloody napkin. Are you he?

speeches.speech: I am. What must we understand by this?
lines: I am. What must we understand by this?

speeches.speech: Some of my shame; if you will know of me What man I am, and how, and why, and where, This handkercher was stain'd.
lines: Some of my shame; if you will know of me
What man I am, and how, and why, and where,
This handkercher was stain'd.

speeches.speech: I pray you, tell it.
lines: I pray you, tell it.

speeches.speech: When last the young Orlando parted from you, He left a promise to return again Within an hour; and, pacing through the forest, Chewing the food of sweet and bitter fancy, Lo, what befell! He threw his eye aside, And mark what object did present itself. Under an oak, whose boughs were moss'd with age, And high top bald with dry antiquity, A wretched ragged man, o'ergrown with hair, Lay sleeping on his back. About his neck A green and gilded snake had wreath'd itself, Who with her head nimble in threats approach'd The opening of his mouth; but suddenly, Seeing Orlando, it unlink'd itself, And with indented glides did slip away Into a bush; under which bush's shade A lioness, with udders all drawn dry, Lay couching, head on ground, with catlike watch, When that the sleeping man should stir; for 'tis The royal disposition of that beast To prey on nothing that doth seem as dead. This seen, Orlando did approach the man, And found it was his brother, his elder brother.
lines: When last the young Orlando parted from you,
He left a promise to return again
Within an hour; and, pacing through the forest,
Chewing the food of sweet and bitter fancy,
Lo, what befell! He threw his eye aside,
And mark what object did present itself.
Under an oak, whose boughs were moss'd with age,
And high top bald with dry antiquity,
A wretched ragged man, o'ergrown with hair,
Lay sleeping on his back. About his neck
A green and gilded snake had wreath'd itself,
Who with her head nimble in threats approach'd
The opening of his mouth; but suddenly,
Seeing Orlando, it unlink'd itself,
And with indented glides did slip away
Into a bush; under which bush's shade
A lioness, with udders all drawn dry,
Lay couching, head on ground, with catlike watch,
When that the sleeping man should stir; for 'tis
The royal disposition of that beast
To prey on nothing that doth seem as dead.
This seen, Orlando did approach the man,
And found it was his brother, his elder brother.

speeches.speech: O, I have heard him speak of that same brother; And he did render him the most unnatural That liv'd amongst men.
lines: O, I have heard him speak of that same brother;
And he did render him the most unnatural
That liv'd amongst men.

speeches.speech: And well he might so do, For well I know he was unnatural.
lines: And well he might so do,
For well I know he was unnatural.

speeches.speech: But, to Orlando: did he leave him there, Food to the suck'd and hungry lioness?
lines: But, to Orlando: did he leave him there,
Food to the suck'd and hungry lioness?

speeches.speech: Twice did he turn his back, and purpos'd so; But kindness, nobler ever than revenge, And nature, stronger than his just occasion, Made him give battle to the lioness, Who quickly fell before him; in which hurtling From miserable slumber I awak'd.
lines: Twice did he turn his back, and purpos'd so;
But kindness, nobler ever than revenge,
And nature, stronger than his just occasion,
Made him give battle to the lioness,
Who quickly fell before him; in which hurtling
From miserable slumber I awak'd.

speeches.speech: Are you his brother?
lines: Are you his brother?

speeches.speech: Was't you he rescu'd?
lines: Was't you he rescu'd?

speeches.speech: Was't you that did so oft contrive to kill him?
lines: Was't you that did so oft contrive to kill him?

speeches.speech: 'Twas I; but 'tis not I. I do not shame To tell you what I was, since my conversion So sweetly tastes, being the thing I am.
lines: 'Twas I; but 'tis not I. I do not shame
To tell you what I was, since my conversion
So sweetly tastes, being the thing I am.

speeches.speech: But for the bloody napkin?
lines: But for the bloody napkin?

speeches.speech: By and by. When from the first to last, betwixt us two, Tears our recountments had most kindly bath'd, As how I came into that desert place- In brief, he led me to the gentle Duke, Who gave me fresh array and entertainment, Committing me unto my brother's love; Who led me instantly unto his cave, There stripp'd himself, and here upon his arm The lioness had torn some flesh away, Which all this while had bled; and now he fainted, And cried, in fainting, upon Rosalind. Brief, I recover'd him, bound up his wound, And, after some small space, being strong at heart, He sent me hither, stranger as I am, To tell this story, that you might excuse His broken promise, and to give this napkin, Dy'd in his blood, unto the shepherd youth That he in sport doth call his Rosalind.
lines: By and by.
When from the first to last, betwixt us two,
Tears our recountments had most kindly bath'd,
As how I came into that desert place-
In brief, he led me to the gentle Duke,
Who gave me fresh array and entertainment,
Committing me unto my brother's love;
Who led me instantly unto his cave,
There stripp'd himself, and here upon his arm
The lioness had torn some flesh away,
Which all this while had bled; and now he fainted,
And cried, in fainting, upon Rosalind.
Brief, I recover'd him, bound up his wound,
And, after some small space, being strong at heart,
He sent me hither, stranger as I am,
To tell this story, that you might excuse
His broken promise, and to give this napkin,
Dy'd in his blood, unto the shepherd youth
That he in sport doth call his Rosalind.

speeches.speech: Why, how now, Ganymede! sweet Ganymede!
lines: Why, how now, Ganymede! sweet Ganymede!

speeches.speech: Many will swoon when they do look on blood.
lines: Many will swoon when they do look on blood.

speeches.speech: There is more in it. Cousin Ganymede!
lines: There is more in it. Cousin Ganymede!

speeches.speech: Look, he recovers.
lines: Look, he recovers.

speeches.speech: I would I were at home.
lines: I would I were at home.

speeches.speech: We'll lead you thither. I pray you, will you take him by the arm?
lines: We'll lead you thither.
I pray you, will you take him by the arm?

speeches.speech: Be of good cheer, youth. You a man! You lack a man's heart.
lines: Be of good cheer, youth. You a man!
You lack a man's heart.

speeches.speech: I do so, I confess it. Ah, sirrah, a body would think this was well counterfeited. I pray you tell your brother how well I counterfeited. Heigh-ho!
lines: I do so, I confess it. Ah, sirrah, a body would think
this was well counterfeited. I pray you tell your brother how
well I counterfeited. Heigh-ho!

speeches.speech: This was not counterfeit; there is too great testimony in your complexion that it was a passion of earnest.
lines: This was not counterfeit; there is too great testimony in
your complexion that it was a passion of earnest.

speeches.speech: Counterfeit, I assure you.
lines: Counterfeit, I assure you.

speeches.speech: Well then, take a good heart and counterfeit to be a man.
lines: Well then, take a good heart and counterfeit to be a man.

speeches.speech: So I do; but, i' faith, I should have been a woman by right.
lines: So I do; but, i' faith, I should have been a woman by
right.

speeches.speech: Come, you look paler and paler; pray you draw homewards. Good sir, go with us.
lines: Come, you look paler and paler; pray you draw homewards.
Good sir, go with us.

speeches.speech: That will I, for I must bear answer back How you excuse my brother, Rosalind.
lines: That will I, for I must bear answer back
How you excuse my brother, Rosalind.

speeches.speech: I shall devise something; but, I pray you, commend my counterfeiting to him. Will you go? Exeunt
lines: I shall devise something; but, I pray you, commend my
counterfeiting to him. Will you go? Exeunt

speeches.speech: We shall find a time, Audrey; patience, gentle Audrey.
lines: We shall find a time, Audrey; patience, gentle Audrey.

speeches.speech: Faith, the priest was good enough, for all the old gentleman's saying.
lines: Faith, the priest was good enough, for all the old
gentleman's saying.

speeches.speech: A most wicked Sir Oliver, Audrey, a most vile Martext. But, Audrey, there is a youth here in the forest lays claim to you.
lines: A most wicked Sir Oliver, Audrey, a most vile Martext.
But, Audrey, there is a youth here in the forest lays claim to
you.

speeches.speech: Ay, I know who 'tis; he hath no interest in me in the world; here comes the man you mean.
lines: Ay, I know who 'tis; he hath no interest in me in the
world; here comes the man you mean.

speeches.speech: It is meat and drink to me to see a clown. By my troth, we that have good wits have much to answer for: we shall be flouting; we cannot hold.
lines: It is meat and drink to me to see a clown. By my troth,
we that have good wits have much to answer for: we shall be
flouting; we cannot hold.

speeches.speech: Good ev'n, Audrey.
lines: Good ev'n, Audrey.

speeches.speech: God ye good ev'n, William.
lines: God ye good ev'n, William.

speeches.speech: And good ev'n to you, sir.
lines: And good ev'n to you, sir.

speeches.speech: Good ev'n, gentle friend. Cover thy head, cover thy head; nay, prithee be cover'd. How old are you, friend?
lines: Good ev'n, gentle friend. Cover thy head, cover thy
head; nay, prithee be cover'd. How old are you, friend?

speeches.speech: Five and twenty, sir.
lines: Five and twenty, sir.

speeches.speech: A ripe age. Is thy name William?
lines: A ripe age. Is thy name William?

speeches.speech: William, sir.
lines: William, sir.

speeches.speech: A fair name. Wast born i' th' forest here?
lines: A fair name. Wast born i' th' forest here?

speeches.speech: Ay, sir, I thank God.
lines: Ay, sir, I thank God.

speeches.speech: 'Thank God.' A good answer. Art rich?
lines: 'Thank God.' A good answer.
Art rich?

speeches.speech: Faith, sir, so so.
lines: Faith, sir, so so.

speeches.speech: 'So so' is good, very good, very excellent good; and yet it is not; it is but so so. Art thou wise?
lines: 'So so' is good, very good, very excellent good; and
yet it is not; it is but so so. Art thou wise?

speeches.speech: Ay, sir, I have a pretty wit.
lines: Ay, sir, I have a pretty wit.

speeches.speech: Why, thou say'st well. I do now remember a saying: 'The fool doth think he is wise, but the wise man knows himself to be a fool.' The heathen philosopher, when he had a desire to eat a grape, would open his lips when he put it into his mouth; meaning thereby that grapes were made to eat and lips to open. You do love this maid?
lines: Why, thou say'st well. I do now remember a saying: 'The
fool doth think he is wise, but the wise man knows himself to be
a fool.' The heathen philosopher, when he had a desire to eat a
grape, would open his lips when he put it into his mouth; meaning
thereby that grapes were made to eat and lips to open. You do
love this maid?

speeches.speech: I do, sir.
lines: I do, sir.

speeches.speech: Give me your hand. Art thou learned?
lines: Give me your hand. Art thou learned?

speeches.speech: No, sir.
lines: No, sir.

speeches.speech: Then learn this of me: to have is to have; for it is a figure in rhetoric that drink, being pour'd out of cup into a glass, by filling the one doth empty the other; for all your writers do consent that ipse is he; now, you are not ipse, for I am he.
lines: Then learn this of me: to have is to have; for it is a
figure in rhetoric that drink, being pour'd out of cup into a
glass, by filling the one doth empty the other; for all your
writers do consent that ipse is he; now, you are not ipse, for I
am he.

speeches.speech: Which he, sir?
lines: Which he, sir?

speeches.speech: He, sir, that must marry this woman. Therefore, you clown, abandon- which is in the vulgar leave- the society- which in the boorish is company- of this female- which in the common is woman- which together is: abandon the society of this female; or, clown, thou perishest; or, to thy better understanding, diest; or, to wit, I kill thee, make thee away, translate thy life into death, thy liberty into bondage. I will deal in poison with thee, or in bastinado, or in steel; I will bandy with thee in faction; will o'er-run thee with policy; I will kill thee a hundred and fifty ways; therefore tremble and depart.
lines: He, sir, that must marry this woman. Therefore, you
clown, abandon- which is in the vulgar leave- the society- which
in the boorish is company- of this female- which in the common is
woman- which together is: abandon the society of this female; or,
clown, thou perishest; or, to thy better understanding, diest;
or, to wit, I kill thee, make thee away, translate thy life into
death, thy liberty into bondage. I will deal in poison with thee,
or in bastinado, or in steel; I will bandy with thee in faction;
will o'er-run thee with policy; I will kill thee a hundred and
fifty ways; therefore tremble and depart.

speeches.speech: Do, good William.
lines: Do, good William.

speeches.speech: God rest you merry, sir. Exit
lines: God rest you merry, sir. Exit

speeches.speech: Our master and mistress seeks you; come away, away.
lines: Our master and mistress seeks you; come away, away.

speeches.speech: Trip, Audrey, trip, Audrey. I attend, I attend.
lines: Trip, Audrey, trip, Audrey. I attend, I attend.

speeches.speech: Is't possible that on so little acquaintance you should like her? that but seeing you should love her? and loving woo? and, wooing, she should grant? and will you persever to enjoy her?
lines: Is't possible that on so little acquaintance you should
like her? that but seeing you should love her? and loving woo?
and, wooing, she should grant? and will you persever to enjoy
her?

speeches.speech: Neither call the giddiness of it in question, the poverty of her, the small acquaintance, my sudden wooing, nor her sudden consenting; but say with me, I love Aliena; say with her that she loves me; consent with both that we may enjoy each other. It shall be to your good; for my father's house and all the revenue that was old Sir Rowland's will I estate upon you, and here live and die a shepherd.
lines: Neither call the giddiness of it in question, the poverty
of her, the small acquaintance, my sudden wooing, nor her sudden
consenting; but say with me, I love Aliena; say with her that she
loves me; consent with both that we may enjoy each other. It
shall be to your good; for my father's house and all the revenue
that was old Sir Rowland's will I estate upon you, and here live
and die a shepherd.

speeches.speech: You have my consent. Let your wedding be to-morrow. Thither will I invite the Duke and all's contented followers. Go you and prepare Aliena; for, look you, here comes my Rosalind.
lines: You have my consent. Let your wedding be to-morrow.
Thither will I invite the Duke and all's contented followers. Go
you and prepare Aliena; for, look you, here comes my Rosalind.

speeches.speech: God save you, brother.
lines: God save you, brother.

speeches.speech: And you, fair sister. Exit
lines: And you, fair sister. Exit

speeches.speech: O, my dear Orlando, how it grieves me to see thee wear thy heart in a scarf!
lines: O, my dear Orlando, how it grieves me to see thee wear
thy heart in a scarf!

speeches.speech: It is my arm.
lines: It is my arm.

speeches.speech: I thought thy heart had been wounded with the claws of a lion.
lines: I thought thy heart had been wounded with the claws of a
lion.

speeches.speech: Wounded it is, but with the eyes of a lady.
lines: Wounded it is, but with the eyes of a lady.

speeches.speech: Did your brother tell you how I counterfeited to swoon when he show'd me your handkercher?
lines: Did your brother tell you how I counterfeited to swoon
when he show'd me your handkercher?

speeches.speech: Ay, and greater wonders than that.
lines: Ay, and greater wonders than that.

speeches.speech: O, I know where you are. Nay, 'tis true. There was never any thing so sudden but the fight of two rams and Caesar's thrasonical brag of 'I came, saw, and overcame.' For your brother and my sister no sooner met but they look'd; no sooner look'd but they lov'd; no sooner lov'd but they sigh'd; no sooner sigh'd but they ask'd one another the reason; no sooner knew the reason but they sought the remedy- and in these degrees have they made pair of stairs to marriage, which they will climb incontinent, or else be incontinent before marriage. They are in the very wrath of love, and they will together. Clubs cannot part them.
lines: O, I know where you are. Nay, 'tis true. There was never
any thing so sudden but the fight of two rams and Caesar's
thrasonical brag of 'I came, saw, and overcame.' For your brother
and my sister no sooner met but they look'd; no sooner look'd but
they lov'd; no sooner lov'd but they sigh'd; no sooner sigh'd but
they ask'd one another the reason; no sooner knew the reason but
they sought the remedy- and in these degrees have they made pair
of stairs to marriage, which they will climb incontinent, or else
be incontinent before marriage. They are in the very wrath of
love, and they will together. Clubs cannot part them.

speeches.speech: They shall be married to-morrow; and I will bid the Duke to the nuptial. But, O, how bitter a thing it is to look into happiness through another man's eyes! By so much the more shall I to-morrow be at the height of heart-heaviness, by how much I shall think my brother happy in having what he wishes for.
lines: They shall be married to-morrow; and I will bid the Duke
to the nuptial. But, O, how bitter a thing it is to look into
happiness through another man's eyes! By so much the more shall I
to-morrow be at the height of heart-heaviness, by how much I
shall think my brother happy in having what he wishes for.

speeches.speech: Why, then, to-morrow I cannot serve your turn for Rosalind?
lines: Why, then, to-morrow I cannot serve your turn for
Rosalind?

speeches.speech: I can live no longer by thinking.
lines: I can live no longer by thinking.

speeches.speech: I will weary you, then, no longer with idle talking. Know of me then- for now I speak to some purpose- that I know you are a gentleman of good conceit. I speak not this that you should bear a good opinion of my knowledge, insomuch I say I know you are; neither do I labour for a greater esteem than may in some little measure draw a belief from you, to do yourself good, and not to grace me. Believe then, if you please, that I can do strange things. I have, since I was three year old, convers'd with a magician, most profound in his art and yet not damnable. If you do love Rosalind so near the heart as your gesture cries it out, when your brother marries Aliena shall you marry her. I know into what straits of fortune she is driven; and it is not impossible to me, if it appear not inconvenient to you, to set her before your eyes to-morrow, human as she is, and without any danger.
lines: I will weary you, then, no longer with idle talking. Know
of me then- for now I speak to some purpose- that I know you are
a gentleman of good conceit. I speak not this that you should
bear a good opinion of my knowledge, insomuch I say I know you
are; neither do I labour for a greater esteem than may in some
little measure draw a belief from you, to do yourself good, and
not to grace me. Believe then, if you please, that I can do
strange things. I have, since I was three year old, convers'd
with a magician, most profound in his art and yet not damnable.
If you do love Rosalind so near the heart as your gesture cries
it out, when your brother marries Aliena shall you marry her. I
know into what straits of fortune she is driven; and it is not
impossible to me, if it appear not inconvenient to you, to set
her before your eyes to-morrow, human as she is, and without any
danger.

speeches.speech: Speak'st thou in sober meanings?
lines: Speak'st thou in sober meanings?

speeches.speech: By my life, I do; which I tender dearly, though I say I am a magician. Therefore put you in your best array, bid your friends; for if you will be married to-morrow, you shall; and to Rosalind, if you will. [Enter SILVIUS and PHEBE] Look, here comes a lover of mine, and a lover of hers.
lines: By my life, I do; which I tender dearly, though I say I
am a magician. Therefore put you in your best array, bid your
friends; for if you will be married to-morrow, you shall; and to
Rosalind, if you will.
[Enter SILVIUS and PHEBE]
Look, here comes a lover of mine, and a lover of hers.

speeches.speech: Youth, you have done me much ungentleness To show the letter that I writ to you.
lines: Youth, you have done me much ungentleness
To show the letter that I writ to you.

speeches.speech: I care not if I have. It is my study To seem despiteful and ungentle to you. You are there follow'd by a faithful shepherd; Look upon him, love him; he worships you.
lines: I care not if I have. It is my study
To seem despiteful and ungentle to you.
You are there follow'd by a faithful shepherd;
Look upon him, love him; he worships you.

speeches.speech: Good shepherd, tell this youth what 'tis to love.
lines: Good shepherd, tell this youth what 'tis to love.

speeches.speech: It is to be all made of sighs and tears; And so am I for Phebe.
lines: It is to be all made of sighs and tears;
And so am I for Phebe.

speeches.speech: And I for Ganymede.
lines: And I for Ganymede.

speeches.speech: And I for Rosalind.
lines: And I for Rosalind.

speeches.speech: And I for no woman.
lines: And I for no woman.

speeches.speech: It is to be all made of faith and service; And so am I for Phebe.
lines: It is to be all made of faith and service;
And so am I for Phebe.

speeches.speech: And I for Ganymede.
lines: And I for Ganymede.

speeches.speech: And I for Rosalind.
lines: And I for Rosalind.

speeches.speech: And I for no woman.
lines: And I for no woman.

speeches.speech: It is to be all made of fantasy, All made of passion, and all made of wishes; All adoration, duty, and observance, All humbleness, all patience, and impatience, All purity, all trial, all obedience; And so am I for Phebe.
lines: It is to be all made of fantasy,
All made of passion, and all made of wishes;
All adoration, duty, and observance,
All humbleness, all patience, and impatience,
All purity, all trial, all obedience;
And so am I for Phebe.

speeches.speech: And so am I for Ganymede.
lines: And so am I for Ganymede.

speeches.speech: And so am I for Rosalind.
lines: And so am I for Rosalind.

speeches.speech: And so am I for no woman.
lines: And so am I for no woman.

speeches.speech: If this be so, why blame you me to love you?
lines: If this be so, why blame you me to love you?

speeches.speech: If this be so, why blame you me to love you?
lines: If this be so, why blame you me to love you?

speeches.speech: If this be so, why blame you me to love you?
lines: If this be so, why blame you me to love you?

speeches.speech: Why do you speak too, 'Why blame you me to love you?'
lines: Why do you speak too, 'Why blame you me to love you?'

speeches.speech: To her that is not here, nor doth not hear.
lines: To her that is not here, nor doth not hear.

speeches.speech: Pray you, no more of this; 'tis like the howling of Irish wolves against the moon. [To SILVIUS] I will help you if I can. [To PHEBE] I would love you if I could.- To-morrow meet me all together. [ To PHEBE ] I will marry you if ever I marry woman, and I'll be married to-morrow. [To ORLANDO] I will satisfy you if ever I satisfied man, and you shall be married to-morrow. [To Silvius] I will content you if what pleases you contents you, and you shall be married to-morrow. [To ORLANDO] As you love Rosalind, meet. [To SILVIUS] As you love Phebe, meet;- and as I love no woman, I'll meet. So, fare you well; I have left you commands.
lines: Pray you, no more of this; 'tis like the howling of Irish
wolves against the moon. [To SILVIUS] I will help you if I can.
[To PHEBE] I would love you if I could.- To-morrow meet me all
together. [ To PHEBE ] I will marry you if ever I marry woman,
and I'll be married to-morrow. [To ORLANDO] I will satisfy you if
ever I satisfied man, and you shall be married to-morrow. [To
Silvius] I will content you if what pleases you contents you, and
you shall be married to-morrow. [To ORLANDO] As you love
Rosalind, meet. [To SILVIUS] As you love Phebe, meet;- and as I
love no woman, I'll meet. So, fare you well; I have left you
commands.

speeches.speech: I'll not fail, if I live.
lines: I'll not fail, if I live.

speeches.speech: Nor I.
lines: Nor I.

speeches.speech: Nor I. Exeunt
lines: Nor I. Exeunt

speeches.speech: To-morrow is the joyful day, Audrey; to-morrow will we be married.
lines: To-morrow is the joyful day, Audrey; to-morrow will we
be married.

speeches.speech: I do desire it with all my heart; and I hope it is no dishonest desire to desire to be a woman of the world. Here come two of the banish'd Duke's pages.
lines: I do desire it with all my heart; and I hope it is no
dishonest desire to desire to be a woman of the world. Here come
two of the banish'd Duke's pages.

speeches.speech: Well met, honest gentleman.
lines: Well met, honest gentleman.

speeches.speech: By my troth, well met. Come sit, sit, and a song.
lines: By my troth, well met. Come sit, sit, and a song.

speeches.speech: We are for you; sit i' th' middle.
lines: We are for you; sit i' th' middle.

speeches.speech: Shall we clap into't roundly, without hawking, or spitting, or saying we are hoarse, which are the only prologues to a bad voice?
lines: Shall we clap into't roundly, without hawking, or
spitting, or saying we are hoarse, which are the only prologues
to a bad voice?

speeches.speech: I'faith, i'faith; and both in a tune, like two gipsies on a horse. SONG. It was a lover and his lass, With a hey, and a ho, and a hey nonino, That o'er the green corn-field did pass In the spring time, the only pretty ring time, When birds do sing, hey ding a ding, ding. Sweet lovers love the spring. Between the acres of the rye, With a hey, and a ho, and a hey nonino, These pretty country folks would lie, In the spring time, &c. This carol they began that hour, With a hey, and a ho, and a hey nonino, How that a life was but a flower, In the spring time, &c. And therefore take the present time, With a hey, and a ho, and a hey nonino, For love is crowned with the prime, In the spring time, &c.
lines: I'faith, i'faith; and both in a tune, like two gipsies
on a horse.
SONG.
It was a lover and his lass,
With a hey, and a ho, and a hey nonino,
That o'er the green corn-field did pass
In the spring time, the only pretty ring time,
When birds do sing, hey ding a ding, ding.
Sweet lovers love the spring.
Between the acres of the rye,
With a hey, and a ho, and a hey nonino,
These pretty country folks would lie,
In the spring time, &c.
This carol they began that hour,
With a hey, and a ho, and a hey nonino,
How that a life was but a flower,
In the spring time, &c.
And therefore take the present time,
With a hey, and a ho, and a hey nonino,
For love is crowned with the prime,
In the spring time, &c.

speeches.speech: Truly, young gentlemen, though there was no great matter in the ditty, yet the note was very untuneable.
lines: Truly, young gentlemen, though there was no great
matter in the ditty, yet the note was very untuneable.

speeches.speech: You are deceiv'd, sir; we kept time, we lost not our time.
lines: You are deceiv'd, sir; we kept time, we lost not our
time.

speeches.speech: By my troth, yes; I count it but time lost to hear such a foolish song. God buy you; and God mend your voices. Come, Audrey. Exeunt
lines: By my troth, yes; I count it but time lost to hear such
a foolish song. God buy you; and God mend your voices. Come,
Audrey. Exeunt

speeches.speech: Dost thou believe, Orlando, that the boy Can do all this that he hath promised?
lines: Dost thou believe, Orlando, that the boy
Can do all this that he hath promised?

speeches.speech: I sometimes do believe and sometimes do not: As those that fear they hope, and know they fear.
lines: I sometimes do believe and sometimes do not:
As those that fear they hope, and know they fear.

speeches.speech: Patience once more, whiles our compact is urg'd: You say, if I bring in your Rosalind, You will bestow her on Orlando here?
lines: Patience once more, whiles our compact is urg'd:
You say, if I bring in your Rosalind,
You will bestow her on Orlando here?

speeches.speech: That would I, had I kingdoms to give with her.
lines: That would I, had I kingdoms to give with her.

speeches.speech: And you say you will have her when I bring her?
lines: And you say you will have her when I bring her?

speeches.speech: That would I, were I of all kingdoms king.
lines: That would I, were I of all kingdoms king.

speeches.speech: You say you'll marry me, if I be willing?
lines: You say you'll marry me, if I be willing?

speeches.speech: That will I, should I die the hour after.
lines: That will I, should I die the hour after.

speeches.speech: But if you do refuse to marry me, You'll give yourself to this most faithful shepherd?
lines: But if you do refuse to marry me,
You'll give yourself to this most faithful shepherd?

speeches.speech: So is the bargain.
lines: So is the bargain.

speeches.speech: You say that you'll have Phebe, if she will?
lines: You say that you'll have Phebe, if she will?

speeches.speech: Though to have her and death were both one thing.
lines: Though to have her and death were both one thing.

speeches.speech: I have promis'd to make all this matter even. Keep you your word, O Duke, to give your daughter; You yours, Orlando, to receive his daughter; Keep your word, Phebe, that you'll marry me, Or else, refusing me, to wed this shepherd; Keep your word, Silvius, that you'll marry her If she refuse me; and from hence I go, To make these doubts all even.
lines: I have promis'd to make all this matter even.
Keep you your word, O Duke, to give your daughter;
You yours, Orlando, to receive his daughter;
Keep your word, Phebe, that you'll marry me,
Or else, refusing me, to wed this shepherd;
Keep your word, Silvius, that you'll marry her
If she refuse me; and from hence I go,
To make these doubts all even.

speeches.speech: I do remember in this shepherd boy Some lively touches of my daughter's favour.
lines: I do remember in this shepherd boy
Some lively touches of my daughter's favour.

speeches.speech: My lord, the first time that I ever saw him Methought he was a brother to your daughter. But, my good lord, this boy is forest-born, And hath been tutor'd in the rudiments Of many desperate studies by his uncle, Whom he reports to be a great magician, Obscured in the circle of this forest.
lines: My lord, the first time that I ever saw him
Methought he was a brother to your daughter.
But, my good lord, this boy is forest-born,
And hath been tutor'd in the rudiments
Of many desperate studies by his uncle,
Whom he reports to be a great magician,
Obscured in the circle of this forest.

speeches.speech: There is, sure, another flood toward, and these couples are coming to the ark. Here comes a pair of very strange beasts which in all tongues are call'd fools.
lines: There is, sure, another flood toward, and these couples are
coming to the ark. Here comes a pair of very strange beasts which
in all tongues are call'd fools.

speeches.speech: Salutation and greeting to you all!
lines: Salutation and greeting to you all!

speeches.speech: Good my lord, bid him welcome. This is the motley-minded gentleman that I have so often met in the forest. He hath been a courtier, he swears.
lines: Good my lord, bid him welcome. This is the motley-minded
gentleman that I have so often met in the forest. He hath been a
courtier, he swears.

speeches.speech: If any man doubt that, let him put me to my purgation. I have trod a measure; I have flatt'red a lady; I have been politic with my friend, smooth with mine enemy; I have undone three tailors; I have had four quarrels, and like to have fought one.
lines: If any man doubt that, let him put me to my purgation.
I have trod a measure; I have flatt'red a lady; I have been
politic with my friend, smooth with mine enemy; I have undone
three tailors; I have had four quarrels, and like to have fought
one.

speeches.speech: And how was that ta'en up?
lines: And how was that ta'en up?

speeches.speech: Faith, we met, and found the quarrel was upon the seventh cause.
lines: Faith, we met, and found the quarrel was upon the
seventh cause.

speeches.speech: How seventh cause? Good my lord, like this fellow.
lines: How seventh cause? Good my lord, like this fellow.

speeches.speech: I like him very well.
lines: I like him very well.

speeches.speech: God 'ild you, sir; I desire you of the like. I press in here, sir, amongst the rest of the country copulatives, to swear and to forswear, according as marriage binds and blood breaks. A poor virgin, sir, an ill-favour'd thing, sir, but mine own; a poor humour of mine, sir, to take that that man else will. Rich honesty dwells like a miser, sir, in a poor house; as your pearl in your foul oyster.
lines: God 'ild you, sir; I desire you of the like. I press in
here, sir, amongst the rest of the country copulatives, to swear
and to forswear, according as marriage binds and blood breaks. A
poor virgin, sir, an ill-favour'd thing, sir, but mine own; a
poor humour of mine, sir, to take that that man else will. Rich
honesty dwells like a miser, sir, in a poor house; as your pearl
in your foul oyster.

speeches.speech: By my faith, he is very swift and sententious.
lines: By my faith, he is very swift and sententious.

speeches.speech: According to the fool's bolt, sir, and such dulcet diseases.
lines: According to the fool's bolt, sir, and such dulcet
diseases.

speeches.speech: But, for the seventh cause: how did you find the quarrel on the seventh cause?
lines: But, for the seventh cause: how did you find the quarrel on
the seventh cause?

speeches.speech: Upon a lie seven times removed- bear your body more seeming, Audrey- as thus, sir. I did dislike the cut of a certain courtier's beard; he sent me word, if I said his beard was not cut well, he was in the mind it was. This is call'd the Retort Courteous. If I sent him word again it was not well cut, he would send me word he cut it to please himself. This is call'd the Quip Modest. If again it was not well cut, he disabled my judgment. This is call'd the Reply Churlish. If again it was not well cut, he would answer I spake not true. This is call'd the Reproof Valiant. If again it was not well cut, he would say I lie. This is call'd the Countercheck Quarrelsome. And so to the Lie Circumstantial and the Lie Direct.
lines: Upon a lie seven times removed- bear your body more
seeming, Audrey- as thus, sir. I did dislike the cut of a certain
courtier's beard; he sent me word, if I said his beard was not
cut well, he was in the mind it was. This is call'd the Retort
Courteous. If I sent him word again it was not well cut, he would
send me word he cut it to please himself. This is call'd the Quip
Modest. If again it was not well cut, he disabled my judgment.
This is call'd the Reply Churlish. If again it was not well cut,
he would answer I spake not true. This is call'd the Reproof
Valiant. If again it was not well cut, he would say I lie. This
is call'd the Countercheck Quarrelsome. And so to the Lie
Circumstantial and the Lie Direct.

speeches.speech: And how oft did you say his beard was not well cut?
lines: And how oft did you say his beard was not well cut?

speeches.speech: I durst go no further than the Lie Circumstantial, nor he durst not give me the Lie Direct; and so we measur'd swords and parted.
lines: I durst go no further than the Lie Circumstantial, nor
he durst not give me the Lie Direct; and so we measur'd swords
and parted.

speeches.speech: Can you nominate in order now the degrees of the lie?
lines: Can you nominate in order now the degrees of the lie?

speeches.speech: O, sir, we quarrel in print by the book, as you have books for good manners. I will name you the degrees. The first, the Retort Courteous; the second, the Quip Modest; the third, the Reply Churlish; the fourth, the Reproof Valiant; the fifth, the Countercheck Quarrelsome; the sixth, the Lie with Circumstance; the seventh, the Lie Direct. All these you may avoid but the Lie Direct; and you may avoid that too with an If. I knew when seven justices could not take up a quarrel; but when the parties were met themselves, one of them thought but of an If, as: 'If you said so, then I said so.' And they shook hands, and swore brothers. Your If is the only peace-maker; much virtue in If.
lines: O, sir, we quarrel in print by the book, as you have
books for good manners. I will name you the degrees. The first,
the Retort Courteous; the second, the Quip Modest; the third, the
Reply Churlish; the fourth, the Reproof Valiant; the fifth, the
Countercheck Quarrelsome; the sixth, the Lie with Circumstance;
the seventh, the Lie Direct. All these you may avoid but the Lie
Direct; and you may avoid that too with an If. I knew when seven
justices could not take up a quarrel; but when the parties were
met themselves, one of them thought but of an If, as: 'If you
said so, then I said so.' And they shook hands, and swore
brothers. Your If is the only peace-maker; much virtue in If.

speeches.speech: Is not this a rare fellow, my lord? He's as good at any thing, and yet a fool.
lines: Is not this a rare fellow, my lord?
He's as good at any thing, and yet a fool.

speeches.speech: He uses his folly like a stalking-horse, and under the presentation of that he shoots his wit. [Enter HYMEN, ROSALIND, and CELIA. Still MUSIC] HYMEN. Then is there mirth in heaven, When earthly things made even Atone together. Good Duke, receive thy daughter; Hymen from heaven brought her, Yea, brought her hither, That thou mightst join her hand with his, Whose heart within his bosom is.
lines: He uses his folly like a stalking-horse, and under the
presentation of that he shoots his wit.
[Enter HYMEN, ROSALIND, and CELIA. Still MUSIC]
HYMEN. Then is there mirth in heaven,
When earthly things made even
Atone together.
Good Duke, receive thy daughter;
Hymen from heaven brought her,
Yea, brought her hither,
That thou mightst join her hand with his,
Whose heart within his bosom is.

speeches.speech: [To DUKE] To you I give myself, for I am yours. [To ORLANDO] To you I give myself, for I am yours.
lines: [To DUKE] To you I give myself, for I am yours.
[To ORLANDO] To you I give myself, for I am yours.

speeches.speech: If there be truth in sight, you are my daughter.
lines: If there be truth in sight, you are my daughter.

speeches.speech: If there be truth in sight, you are my Rosalind.
lines: If there be truth in sight, you are my Rosalind.

speeches.speech: If sight and shape be true, Why then, my love adieu!
lines: If sight and shape be true,
Why then, my love adieu!

speeches.speech: I'll have no father, if you be not he; I'll have no husband, if you be not he; Nor ne'er wed woman, if you be not she.
lines: I'll have no father, if you be not he;
I'll have no husband, if you be not he;
Nor ne'er wed woman, if you be not she.

speeches.speech: Peace, ho! I bar confusion; 'Tis I must make conclusion Of these most strange events. Here's eight that must take hands To join in Hymen's bands, If truth holds true contents. You and you no cross shall part; You and you are heart in heart; You to his love must accord, Or have a woman to your lord; You and you are sure together, As the winter to foul weather. Whiles a wedlock-hymn we sing, Feed yourselves with questioning, That reason wonder may diminish, How thus we met, and these things finish. SONG Wedding is great Juno's crown; O blessed bond of board and bed! 'Tis Hymen peoples every town; High wedlock then be honoured. Honour, high honour, and renown, To Hymen, god of every town!
lines: Peace, ho! I bar confusion;
'Tis I must make conclusion
Of these most strange events.
Here's eight that must take hands
To join in Hymen's bands,
If truth holds true contents.
You and you no cross shall part;
You and you are heart in heart;
You to his love must accord,
Or have a woman to your lord;
You and you are sure together,
As the winter to foul weather.
Whiles a wedlock-hymn we sing,
Feed yourselves with questioning,
That reason wonder may diminish,
How thus we met, and these things finish.
SONG
Wedding is great Juno's crown;
O blessed bond of board and bed!
'Tis Hymen peoples every town;
High wedlock then be honoured.
Honour, high honour, and renown,
To Hymen, god of every town!

speeches.speech: O my dear niece, welcome thou art to me! Even daughter, welcome in no less degree.
lines: O my dear niece, welcome thou art to me!
Even daughter, welcome in no less degree.

speeches.speech: I will not eat my word, now thou art mine; Thy faith my fancy to thee doth combine. Enter JAQUES DE BOYS
lines: I will not eat my word, now thou art mine;
Thy faith my fancy to thee doth combine.
Enter JAQUES DE BOYS

speeches.speech: Let me have audience for a word or two. I am the second son of old Sir Rowland, That bring these tidings to this fair assembly. Duke Frederick, hearing how that every day Men of great worth resorted to this forest, Address'd a mighty power; which were on foot, In his own conduct, purposely to take His brother here, and put him to the sword; And to the skirts of this wild wood he came, Where, meeting with an old religious man, After some question with him, was converted Both from his enterprise and from the world; His crown bequeathing to his banish'd brother, And all their lands restor'd to them again That were with him exil'd. This to be true I do engage my life.
lines: Let me have audience for a word or two.
I am the second son of old Sir Rowland,
That bring these tidings to this fair assembly.
Duke Frederick, hearing how that every day
Men of great worth resorted to this forest,
Address'd a mighty power; which were on foot,
In his own conduct, purposely to take
His brother here, and put him to the sword;
And to the skirts of this wild wood he came,
Where, meeting with an old religious man,
After some question with him, was converted
Both from his enterprise and from the world;
His crown bequeathing to his banish'd brother,
And all their lands restor'd to them again
That were with him exil'd. This to be true
I do engage my life.

speeches.speech: Welcome, young man. Thou offer'st fairly to thy brothers' wedding: To one, his lands withheld; and to the other, A land itself at large, a potent dukedom. First, in this forest let us do those ends That here were well begun and well begot; And after, every of this happy number, That have endur'd shrewd days and nights with us, Shall share the good of our returned fortune, According to the measure of their states. Meantime, forget this new-fall'n dignity, And fall into our rustic revelry. Play, music; and you brides and bridegrooms all, With measure heap'd in joy, to th' measures fall.
lines: Welcome, young man.
Thou offer'st fairly to thy brothers' wedding:
To one, his lands withheld; and to the other,
A land itself at large, a potent dukedom.
First, in this forest let us do those ends
That here were well begun and well begot;
And after, every of this happy number,
That have endur'd shrewd days and nights with us,
Shall share the good of our returned fortune,
According to the measure of their states.
Meantime, forget this new-fall'n dignity,
And fall into our rustic revelry.
Play, music; and you brides and bridegrooms all,
With measure heap'd in joy, to th' measures fall.

speeches.speech: Sir, by your patience. If I heard you rightly, The Duke hath put on a religious life, And thrown into neglect the pompous court.
lines: Sir, by your patience. If I heard you rightly,
The Duke hath put on a religious life,
And thrown into neglect the pompous court.

speeches.speech: He hath.
lines: He hath.

speeches.speech: To him will I. Out of these convertites There is much matter to be heard and learn'd. [To DUKE] You to your former honour I bequeath; Your patience and your virtue well deserves it. [To ORLANDO] You to a love that your true faith doth merit; [To OLIVER] You to your land, and love, and great allies [To SILVIUS] You to a long and well-deserved bed; [To TOUCHSTONE] And you to wrangling; for thy loving voyage Is but for two months victuall'd.- So to your pleasures; I am for other than for dancing measures.
lines: To him will I. Out of these convertites
There is much matter to be heard and learn'd.
[To DUKE] You to your former honour I bequeath;
Your patience and your virtue well deserves it.
[To ORLANDO] You to a love that your true faith doth merit;
[To OLIVER] You to your land, and love, and great allies
[To SILVIUS] You to a long and well-deserved bed;
[To TOUCHSTONE] And you to wrangling; for thy loving voyage
Is but for two months victuall'd.- So to your pleasures;
I am for other than for dancing measures.

speeches.speech: Stay, Jaques, stay.
lines: Stay, Jaques, stay.

speeches.speech: To see no pastime I. What you would have I'll stay to know at your abandon'd cave. Exit
lines: To see no pastime I. What you would have
I'll stay to know at your abandon'd cave. Exit

speeches.speech: Proceed, proceed. We will begin these rites, As we do trust they'll end, in true delights. [A dance] Exeunt EPILOGUE
lines: Proceed, proceed. We will begin these rites,
As we do trust they'll end, in true delights. [A dance] Exeunt EPILOGUE

speeches.speech: It is not the fashion to see the lady the epilogue; but it is no more unhandsome than to see the lord the prologue. If it be true that good wine needs no bush, 'tis true that a good play needs no epilogue. Yet to good wine they do use good bushes; and good plays prove the better by the help of good epilogues. What a case am I in then, that am neither a good epilogue, nor cannot insinuate with you in the behalf of a good play! I am not furnish'd like a beggar; therefore to beg will not become me. My way is to conjure you; and I'll begin with the women. I charge you, O women, for the love you bear to men, to like as much of this play as please you; and I charge you, O men, for the love you bear to women- as I perceive by your simp'ring none of you hates them- that between you and the women the play may please. If I were a woman, I would kiss as many of you as had beards that pleas'd me, complexions that lik'd me, and breaths that I defied not; and, I am sure, as many as have good beards, or good faces, or sweet breaths, will, for my kind offer, when I make curtsy, bid me farewell.
